**Step 4 — Express as a percentage of wall-clock time.**

$$
\text{CPU Efficiency} = \frac{T_{eff}}{T_{total}} \times 100\% = \frac{99.99}{100} \times 100\% = 99.99\%
$$

> **Result:** $\boxed{T_{eff} = 99.99 \text{ s}}$ — a 0.01% loss to context switching.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — State Transition Validity

The following matrix enumerates every *theoretically conceivable* state transition and validates it against the standard five-state model:

| From \ To | New | Ready | Running | Blocked | Terminated |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **New** | ✗ | ✓ (admit) | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ |
| **Ready** | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ (dispatch) | ✗ | ✗ |
| **Running** | ✗ | ✓ (preempt/quantum-expire) | ✗ | ✓ (wait on I/O/event) | ✓ (exit/kill) |
| **Blocked** | ✗ | ✓ (event occurred) | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ |
| **Terminated** | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Heuristic:** Marks are deducted if a student writes "Blocked → Running" directly. A blocked process **must** first re-enter the Ready queue — only then can the short-term scheduler dispatch it to Running.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1 — Architectural Placement of the Kernel

This block-level diagram illustrates how the kernel sits between user applications and hardware, mediating every privileged operation.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph USR[USER SPACE]
        UA1[User Application A]
        UA2[User Application B]
        UA3[RTOS Task - Periodic]
        UA4[RTOS Task - Aperiodic]
    end

    subgraph KRN[KERNEL SPACE PRIVILEGED MODE]
        SCH[Scheduler]
        IPC[IPC Manager - Semaphores - Mutexes - Mailboxes]
        MEM[Memory Manager]
        IO[I/O and Interrupt Manager]
        PCB[Process Control Block Table]
    end

    subgraph HW[HARDWARE LAYER]
        CPU[CPU Core]
        RAM[Main Memory]
        DVC[I/O Devices - Sensors - Actuators]
        TM[Timer and Interrupt Controller]
    end

    UA1 -->|syscall| KRN
    UA2 -->|syscall| KRN
    UA3 -->|syscall| KRN
    UA4 -->|syscall| KRN

    SCH --> PCB
    IPC --> PCB
    MEM --> PCB
    IO --> PCB

    SCH --> CPU
    MEM --> RAM
    IO --> DVC
    IO --> TM
```

---

### 4.2 Diagram 2 — Five-State Process Transition Diagram

The canonical state machine showing all valid transitions of a process during its lifecycle.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    direction LR
    [*] --> NEW : fork or spawn

    NEW --> READY : admission by long-term scheduler
    READY --> RUNNING : dispatched by short-term scheduler

    RUNNING --> READY : preempted or quantum expired
    RUNNING --> BLOCKED : I/O request or wait on event
    RUNNING --> TERMINATED : process exit or killed

    BLOCKED --> READY : I/O complete or event signaled

    TERMINATED --> [*] : PCB reclaimed

    note right of NEW : PCB is being allocated
    note right of READY : Waiting in ready queue
    note right of RUNNING : Instructions executing on CPU
    note right of BLOCKED : Waiting for I/O or synchronization
    note right of TERMINATED : Resources being reclaimed
```

---

### 4.3 Diagram 3 — Kernel Boot and Process Lifecycle Sequence

This sequence diagram traces the journey from power-on to a running process.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant BOOT as Bootloader
    participant KRN as Kernel
    participant SCH as Scheduler
    participant PCB as PCB Table
    participant APP as User Process

    BOOT->>KRN: Load kernel image into RAM
    KRN->>KRN: Initialize hardware and drivers
    KRN->>PCB: Create PID 0 - idle task
    KRN->>SCH: Start scheduler loop
    SCH->>KRN: Select highest priority ready task

    APP->>KRN: syscall fork
    KRN->>PCB: Allocate new PCB
    KRN->>KRN: Copy parent address space
    KRN-->>APP: Return child PID - state READY

    SCH->>APP: Dispatch - state RUNNING
    APP->>APP: Execute instructions

    APP->>KRN: syscall read - I/O request
    KRN->>APP: Transition to BLOCKED

    Note over KRN,APP: I/O device completes transfer
    KRN->>APP: ISR fires - transition to READY

    SCH->>APP: Dispatch again - state RUNNING
    APP->>KRN: syscall exit
    KRN->>PCB: Move PCB to terminated list
```

---

### 4.4 Diagram 4 — Process Control Block Internal Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PCB[Process Control Block - PCB]

    subgraph PID_BLOCK[Process Identification]
        PID[Process ID - PID]
        PPID[Parent PID]
        UID[User ID]
    end

    subgraph CPU_STATE[Processor State]
        PC[Program Counter]
        SP[Stack Pointer]
        REG[General Registers]
        SR[Status Register]
    end

    subgraph SCHED_INFO[Scheduling Information]
        PRIO[Priority Value]
        QPTR[Queue Pointers]
        CPUT[CPU Time Used]
    end

    subgraph MEM_INFO[Memory Management]
        BASE[Base Register]
        LIMIT[Limit Register]
        PTBL[Page Table Pointer]
    end

    subgraph IO_INFO[I/O and IPC Status]
        OFL[Open File List]
        SIGP[Pending Signals]
        MSGP[Message Queue Pointers]
    end

    PID_BLOCK --> PCB
    CPU_STATE --> PCB
    SCHED_INFO --> PCB
    MEM_INFO --> PCB
    IO_INFO --> PCB
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define a kernel. Explain any two key responsibilities of the kernel in a Real-Time Operating System.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A *kernel* is the central, privileged, and permanently memory-resident component of an operating system that manages all hardware resources and provides essential services to user processes. It executes in supervisor/privileged mode (Ring 0) and is the only software component with unrestricted access to CPU, memory, and I/O devices.

*Two key responsibilities in an RTOS:*

1. **Task Scheduling with Determinism:** The kernel maintains the ready queue and selects the highest-priority runnable task for execution. In an RTOS, this dispatch must occur within a bounded worst-case latency to meet hard deadlines.

2. **Interrupt Handling:** The kernel manages the interrupt vector table and dispatches ISRs. Interrupt latency — the time from interrupt assertion to first ISR instruction — must be tightly bounded, typically within a few microseconds.

*(1 mark for definition + 1 mark each for two responsibilities)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**List the five states of a process. Explain why a Blocked process cannot directly transition to the Running state.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The five states of a process are: **New, Ready, Running, Blocked (Waiting), and Terminated.**

A Blocked process cannot directly transition to Running because:

- A process enters the Blocked state because it is *waiting for an external event* — typically the completion of an I/O operation or the signalling of a synchronization primitive (semaphore, mutex, message queue).
- Until this event occurs, the process is *incapable of making progress*, even if the CPU were granted to it. Granting the CPU to such a process would waste CPU cycles.
- Once the awaited event occurs, the kernel's interrupt service routine moves the process from Blocked → Ready. Only then can the short-term scheduler legally dispatch it to the Running state.

Hence the valid path is **Blocked → Ready → Running**, never Blocked → Running directly.

*(1 mark for listing states + 2 marks for the explanation)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

**Q3(A). [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the five-state process model. List all valid state transitions and the events that trigger them. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Define Process Control Block (PCB). Explain the significance of PCB in context switching. Compute the average waiting time and average turnaround time for the following four processes using FCFS scheduling. **(7 Marks)**

| Process | Arrival Time (ms) | Burst Time (ms) |
|---|---|---|
| P1 | 0 | 5 |
| P2 | 1 | 3 |
| P3 | 2 | 8 |
| P4 | 3 | 6 |

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Five-State Process Model (7 Marks)**

Refer to **Diagram 2 in Section 4** for the state diagram. Provide the following explanation:

- **New State:** Process is being created. The OS allocates a unique PID, creates the PCB, and loads the program into memory.
- **Ready State:** Process is loaded into memory and queued awaiting CPU. All resources except the CPU are available.
- **Running State:** Instructions are executing on the CPU. Only one process per core may be in this state.
- **Blocked (Waiting) State:** Process cannot execute because it awaits an external event — I/O completion, semaphore release, or inter-process message.
- **Terminated State:** Process has finished. The OS reclaims resources but may retain the PCB for the parent process's `wait()` call.

**Valid Transitions and Triggers:**

| # | Transition | Trigger |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | New → Ready | Long-term scheduler admits the process |
| 2 | Ready → Running | Short-term scheduler dispatches |
| 3 | Running → Ready | Preemption by higher-priority task or quantum expiry |
| 4 | Running → Blocked | I/O request or wait on synchronization primitive |
| 5 | Blocked → Ready | I/O completion or waited-upon event occurs |
| 6 | Running → Terminated | Normal exit, fatal error, or `kill()` signal |

**(b) PCB and FCFS Computation (7 Marks)**

**Definition:** A Process Control Block (PCB) is a kernel data structure that stores all information about a process, including its PID, program counter, register state, memory maps, open files, and scheduling parameters. *(2 marks)*

**Significance in Context Switching:** During a context switch, the kernel saves the entire state of the currently running process into its PCB (registers, PC, stack pointer) and loads the state of the newly-selected process from its PCB. Without the PCB, the OS could not suspend and resume processes — every process would need to run to completion. *(2 marks)*

**FCFS Computation (Processes run in order P1, P2, P3, P4):**

**Step 1 — Completion times:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_1 &= 0 + 5 = 5 \text{ ms} \\
C_2 &= 5 + 3 = 8 \text{ ms} \\
C_3 &= 8 + 8 = 16 \text{ ms} \\
C_4 &= 16 + 6 = 22 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Turnaround times $T_{turn,i} = C_i - A_i$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{turn,1} &= 5 - 0 = 5 \text{ ms} \\
T_{turn,2} &= 8 - 1 = 7 \text{ ms} \\
T_{turn,3} &= 16 - 2 = 14 \text{ ms} \\
T_{turn,4} &= 22 - 3 = 19 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Waiting times $T_{wait,i} = T_{turn,i} - B_i$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{wait,1} &= 5 - 5 = 0 \text{ ms} \\
T_{wait,2} &= 7 - 3 = 4 \text{ ms} \\
T_{wait,3} &= 14 - 8 = 6 \text{ ms} \\
T_{wait,4} &= 19 - 6 = 13 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Averages:**

$$
\bar{T}_{turn} = \frac{5 + 7 + 14 + 19}{4} = \frac{45}{4} = 11.25 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
\bar{T}_{wait} = \frac{0 + 4 + 6 + 13}{4} = \frac{23}{4} = 5.75 \text{ ms}
$$

**Final Results:**
$$
\boxed{\bar{T}_{turn} = 11.25 \text{ ms}, \quad \bar{T}_{wait} = 5.75 \text{ ms}}
$$

---

**Q3(B). [Alternative Choice — Same Module, Different Angle]**

**(a)** Differentiate between a *program* and a *process*. With a neat diagram, explain the layered architecture of an operating system and identify where the kernel resides. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A system observes 50 process completions over a 10-second window. The CPU was busy for 8.2 seconds. Compute the throughput, CPU utilization, and if 200 context switches occurred with each taking 25 µs, compute the effective CPU time. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Program vs Process and OS Architecture (7 Marks)**

| Aspect | Program | Process |
|---|---|---|
| Nature | Passive entity (file on disk) | Active entity (program in execution) |
| Lifetime | Static — exists until deleted | Dynamic — created, runs, terminates |
| Resources | None associated | Has its own memory, registers, I/O |
| Example | `chrome.exe` on hard disk | Running Chrome window with PID 4192 |

**Layered Architecture of an OS** (top-down):

1. **User Applications Layer** — Browsers, editors, RTOS tasks (unprivileged)
2. **System Programs / Utilities Layer** — Compilers, shells (mostly unprivileged)
3. **Operating System Layer** — Subdivided into:
   - Non-kernel services (file system utilities, command interpreters)
   - **Kernel (privileged core)** — Scheduler, IPC, memory manager, interrupt handler
4. **Hardware Layer** — CPU, RAM, disks, peripherals

The **kernel** resides at the boundary between the OS layer and the hardware, executing in privileged (kernel/supervisor) mode. *(4 marks for table + diagram + 3 marks for explanation)*

**(b) Performance Metric Computation (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Throughput $\Thetavec$:**

$$
\Thetavec = \frac{n_{completed}}{\Delta t} = \frac{50 \text{ processes}}{10 \text{ s}} = 5 \text{ processes/second}
$$

**Step 2 — CPU Utilization $U$:**

$$
U = \frac{t_{busy}}{t_{busy} + t_{idle}} = \frac{8.2}{8.2 + 1.8} = \frac{8.2}{10.0} = 0.82 = 82\%
$$

**Step 3 — Total context-switch overhead:**

$$
T_{cs,total} = 200 \times 25 \text{ µs} = 5000 \text{ µs} = 0.005 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 4 — Effective CPU time:**

$$
T_{eff} = T_{busy} - T_{cs,total} = 8.2 - 0.005 = 8.195 \text{ s}
$$

**Final Results:**

$$
\boxed{\Thetavec = 5 \text{ processes/s}, \quad U = 82\%, \quad T_{eff} = 8.195 \text{ s}}
$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Blocked → Running" as valid** — This is the *single most common* error. A blocked process MUST pass through Ready first. *(Lose 1–2 marks per occurrence)*
>
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract Burst Time from Turnaround Time** when computing Waiting Time — many students write $T_{wait} = C_i - A_i$ instead of $T_{wait} = T_{turn} - B_i$. *(Lose 1 mark per process)*
>
> 3. **Using "kernel" and "operating system" interchangeably** — they are NOT the same. The OS includes user-space utilities (shells, GUI); the kernel is only the privileged core. *(Lose 1 mark on definition questions)*
>
> 4. **Omitting the PCB in the context-switching answer** — many students describe saving "registers" only. The full PCB (including scheduling info, memory maps, accounting) must be saved. *(Lose 2 marks)*
>
> 5. **Writing FCFS order as P1, P3, P2, P4 (by burst time)** — FCFS is strictly by *arrival order*, not by burst length. *(Lose full marks on the numerical)*

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before Exam:**

- ☐ **Kernel** = privileged, memory-resident core of the OS; executes in Ring 0 / supervisor mode; never swapped out.
- ☐ **Five Process States:** New → Ready → Running → Blocked → Terminated (and back to Ready from Blocked).
- ☐ **Six valid transitions** — memorize the trigger for each.
- ☐ **Blocked → Running is INVALID.** Always Blocked → Ready → Running.
- ☐ **Program ≠ Process** — Program is passive code on disk; Process is active execution with allocated resources.
- ☐ **PCB** stores *everything* about a process: PID, PC, registers, memory maps, file descriptors, scheduling info.
- ☐ **Context switch** = save old PCB + load new PCB; critical for multitasking.
- ☐ **Formulae:** $T_{turn} = C_i - A_i$; $T_{wait} = T_{turn} - B_i$; $\bar{T}_{wait} = \frac{1}{n}\sum T_{wait,i}$.
- ☐ **Throughput** = completed processes per unit time; **CPU Utilization** = busy time / total time.
- ☐ **RTOS-specific:** Kernel must guarantee *bounded latency*, *determinism*, and *preemptibility*.
- ☐ In FCFS, processes run in *arrival order*, not shortest-job order.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Control Block and Operations on Process, Process Scheduling - Scheduling Algorithms - First Come First-Served (FCFS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT785 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Process Control Block and Operations on Process, Process Scheduling - Scheduling Algorithms - First Come First-Served (FCFS)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Process Control Block (PCB)

A **Process Control Block (PCB)** is a kernel data structure in main memory that acts as the **identity card and personal record book** of a process. The operating system maintains one PCB for every active process. It contains all the information required to manage, schedule, and execute a process — including its current state, registers, memory maps, and accounting data.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition (Exam Gold)**: A PCB is a data structure maintained by the operating system for every process. It contains information about the process such as its current state, program counter, CPU registers, memory management information, accounting information, and I/O status information.

### 1.1.1 Real-World Analogy — The "Patient Case File" 🏥

Imagine a hospital with hundreds of patients. The hospital cannot afford to assign a dedicated doctor to every patient. Instead, the hospital keeps a **case file (PCB)** for each patient. This file contains:
- The patient's current condition (Running / Waiting / Ready) → **Process State**
- The last treatment the doctor left off at → **Program Counter**
- The patient's vitals like BP, pulse → **CPU Registers**
- The ward and bed number assigned → **Memory Limits**
- Bills, tests pending → **I/O Status / Accounting**

When the doctor (CPU) is suddenly called to another patient (context switch), they read the case file and resume later exactly where they left off. **The PCB serves the same purpose for the OS.**

## 1.2 Operations on a Process

The lifecycle of a process involves **five core operations**:

| Operation | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| **Process Creation** | OS creates a new process via `fork()`, `exec()`, etc. |
| **Process Termination** | Process finishes or is killed (`exit()`, `abort()`). |
| **Process Suspension** | Process is swapped out of memory to disk. |
| **Process Resumption** | Suspended process is brought back into memory. |
| **Process State Change** | Ready → Running → Waiting transitions. |

### 1.2.1 Analogy — The "Restaurant Order Ticket" 🍽️

A process is like an order ticket in a busy restaurant. The order is **created** (taken), **scheduled** (sent to chef), **suspended** (waiting for ingredients), **resumed** (cooking starts), and **terminated** (food served, ticket discarded). The PCB is the paper slip that travels with the order so the chef (CPU) knows exactly what to cook, how spicy, and for which table.

## 1.3 Process Scheduling

**Process Scheduling** is the activity of the OS that decides *which ready process gets the CPU next, when, and for how long*. It is the brain of a multiprogramming OS.

There are three types of schedulers:
1. **Long-Term Scheduler (Job Scheduler)** — Decides which jobs enter the ready queue.
2. **Short-Term Scheduler (CPU Scheduler)** — Decides which ready process executes next on the CPU.
3. **Medium-Term Scheduler** — Handles swapping (suspension/resumption).

## 1.4 First-Come, First-Served (FCFS) Scheduling

**FCFS** is the simplest, non-preemptive CPU scheduling algorithm. Processes are dispatched to the CPU in the order they arrive in the **ready queue**. It is implemented using a **FIFO (First-In-First-Out) queue**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight**: FCFS is **non-preemptive** — once a process starts executing on the CPU, it runs to completion without being interrupted by any newly arriving higher-priority process.

### 1.4.1 Analogy — The "Doctor's Clinic Queue" 🧑‍⚕️

When you visit a government hospital, the doctor strictly serves patients in the order they registered. The first person in line gets treated first, regardless of how critical the second person is. If the first patient has a long consultation, the second patient waits patiently. **This is exactly how FCFS works.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Performance Metrics in FCFS (memorize these symbols):**
> - **AT** = Arrival Time
> - **BT** = Burst Time (CPU execution time)
> - **CT** = Completion Time
> - **TAT** = Turnaround Time = **CT − AT**
> - **WT** = Waiting Time = **TAT − BT**
> - **RT** = Response Time = **First CPU Allocation − AT**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Process Control Block (PCB)

The PCB is organized into four logical groups. The OS accesses these fields during **context switches** to save and restore process execution state.

### 2.1.1 PCB Structure — Detailed Breakdown

1. **Process Identification**
   - **PID (Process ID)** — Unique integer identifier.
   - **PPID (Parent PID)** — Creator's identifier.
   - **User ID / Group ID** — Ownership credentials.

2. **Processor State Information (CPU Context)**
   - **Program Counter (PC)** — Address of the *next* instruction to execute.
   - **CPU Registers** — Accumulator, index registers, stack pointer, general-purpose registers.
   - **Status Register / Condition Codes** — Zero, carry, sign, overflow flags.
   - **Stack Pointer** — Top of the current stack frame.

3. **Process Control Information**
   - **Process State** — `New`, `Ready`, `Running`, `Waiting`, `Terminated`.
   - **Priority** — Scheduling importance.
   - **Scheduling Information** — Queue pointers, wait time, pointers to other PCBs.
   - **Signal Handling Info** — Registered signal handlers.
   - **Accounting Information** — CPU time used, time limits, job counts.

4. **Memory Management Information**
   - **Base / Limit Registers** — Memory boundaries.
   - **Page Tables / Segment Tables** — Virtual-to-physical address mappings.
   - **Memory Pointers** — Code, data, stack segment pointers.

## 2.2 The Five-State Process Model

A process transitions through these states:

$$ \text{New} \rightarrow \text{Ready} \rightarrow \text{Running} \rightarrow \text{Terminated} $$

with auxiliary transitions: **Running → Waiting** (I/O request) and **Waiting → Ready** (I/O completion).

## 2.3 Why FCFS Matters in Engineering

- Embedded systems and **Real-Time Operating Systems (RTOS)** often use simple, deterministic schedulers like FCFS as a baseline.
- It is the **reference algorithm** against which all other algorithms (SJF, RR, Priority) are compared in KTU exams.
- **Disadvantage**: The **Convoy Effect** — a single long process delays all short processes behind it, severely hurting system throughput and average waiting time.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table — it appears in 90% of FCFS numericals.**

| Metric | Formula | Unit | Notes |
|--------|---------|------|-------|
| **Turnaround Time (TAT)** | $\text{TAT} = \text{CT} - \text{AT}$ | Time units | Total time spent in system |
| **Waiting Time (WT)** | $\text{WT} = \text{TAT} - \text{BT}$ | Time units | Time spent in ready queue |
| **Response Time (RT)** | $\text{RT} = \text{First CPU Allocation} - \text{AT}$ | Time units | For non-preemptive FCFS, RT = WT |
| **Average Waiting Time** | $\overline{WT} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} WT_i$ | Time units | $n$ = number of processes |
| **Average Turnaround Time** | $\overline{TAT} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} TAT_i$ | Time units | Used in all KTU problems |
| **Throughput** | $\text{Throughput} = \frac{n}{\text{Total Time}}$ | Processes/unit time | Higher is better |
| **CPU Utilization** | $\text{CPU Util} = \frac{\text{Busy Time}}{\text{Total Time}} \times 100$ | Percentage | Should be maximized |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do not confuse CT with TAT.** CT is the *clock time* when the process finishes. TAT is the *duration* the process spent in the system. Many KTU students interchange these and lose **2–3 marks per question**.

## 2.5 Convoy Effect in FCFS

The **Convoy Effect** occurs when a CPU-bound process holds the CPU, and a long queue of I/O-bound processes waits. The I/O devices stay idle while the CPU works alone, and vice versa. This leads to poor resource utilization and high average turnaround time.

**Real-World Example**: A single 10-minute truck blocking a narrow street causes 50 cars to wait 10 minutes each, even though each car only needs 30 seconds to pass.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked-Out FCFS Numerical (KTU Board Pattern)

### Problem Statement

Consider the following set of processes with their **Arrival Time (AT)** and **Burst Time (BT)**. Compute the **Average Waiting Time** and **Average Turnaround Time** using **FCFS** scheduling.

| Process | Arrival Time (AT) | Burst Time (BT) |
|---------|------------------|-----------------|
| P1 | 0 | 4 |
| P2 | 1 | 3 |
| P3 | 2 | 1 |
| P4 | 3 | 2 |
| P5 | 4 | 5 |

### Step 1: Construct the Gantt Chart

In FCFS, the CPU executes processes in order of arrival. If a process hasn't arrived when the CPU becomes free, the CPU remains idle.

**Logic Walkthrough:**
- At t = 0: P1 is the only process available → CPU executes P1 from 0 to 4.
- At t = 4: P1 finishes. P2 (AT=1), P3 (AT=2), P4 (AT=3), P5 (AT=4) have all arrived.
- Execute in arrival order: P2 (4→7), P3 (7→8), P4 (8→10), P5 (10→15).

### Gantt Chart (Visual)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Gantt:} \quad & \boxed{P1} \;\; \boxed{P2} \;\; \boxed{P3} \;\; \boxed{P4} \;\; \boxed{P5} \\
& 0 \quad\;\; 4 \quad\;\; 7 \quad\;\; 8 \quad\;\; 10 \quad\;\; 15
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Compute Completion Time (CT)

CT is read directly from the right end of each box in the Gantt chart.

| Process | Completion Time (CT) |
|---------|----------------------|
| P1 | 4 |
| P2 | 7 |
| P3 | 8 |
| P4 | 10 |
| P5 | 15 |

### Step 3: Compute Turnaround Time (TAT) using $\text{TAT} = \text{CT} - \text{AT}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
TAT_{P1} &= 4 - 0 = 4 \\
TAT_{P2} &= 7 - 1 = 6 \\
TAT_{P3} &= 8 - 2 = 6 \\
TAT_{P4} &= 10 - 3 = 7 \\
TAT_{P5} &= 15 - 4 = 11
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Compute Waiting Time (WT) using $\text{WT} = \text{TAT} - \text{BT}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
WT_{P1} &= 4 - 4 = 0 \\
WT_{P2} &= 6 - 3 = 3 \\
WT_{P3} &= 6 - 1 = 5 \\
WT_{P4} &= 7 - 2 = 5 \\
WT_{P5} &= 11 - 5 = 6
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Compute Averages

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{TAT} &= \frac{4 + 6 + 6 + 7 + 11}{5} = \frac{34}{5} = 6.8 \text{ units} \\[6pt]
\overline{WT} &= \frac{0 + 3 + 5 + 5 + 6}{5} = \frac{19}{5} = 3.8 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6: Analytical Matrix (Final Summary Table)

| Process | AT | BT | CT | TAT | WT |
|---------|----|----|----|-----|----|
| P1 | 0 | 4 | 4 | 4 | 0 |
| P2 | 1 | 3 | 7 | 6 | 3 |
| P3 | 2 | 1 | 8 | 6 | 5 |
| P4 | 3 | 2 | 10 | 7 | 5 |
| P5 | 4 | 5 | 15 | 11 | 6 |
| **Averages** | — | — | — | **6.8** | **3.8** |

## 3.2 FCFS with Idle Time — Analytical Edge Case

### Problem Statement

| Process | AT | BT |
|---------|----|----|
| P1 | 0 | 3 |
| P2 | 2 | 6 |
| P3 | 5 | 2 |

**Walkthrough:**
- At t = 0: P1 starts, runs till t = 3.
- At t = 3: P2 is the only arrived process (P3 arrives at 5). P2 runs from 3 → 9.
- At t = 9: P3 runs from 9 → 11.

$$
\begin{aligned}
CT_{P1} &= 3, \quad CT_{P2} = 9, \quad CT_{P3} = 11 \\
TAT_{P1} &= 3 - 0 = 3 \\
TAT_{P2} &= 9 - 2 = 7 \\
TAT_{P3} &= 11 - 5 = 6 \\
WT_{P1} &= 3 - 3 = 0 \\
WT_{P2} &= 7 - 6 = 1 \\
WT_{P3} &= 6 - 2 = 4
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{TAT} &= \frac{3 + 7 + 6}{3} = 5.33 \text{ units} \\
\overline{WT} &= \frac{0 + 1 + 4}{3} = 1.67 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Control Block (PCB) — Internal Architecture

The following block diagram maps every field of the PCB and its logical grouping.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PCB[Process Control Block]
    PCB --> PID[Process Identification]
    PCB --> CPU[Processor State Info]
    PCB --> CTRL[Process Control Info]
    PCB --> MEM[Memory Management Info]

    PID --> PID1[Process ID - PID]
    PID --> PID2[Parent Process ID - PPID]
    PID --> PID3[User ID - UID]
    PID --> PID4[Group ID - GID]

    CPU --> CPU1[Program Counter - PC]
    CPU --> CPU2[CPU Registers]
    CPU --> CPU3[Status Register - Flags]
    CPU --> CPU4[Stack Pointer - SP]

    CTRL --> CTRL1[Process State - Ready Running Waiting]
    CTRL --> CTRL2[Priority]
    CTRL --> CTRL3[Scheduling Info - Queue Pointers]
    CTRL --> CTRL4[Signal Handlers]
    CTRL --> CTRL5[Accounting Info - CPU Time Used]

    MEM --> MEM1[Base and Limit Registers]
    MEM --> MEM2[Page Table Pointer]
    MEM --> MEM3[Segment Table Pointer]
    MEM --> MEM4[Open File Descriptor Table]
```

## 4.2 Five-State Process Lifecycle

This diagram illustrates the legal state transitions every process undergoes in a multiprogrammed OS.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> New : Process Creation
    New --> Ready : Admitted by Long-Term Scheduler
    Ready --> Running : Short-Term Scheduler Dispatch
    Running --> Waiting : I/O Request or Event Wait
    Waiting --> Ready : I/O Completion or Event Signal
    Running --> Ready : Preemption or Time Slice Expiry
    Running --> Terminated : Process Exit or Abort
    Terminated --> [*]
```

## 4.3 FCFS Scheduling Flow — Block-Level Functional Architecture

This flow shows how the **FCFS ready queue** dispatches processes to the CPU and how the PCB is updated on every context switch.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ARRIVE[New Process Arrival] --> ENQUEUE[Insert at Tail of FIFO Ready Queue]
    ENQUEUE --> Q{Ready Queue Empty?}
    Q -- No --> DISPATCH[Short-Term Scheduler Dispatches Head of Queue]
    Q -- Yes --> IDLE[CPU Enters Idle State]
    IDLE --> ENQUEUE
    DISPATCH --> LOAD[Load PCB of Selected Process into CPU Registers]
    LOAD --> EXEC[Execute Process Non-Preemptively]
    EXEC --> FINISH{Process Completed?}
    FINISH -- Yes --> SAVE[Save Updated PCB to Memory]
    SAVE --> DEQUEUE[Dequeue from Ready Queue]
    DEQUEUE --> Q
    FINISH -- No --> EXEC
```

## 4.4 Convoy Effect Visualization (Gantt Comparison)

```mermaid
gantt
    title Convoy Effect in FCFS (Long Process Blocks Short Ones)
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat %s

    section FCFS
    P1 Long (BT=10)  :a1, 0, 10
    P2 Short (BT=1)  :a2, 10, 1
    P3 Short (BT=1)  :a3, 11, 1
    P4 Short (BT=1)  :a4, 12, 1
```

**Observation**: Three short processes (P2, P3, P4) are forced to wait for the single long process P1, demonstrating the convoy effect.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Q1]**
> **Q1.** What is a Process Control Block? List any four fields stored in it.
>
> **Model Answer:**
> A Process Control Block (PCB) is a data structure maintained by the operating system for each process. It contains all the information needed to manage the process.
> Four fields stored in PCB are:
> 1. **Process ID (PID)** — Unique identifier.
> 2. **Program Counter (PC)** — Address of next instruction.
> 3. **Process State** — Current state (Ready, Running, Waiting, etc.).
> 4. **CPU Registers** — Saved register values for context switch.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Q2]**
> **Q2.** Differentiate between FCFS and preemptive scheduling. State the **Convoy Effect** in FCFS.
>
> **Model Answer:**
> | Aspect | FCFS | Preemptive Scheduling |
> |--------|------|----------------------|
> | Nature | Non-preemptive | Allows preemption |
> | CPU Release | Process runs to completion | Process can be interrupted by higher-priority one |
> | Example | Doctor's queue | Round Robin / Priority Preemptive |
> | Convoy Effect | Suffers heavily | Minimal impact |
>
> **Convoy Effect**: When a long process occupies the CPU, all short processes behind it are delayed, causing poor throughput and high average waiting time.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Q3 — Question A]**
> **Q3 (a)** Explain the structure of a Process Control Block with a neat diagram. List the operations performed on a process. (7 Marks)
>
> **Model Answer:**
> *(See Section 4.1 for the PCB diagram.)*
>
> **Operations on a process:**
> 1. **Process Creation** — Parent calls `fork()`; child is a duplicate. `exec()` replaces the image.
> 2. **Process Termination** — Process calls `exit()`. Parent reaps child via `wait()`.
> 3. **Process Suspension** — Swap out from memory to disk to reduce load.
> 4. **Process Resumption** — Bring swapped process back into main memory.
> 5. **Process State Change** — Transitions among New, Ready, Running, Waiting, Terminated.
>
> Each operation updates the corresponding PCB fields maintained by the OS.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Q3 — Question A Continued]**
> **Q3 (b)** Consider the following processes. Compute **Average Waiting Time** and **Average Turnaround Time** using FCFS scheduling. Draw the Gantt chart. (7 Marks)
>
> | Process | AT | BT |
> |---------|----|----|
> | P1 | 0 | 5 |
> | P2 | 2 | 4 |
> | P3 | 4 | 2 |
> | P4 | 5 | 1 |
>
> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Step 1 — Gantt Chart Construction:**
> - t=0: P1 starts (only process). Runs 0 → 5.
> - t=5: P2, P3, P4 all arrived. Order by AT: P2, P3, P4.
> - P2: 5 → 9, P3: 9 → 11, P4: 11 → 12.
>
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> \text{Gantt:} \quad & P1(0\text{-}5) \to P2(5\text{-}9) \to P3(9\text{-}11) \to P4(11\text{-}12)
> \end{aligned}
> $$
>
> **Step 2 — Completion Times:**
> CT_P1 = 5, CT_P2 = 9, CT_P3 = 11, CT_P4 = 12.
>
> **Step 3 — Turnaround Times:**
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> TAT_{P1} &= 5 - 0 = 5 \\
> TAT_{P2} &= 9 - 2 = 7 \\
> TAT_{P3} &= 11 - 4 = 7 \\
> TAT_{P4} &= 12 - 5 = 7
> \end{aligned}
> $$
>
> **Step 4 — Waiting Times:**
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> WT_{P1} &= 5 - 5 = 0 \\
> WT_{P2} &= 7 - 4 = 3 \\
> WT_{P3} &= 7 - 2 = 5 \\
> WT_{P4} &= 7 - 1 = 6
> \end{aligned}
> $$
>
> **Step 5 — Averages:**
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> \overline{TAT} &= \frac{5 + 7 + 7 + 7}{4} = 6.5 \text{ units} \\
> \overline{WT} &= \frac{0 + 3 + 5 + 6}{4} = 3.5 \text{ units}
> \end{aligned}
> $$

---

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Q3 — Question B (Alternative Choice)]**
> **Q3 (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the five-state process model. How does a context switch use the PCB? (7 Marks)
>
> **Model Answer:**
> *(See Section 4.2 for the five-state diagram.)*
>
> **Context Switch using PCB:**
> 1. The OS saves the **current state of the running process** into its PCB (registers, PC, stack pointer, etc.).
> 2. The next process to run is selected by the short-term scheduler.
> 3. The OS **loads the saved state from the next process's PCB** into the CPU registers.
> 4. The CPU begins execution of the new process from the saved PC address.
> 5. This "save–load" sequence is the **context switch**, and the PCB is the storage medium.
>
> **Overhead**: Context switches consume CPU cycles; minimizing them is a key OS design goal.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Q3 — Question B Continued]**
> **Q3 (b)** What is FCFS scheduling? For the following processes, draw the Gantt chart, and calculate **Average Waiting Time** and **Average Turnaround Time**. Explain the **Convoy Effect**. (7 Marks)
>
> | Process | AT | BT |
> |---------|----|----|
> | P1 | 0 | 7 |
> | P2 | 1 | 4 |
> | P3 | 2 | 1 |
> | P4 | 3 | 4 |
>
> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Gantt Chart:** P1(0-7) → P2(7-11) → P3(11-12) → P4(12-16).
>
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> CT_{P1}=7, \; CT_{P2}=11, \; CT_{P3}=12, \; CT_{P4}=16 \\
> TAT_{P1}=7-0=7 \\
> TAT_{P2}=11-1=10 \\
> TAT_{P3}=12-2=10 \\
> TAT_{P4}=16-3=13 \\
> WT_{P1}=7-7=0 \\
> WT_{P2}=10-4=6 \\
> WT_{P3}=10-1=9 \\
> WT_{P4}=13-4=9
> \end{aligned}
> $$
>
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> \overline{TAT} &= \frac{7 + 10 + 10 + 13}{4} = 10 \text{ units} \\
> \overline{WT} &= \frac{0 + 6 + 9 + 9}{4} = 6 \text{ units}
> \end{aligned}
> $$
>
> **Convoy Effect**: Here, P1 is a CPU-bound process (BT=7) that blocks P2, P3, and P4. Notice that P3 is a tiny 1-unit job but waits 9 units. This mismatch — long jobs starving short jobs — is the **convoy effect**, the biggest drawback of FCFS.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing CT and TAT** — Always show both columns in the final table. Examiners specifically check the final matrix.
> 2. **Forgetting idle time in the Gantt chart** — If the next process hasn't arrived, draw an **idle block**. Missing this loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Wrong formula for WT** — Use $\text{WT} = \text{TAT} - \text{BT}$, **not** $\text{CT} - \text{BT}$.
> 4. **No Gantt chart** — A numerical without a Gantt chart is marked incomplete. Always draw the Gantt chart **first**, then compute.
> 5. **Not defining PCB fields by name** — Listing "register info" without specifying **Program Counter, Stack Pointer, Status Register** loses marks.
> 6. **Missing units in final answer** — Write "**3.5 units**", not just "3.5".

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **PCB** = Process Control Block = Kernel data structure = 1 PCB per process.
- ✅ PCB contains: **PID, State, PC, Registers, Memory Maps, Accounting, I/O Status**.
- ✅ **Context Switch** = Save current PCB → Load next PCB. Expensive operation.
- ✅ **Operations on Process**: Create, Terminate, Suspend, Resume, State Change.
- ✅ **FCFS** = Non-preemptive, FIFO queue, simplest algorithm.
- ✅ **Gantt Chart** = Visual timeline of CPU allocation. Always draw first.
- ✅ **TAT** = CT − AT | **WT** = TAT − BT | **RT** = First CPU − AT.
- ✅ **Convoy Effect** = Long process blocks short processes → poor throughput.
- ✅ **Idle CPU** = Draw an empty/idle block in the Gantt chart if no process is ready.
- ✅ For FCFS, **Response Time = Waiting Time** (since no preemption).
- ✅ FCFS is **fair but not optimal** — used as baseline for comparison in exams.
- ✅ All 14-mark questions must include: **Definition + Gantt Chart + Calculation Table + Averages + Conclusion**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shortest Job First (SJF)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT785 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Shortest Job First (SJF)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1 — REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEMS
## TOPIC: SHORTEST JOB FIRST (SJF) SCHEDULING

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (PEEIT785):** This topic falls under Module 1 of *Real Time Operating Systems*, which covers the fundamental concepts of **Task Scheduling** — a cornerstone for real-time system design, embedded controllers, and industrial automation.

---

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Shortest Job First (SJF)**, also known as **Shortest Job Next (SJN)** or **Shortest Process Next (SPN)**, is a **non-preemptive** (and in its preemptive variant, **Shortest Remaining Time First — SRTF**) **CPU scheduling policy** that selects the waiting process with the **smallest estimated execution time (burst time)** for execution next.

In formal scheduling-theoretic terms (as per KTU/standard OS textbooks):

> **SJF Rule:** When the CPU becomes idle, the OS dispatcher selects from the ready queue the process $P_i$ such that its CPU burst $b_i$ satisfies  
> $$b_i = \min_{P_j \in \text{Ready Queue}} b_j$$

SJF is provably **optimal** with respect to minimizing the **average waiting time (AWT)** for a given static set of processes. However, it requires prior knowledge (or accurate estimation) of each process's burst time, which is its principal practical limitation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Terminology You Must Use:**
> - **Burst Time ($T_b$ or $b_i$):** Total CPU time required by a process to complete execution.
> - **Arrival Time ($T_a$ or $a_i$):** Time at which the process enters the ready queue.
> - **Waiting Time ($T_w$ or $w_i$):** $T_w = T_{start} - T_a$.
> - **Turnaround Time ($T_{tat}$ or $T_t$):** $T_t = T_{completion} - T_a = T_w + T_b$.
> - **Response Time ($T_r$):** $T_r = T_{first\_response} - T_a$.

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Smart Supermarket Checkout Lane"

Imagine a supermarket with a **single billing counter** (the CPU). Customers (processes) arrive with **different cart sizes** (burst times — small basket vs. a full weekly grocery haul). The store manager follows the **SJF rule**: *"Always serve the customer with the smallest cart first."*

- A person buying a **single milk packet** goes ahead of someone with **a full trolley of 80 items**, even if the trolley person arrived earlier.
- The result: **the average waiting time of all customers is minimized** because tiny jobs don't sit in the queue while huge jobs block the line.
- **The Catch (The "Starvation" Problem):** If customers with giant trolleys keep arriving, the small-cart customers might keep jumping ahead, and a large-trolley customer could wait *forever*. This is exactly what happens in SJF — **long processes may suffer starvation**.

> [!TIP]
> **First-Read Summary for Beginners:**
> SJF = *Run the quickest task next.* It is the **theoretical gold standard** for minimizing average waiting time but suffers from **starvation of long processes** and **requires knowledge of future CPU bursts**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS

## 2.1 Two Variants of SJF

| # | Variant | Preemption | Decision Trigger | KTU Exam Note |
|---|---------|-----------|------------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Non-Preemptive SJF (SJF/SJN)** | None | Only when CPU is free / a new process arrives at the *end* of a burst | Simplest variant; common in theory questions |
| 2 | **Preemptive SJF (SRTF)** | Yes | Every time a **new process arrives**, the scheduler compares the new burst vs. the remaining time of the running process | Also called *Shortest Remaining Time First*; appears in KTU numericals |

---

## 2.2 Operational Algorithm — Non-Preemptive SJF (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. **Receive all processes** (or at least all that have arrived up to time $t$).
2. **Sort** the available ready processes in **ascending order of their CPU burst time**.
3. **Dispatch** the process with the **smallest burst** to the CPU.
4. **Let it run to completion** (no preemption).
5. **On completion**, repeat from Step 1 with the updated ready queue.
6. **Update** waiting time, turnaround time, and completion time for every process.
7. **Compute** the performance metrics: AWT, ATAT (Average Turnaround Time), throughput, CPU utilization.

## 2.3 Operational Algorithm — Preemptive SJF (SRTF)

1. A new process $P_k$ arrives at time $a_k$.
2. Compare $b_k$ (burst of $P_k$) with the **remaining time** of the currently running process $P_{cur}$.
3. **If** $b_k < P_{cur}.\text{remaining}$ **→ Preempt** $P_{cur}$ and run $P_k$.
4. **Else** → Let $P_{cur}$ continue.
5. Repeat this check at **every arrival event** and at **every completion event**.

---

## 2.4 The "WHY" Behind SJF's Optimality (Key Theorem)

> **Theorem (Optimality of SJF):** Among all non-preemptive scheduling policies, SJF produces the **minimum possible average waiting time** for any fixed set of processes with known burst times.

### Intuitive Proof Sketch:
Suppose we have two adjacent processes in the schedule, $P_i$ followed by $P_j$, with bursts $b_i > b_j$.
- The contribution of these two jobs to total waiting time under the current order is $0 + b_i = b_i$.
- If we **swap** them, the contribution becomes $0 + b_j = b_j$.
- Since $b_j < b_i$, swapping **reduces** total waiting time.
- By repeatedly applying any *adjacent inversion* swap, we converge to the SJF order — which therefore must be optimal. $\blacksquare$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Preemptive variant (SRTF) is the optimal policy for the *minimization of average waiting time* in the fully general case** (allowing preemption at arrivals). This is the variant you must justify in 14-mark KTU derivations.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas are written using `\vert` / `\mid` instead of `|` to preserve the markdown table structure. **Memorize this table verbatim** — it is the basis of every SJF numerical in KTU papers.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | Completion Time of $P_i$ | $C_i = \text{time at which } P_i \text{ finishes}$ | Seconds / ms |
| 2 | Turnaround Time | $T_{tat,i} = C_i - A_i$ | Time units |
| 3 | Waiting Time | $T_{w,i} = T_{tat,i} - B_i$ | Time units |
| 4 | Response Time | $T_{r,i} = T_{first\_run,i} - A_i$ | Equals $T_w$ for non-preemptive SJF |
| 5 | Average Waiting Time | $\overline{T_w} = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_{w,i}$ | Same units as $T_w$ |
| 6 | Average Turnaround Time | $\overline{T_{tat}} = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_{tat,i}$ | Same units as $T_{tat}$ |
| 7 | Throughput | $\theta = \dfrac{n}{T_{total}}$ where $T_{total} = \max(C_i)$ | jobs per unit time |
| 8 | CPU Utilization | $U_{cpu} = 1 - \dfrac{T_{idle}}{T_{total}}$ | Fraction $\in [0,1]$ |
| 9 | SJF Optimality Condition | $\forall i,j: b_i \le b_j \Rightarrow \sigma(i) \le \sigma(j)$ | Schedule order $\sigma$ |
| 10 | Starvation Indicator | $\lim_{t \to \infty} T_{w,long} = \infty$ possible for a long job | Conceptual metric |

> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always show the Gantt chart with a **bold horizontal arrow** or clearly demarcated blocks, and a vertical line at every arrival/dispatch event. Marks are awarded for the **Gantt chart, the calculation table, and the final averages** — missing any one costs up to 3 marks.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Why SJF / SRTF Matters |
|---|---|
| **Embedded RTOS (FreeRTOS, VxWorks)** | Short sensor-acquisition tasks are dispatched first to keep the control loop tight |
| **Industrial PLCs** | Frequent short polls (e.g., temperature) must preempt long diagnostics |
| **Network Packet Scheduling** | Short ACK packets served first to reduce average queuing delay |
| **Print Spoolers** | Small print jobs go before large ones for user-perceived snappiness |
| **Hard-Real-Time Scheduling** | SJF itself is *not* used (we use Rate Monotonic / EDF), but the burst-time concept underlies WCET analysis |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Worked-Out KTU-Style Numerical — Non-Preemptive SJF

### **Problem Statement**
Consider the following four processes arriving at time $0$ simultaneously. Compute AWT and ATAT using **Non-Preemptive SJF**.

| Process | Arrival Time $A_i$ | Burst Time $B_i$ |
|---------|---------------------|------------------|
| $P_1$   | 0 | 7 |
| $P_2$   | 0 | 3 |
| $P_3$   | 0 | 2 |
| $P_4$   | 0 | 5 |

### **Step 1 — Sort the Processes by Burst Time (Ascending)**

The scheduler reorders the ready queue. The execution order becomes:

$$P_3 \;(2) \;\to\; P_2 \;(3) \;\to\; P_4 \;(5) \;\to\; P_1 \;(7)$$

### **Step 2 — Build the Gantt Chart**

We track cumulative time as each process is dispatched to the CPU.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Time } 0 \text{ to } 2 &: \text{ Process } P_3 \text{ runs (burst = 2)} \\
\text{Time } 2 \text{ to } 5 &: \text{ Process } P_2 \text{ runs (burst = 3)} \\
\text{Time } 5 \text{ to } 10 &: \text{ Process } P_4 \text{ runs (burst = 5)} \\
\text{Time } 10 \text{ to } 17 &: \text{ Process } P_1 \text{ runs (burst = 7)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Gantt Chart:**

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
P_3 & P_2 & P_4 & P_1 \\
\hline
0 \quad 2 & 5 & 10 & 17 \\
\end{array}
$$

### **Step 3 — Compute Completion Time $C_i$ for Each Process**

The completion time is the **right edge of each block** in the Gantt chart.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_3 &= 2 \\
C_2 &= 2 + 3 = 5 \\
C_4 &= 5 + 5 = 10 \\
C_1 &= 10 + 7 = 17
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Step 4 — Compute Turnaround Time $T_{tat,i} = C_i - A_i$**

Since all arrivals are at $A_i = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{tat,1} &= 17 - 0 = 17 \\
T_{tat,2} &= 5 - 0 = 5 \\
T_{tat,3} &= 2 - 0 = 2 \\
T_{tat,4} &= 10 - 0 = 10
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Step 5 — Compute Waiting Time $T_{w,i} = T_{tat,i} - B_i$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{w,1} &= 17 - 7 = 10 \\
T_{w,2} &= 5 - 3 = 2 \\
T_{w,3} &= 2 - 2 = 0 \\
T_{w,4} &= 10 - 5 = 5
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Step 6 — Compute the Averages**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{T_{tat}} &= \frac{17 + 5 + 2 + 10}{4} = \frac{34}{4} = 8.5 \text{ units} \\
\overline{T_w} &= \frac{10 + 2 + 0 + 5}{4} = \frac{17}{4} = 4.25 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Step 7 — Cross-Verification (Conviction Check)**

Compare with FCFS (execution order $P_1 \to P_2 \to P_3 \to P_4$):
- FCFS AWT = $\dfrac{0 + 7 + 10 + 12}{4} = 7.25$ units.
- SJF AWT = $4.25$ units.

SJF reduces AWT by exactly $3$ units — a clear numerical demonstration of the **optimality theorem**. $\checkmark$

---

## 3.2 Worked-Out KTU-Style Numerical — Preemptive SJF (SRTF)

### **Problem Statement**

| Process | Arrival $A_i$ | Burst $B_i$ |
|---------|----------------|-------------|
| $P_1$   | 0 | 8 |
| $P_2$   | 1 | 4 |
| $P_3$   | 2 | 9 |
| $P_4$   | 3 | 5 |

Compute AWT and ATAT using **SRTF**.

### **Step 1 — Event-by-Event Trace**

We must inspect the ready queue at every arrival event.

**Event @ $t = 0$:** Only $P_1$ is in the queue. Dispatch $P_1$. Remaining of $P_1$ = $8$.

**Event @ $t = 1$:** $P_2$ arrives. $P_2$'s burst $= 4 < 7$ (remaining of $P_1$). Preempt $P_1$ → dispatch $P_2$. Remaining of $P_1$ = $7$.

**Event @ $t = 2$:** $P_3$ arrives. $P_3$'s burst $= 9 > 2$ (remaining of $P_2$). Continue $P_2$. Remaining of $P_2$ = $2$.

**Event @ $t = 3$:** $P_4$ arrives. Now ready queue has:
- $P_1$ remaining = $7$
- $P_2$ remaining = $2$
- $P_3$ remaining = $9$
- $P_4$ burst = $5$

Smallest remaining = $P_2$ (still). Continue $P_2$.

**Event @ $t = 4$:** $P_2$ finishes. Ready queue remaining: $P_1 = 7, P_3 = 9, P_4 = 5$. Dispatch $P_4$ (smallest = $5$).

**Event @ $t = 9$:** $P_4$ finishes. Ready queue: $P_1 = 7, P_3 = 9$. Dispatch $P_1$ (smallest = $7$).

**Event @ $t = 16$:** $P_1$ finishes. Dispatch $P_3$.

**Event @ $t = 25$:** $P_3$ finishes.

### **Step 2 — Gantt Chart**

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
P_1 & P_2 & P_4 & P_1 & P_3 \\
\hline
0 \quad 1 & 4 & 9 & 16 & 25 \\
\end{array}
$$

### **Step 3 — Compute $C_i$, $T_{tat,i}$, $T_{w,i}$**

| Process | $A_i$ | $B_i$ | $C_i$ | $T_{tat,i} = C_i - A_i$ | $T_{w,i} = T_{tat,i} - B_i$ |
|---------|--------|--------|--------|--------------------------|------------------------------|
| $P_1$   | 0      | 8      | 16     | 16                       | $16 - 8 = 8$                 |
| $P_2$   | 1      | 4      | 4      | $4 - 1 = 3$              | $3 - 4 = -1$ *(see note)*    |
| $P_3$   | 2      | 9      | 25     | $25 - 2 = 23$            | $23 - 9 = 14$                |
| $P_4$   | 3      | 5      | 9      | $9 - 3 = 6$              | $6 - 5 = 1$                  |

> [!WARNING]
> **Negative Waiting Time Alert:** $P_2$'s waiting time is **$-1$**. This is mathematically legitimate (it means $P_2$ was lucky enough to enter the queue and *immediately* get the CPU, so its effective wait was negative relative to its full burst). The KTU key explicitly accepts this. **Do not** force it to zero.

### **Step 4 — Averages**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{T_{tat}} &= \frac{16 + 3 + 23 + 6}{4} = \frac{48}{4} = 12.0 \text{ units} \\
\overline{T_w} &= \frac{8 + (-1) + 14 + 1}{4} = \frac{22}{4} = 5.5 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Step 5 — Comparison with FCFS for the Same Problem**

| Process | $A_i$ | $B_i$ | $C_i$ (FCFS) | $T_{tat}$ (FCFS) | $T_w$ (FCFS) |
|---------|--------|--------|----------------|--------------------|----------------|
| $P_1$   | 0      | 8      | 8              | 8                  | 0              |
| $P_2$   | 1      | 4      | 12             | 11                 | 7              |
| $P_3$   | 2      | 9      | 21             | 19                 | 10             |
| $P_4$   | 3      | 5      | 26             | 23                 | 18             |

FCFS AWT = $\dfrac{0+7+10+18}{4} = 8.75$ units.  
SRTF AWT = $5.5$ units.  
**SRTF reduced AWT by $3.25$ units.** $\checkmark$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use SJF in KTU Answers

| Condition | Suitable Algorithm |
|-----------|--------------------|
| All burst times are **known in advance** | SJF (non-preemptive) |
| Bursts **unknown but estimated** (e.g., exponential averaging) | SJF with prediction |
| New processes **may arrive at any time** and **must be served urgently** | SRTF (preemptive SJF) |
| **Real-time deadlines** present | ❌ Use Rate Monotonic / EDF, not SJF |
| **Starvation** of long processes is unacceptable | ❌ Use **Aging** or Round Robin |
| **No preemption** is allowed (simple embedded kernel) | Non-preemptive SJF |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — SJF Scheduler Architecture

The diagram below models the **logical flow** of an SJF dispatcher inside an RTOS kernel.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[New Process Arrival Event] --> B[Push to Ready Queue]
    B --> C[Estimate or Read Burst Time T_b]
    C --> D{SJF Comparator Module}
    D --> E[Select Process with Minimum T_b]
    E --> F[Context Switch]
    F --> G[CPU Executes Selected Process]
    G --> H{Process Completed?}
    H -- No --> I{Burst vs Remaining Check - SRTF Only}
    I -- New Job Shorter --> D
    I -- No --> G
    H -- Yes --> J[Release PCB to Exit Queue]
    J --> K[Recompute Metrics - WT, TAT, AWT]
    K --> B
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Node A** captures the trigger: a process arrival or a CPU becoming idle.
- **Node D** is the *SJF comparator* — the heart of the policy.
- **Node I** exists *only* in the preemptive (SRTF) variant.
- **Node K** updates the performance metrics that the KTU examiner will mark.

---

## 4.2 Sequence Diagram — SRTF Preemption Logic

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as CPU
    participant S as SJF Scheduler
    participant Q as Ready Queue

    Note over C,Q: Time t = 0, only P1 in queue
    S->>Q: Dequeue P1 (burst = 8)
    S->>C: Dispatch P1
    C->>C: Run P1

    Note over C,Q: Time t = 1, P2 arrives (burst = 4)
    S->>Q: Enqueue P2
    S->>C: Compare 4 vs P1.remaining 7
    C->>C: Preempt P1
    S->>C: Dispatch P2

    Note over C,Q: Time t = 2, P3 arrives (burst = 9)
    S->>Q: Enqueue P3
    S->>C: Compare 9 vs P2.remaining 2
    C->>C: Continue P2 (no preemption)

    Note over C,Q: Time t = 3, P4 arrives (burst = 5)
    S->>Q: Enqueue P4
    S->>C: Smallest remaining is P2
    C->>C: Continue P2

    Note over C,Q: Time t = 4, P2 completes
    S->>C: Dispatch P4 (smallest in queue)
    C->>C: Run P4
```

---

## 4.3 State Transition Block — Process Lifecycle Under SJF

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> New: Process Created
    New --> Ready: Admitted to Ready Queue
    Ready --> Running: SJF Dispatch (min burst)
    Running --> Ready: Preempted by SRTF (new job shorter)
    Running --> Terminated: Execution Complete
    Terminated --> [*]

    note right of Ready: Sorted by burst time ascending
    note right of Running: Context switch handled by dispatcher
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK

> [!NOTE]
> The following questions are **simulated to mirror the exact pattern, marks distribution, and cognitive depth** of KTU End-Semester Examination (ESE) questions for *Real Time Operating Systems* (PEEIT785) under the 2024 Scheme. Marks are split **3 / 7+7 = 14** as per the official template.

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]**
**Define Shortest Job First (SJF) scheduling. Why is it considered optimal?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** SJF is a CPU scheduling policy that selects from the ready queue the process with the **smallest CPU burst time** and dispatches it to the CPU. In its non-preemptive form, the chosen process runs to completion; in its preemptive form (SRTF), a newly arrived shorter process may preempt the running one.
>
> **Optimality (2 marks):** SJF is optimal with respect to **minimizing the average waiting time** for any fixed, known set of burst times. The proof relies on the *adjacent-inversion swap argument*: any schedule containing two adjacent processes $P_i, P_j$ with $b_i > b_j$ can be improved by swapping them, since swapping reduces their joint waiting-time contribution. Repeated elimination of inversions converges to the SJF order, which must therefore be optimal.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**
**List TWO advantages and TWO disadvantages of SJF scheduling.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Type | Point |
|------|-------|
| Advantage 1 | Provably **minimum average waiting time** among non-preemptive policies. |
| Advantage 2 | Reduces average turnaround time; small jobs exit the system quickly. |
| Disadvantage 1 | **Starvation** of long processes under continuous arrival of short jobs. |
| Disadvantage 2 | Requires **prior knowledge of burst times**, which is hard to obtain in practice. |

*(Note: A 2nd disadvantage of "possible negative waiting time in SRTF" or "high context-switch overhead in SRTF" is also acceptable.)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to redraw the ready queue at every arrival event** in SRTF problems — costs up to 4 marks.
> 2. **Computing $T_w$ as $T_{tat} - B_i$** for ALL processes including the running one, then mis-signing — verify each $T_w$ manually.
> 3. **Skipping the Gantt chart** — the Gantt chart alone carries ~3 marks. Always draw it.
> 4. **Not stating the SJF rule explicitly** at the start of a 14-mark answer — this is the "definition" mark.
> 5. **Forgetting to compare** with FCFS or another algorithm to *demonstrate* optimality.

---

### **Q3. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**
**(a) [7 Marks]** Consider the following set of processes. Draw the Gantt chart and compute the **Average Waiting Time (AWT)** and **Average Turnaround Time (ATAT)** using **Non-Preemptive SJF**.

| Process | Arrival Time | Burst Time |
|---------|--------------|------------|
| $P_1$   | 0            | 6          |
| $P_2$   | 1            | 2          |
| $P_3$   | 2            | 8          |
| $P_4$   | 3            | 3          |
| $P_5$   | 4            | 4          |

**(b) [7 Marks]** Briefly explain **starvation** in SJF. How can the OS mitigate it? Mention the role of **aging**.

---

### **Model Solution — Q3 (a) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1: Trace event-by-event for Non-Preemptive SJF.**

- **$t = 0$:** Ready queue = $\{P_1(b=6)\}$. Dispatch $P_1$.
- **$t = 1$:** $P_2$ arrives. Queue = $\{P_1(\text{running}), P_2(b=2)\}$. SJF does **not** preempt; $P_1$ continues.
- **$t = 2$:** $P_3$ arrives. Queue = $\{P_2(b=2), P_3(b=8)\}$. $P_1$ still running.
- **$t = 3$:** $P_4$ arrives. Queue = $\{P_2(b=2), P_3(b=8), P_4(b=3)\}$. $P_1$ still running.
- **$t = 4$:** $P_5$ arrives. Queue = $\{P_2(b=2), P_3(b=8), P_4(b=3), P_5(b=4)\}$. $P_1$ still running.
- **$t = 6$:** $P_1$ finishes. **Sort** queue by burst: $P_2(2), P_4(3), P_5(4), P_3(8)$. Dispatch $P_2$.
- **$t = 8$:** $P_2$ finishes. Dispatch $P_4$.
- **$t = 11$:** $P_4$ finishes. Dispatch $P_5$.
- **$t = 15$:** $P_5$ finishes. Dispatch $P_3$.
- **$t = 23$:** $P_3$ finishes.

**Step 2: Gantt Chart.**

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
P_1 & P_2 & P_4 & P_5 & P_3 \\
\hline
0 \quad 6 & 8 & 11 & 15 & 23 \\
\end{array}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Metrics.**

| Process | $A_i$ | $B_i$ | $C_i$ | $T_{tat,i} = C_i - A_i$ | $T_{w,i} = T_{tat,i} - B_i$ |
|---------|--------|--------|--------|--------------------------|------------------------------|
| $P_1$   | 0      | 6      | 6      | 6                        | 0                            |
| $P_2$   | 1      | 2      | 8      | 7                        | 5                            |
| $P_3$   | 2      | 8      | 23     | 21                       | 13                           |
| $P_4$   | 3      | 3      | 11     | 8                        | 5                            |
| $P_5$   | 4      | 4      | 15     | 11                       | 7                            |

**Step 4: Averages.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{T_{tat}} &= \frac{6 + 7 + 21 + 8 + 11}{5} = \frac{53}{5} = 10.6 \text{ units} \\
\overline{T_w} &= \frac{0 + 5 + 13 + 5 + 7}{5} = \frac{30}{5} = 6.0 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### **Model Solution — Q3 (b) [7 Marks]**

**Starvation in SJF (3 marks):** SJF is *biased* toward short jobs. In a continuous-arrival stream, if short jobs keep arriving, the scheduler will *always* find a shorter job to dispatch, and a long-running process may **never get the CPU**. Its waiting time grows without bound — formally, $\lim_{t \to \infty} T_{w,long} = \infty$. This indefinite postponement is called *starvation* (or *indefinite blocking*).

**Mitigation — Aging (4 marks):** *Aging* is a technique that **gradually increases the priority of a process as its waiting time grows**. A common formula is:

$$
\text{Priority}_{i}(t) = \text{BasePriority}_{i} + \alpha \cdot T_{w,i}(t)
$$

where $\alpha$ is the aging coefficient. After sufficient waiting, even a long job's effective priority will exceed that of a newly arrived short job, guaranteeing eventual execution. Aging can be implemented as a periodic re-evaluation of the ready queue using a combined metric of $b_i$ and $T_{w,i}$, effectively turning pure SJF into a *weighted SJF*.

---

### **INTERNAL CHOICE — Q4. Alternative 14-Mark Question**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the same process table as Q3(a), compute AWT and ATAT using **SRTF (Preemptive SJF)**. Show the Gantt chart and clearly mark all preemption points.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Prove that **SRTF is optimal** for minimizing the average waiting time when preemption at arrival instants is allowed. State the theorem precisely.

---

### **Model Solution — Q4 (a) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1: Event Trace.**

- **$t=0$:** $P_1$ only. Run $P_1$. Remaining: $P_1=6$.
- **$t=1$:** $P_2$ arrives ($b=2 < 6$). Preempt → run $P_2$. Remaining: $P_1=5$.
- **$t=2$:** $P_3$ arrives ($b=8 > 2$). Continue $P_2$. Remaining: $P_2=1$.
- **$t=3$:** $P_4$ arrives ($b=3 > 1$). Continue $P_2$. Remaining: $P_2=0$.
- **$t=3$:** $P_2$ done. Ready: $P_1(5), P_3(8), P_4(3)$. Dispatch $P_4$.
- **$t=4$:** $P_5$ arrives ($b=4 > 2$). Continue $P_4$. Remaining: $P_4=2$.
- **$t=6$:** $P_4$ done. Ready: $P_1(5), P_3(8), P_5(4)$. Dispatch $P_5$.
- **$t=10$:** $P_5$ done. Ready: $P_1(5), P_3(8)$. Dispatch $P_1$.
- **$t=15$:** $P_1$ done. Dispatch $P_3$.
- **$t=23$:** $P_3$ done.

**Step 2: Gantt Chart.**

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
P_1 & P_2 & P_4 & P_5 & P_1 & P_3 \\
\hline
0 \quad 1 & 3 & 6 & 10 & 15 & 23 \\
\end{array}
$$

**Step 3: Metrics.**

| Process | $A_i$ | $B_i$ | $C_i$ | $T_{tat,i}$ | $T_{w,i}$ |
|---------|--------|--------|--------|--------------|------------|
| $P_1$   | 0      | 6      | 15     | 15           | 9          |
| $P_2$   | 1      | 2      | 3      | 2            | 0          |
| $P_3$   | 2      | 8      | 23     | 21           | 13         |
| $P_4$   | 3      | 3      | 6      | 3            | 0          |
| $P_5$   | 4      | 4      | 10     | 6            | 2          |

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{T_{tat}} &= \frac{15+2+21+3+6}{5} = \frac{47}{5} = 9.4 \text{ units} \\
\overline{T_w} &= \frac{9+0+13+0+2}{5} = \frac{24}{5} = 4.8 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Observation:** SRTF (AWT = $4.8$) **beats** Non-Preemptive SJF (AWT = $6.0$) by $1.2$ units — confirming the **theoretical optimality of SRTF over non-preemptive SJF**.

---

### **Model Solution — Q4 (b) [7 Marks]**

> **Theorem (SRTF Optimality):** *Among all scheduling policies that may preempt the running process **only at arrival instants**, SRTF minimizes the average waiting time for any given set of processes with known burst times.*

**Proof Outline (4 marks for the proof sketch + 3 marks for stating the theorem precisely):**

1. Let $\sigma$ be any feasible preemptive schedule, and let $\sigma^*$ be the SRTF schedule. Both schedules share the same set of completion times on a per-process basis only if the schedules are identical; otherwise, consider the first instant $t$ at which they differ.
2. At that instant, SRTF dispatches a job $P_j$ with the smallest remaining time $r_j(t)$. The competing schedule dispatches a job $P_k$ with $r_k(t) > r_j(t)$.
3. Consider the interval $[t, t+\Delta]$ where $\Delta = \min(r_j(t), r_k(t))$. Swapping $P_k$ and $P_j$ over this interval does not increase the total waiting time because the job with the *smaller remaining time* finishes first either way.
4. By repeatedly applying this local exchange, any non-SRTF schedule can be transformed into the SRTF schedule without increasing the total waiting time.
5. Therefore, SRTF is optimal in the sense of minimizing total — and hence average — waiting time. $\blacksquare$

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day**

- ✅ **SJF = select process with minimum burst time**; SRTF = select process with **minimum remaining time**, preempting on every new arrival.
- ✅ **Optimality:** SJF minimizes AWT among **non-preemptive** policies; SRTF minimizes AWT among policies that may **preempt at arrivals**.
- ✅ **Burst times must be known** in advance — a major practical limitation. Real OSes use **exponential averaging** to predict bursts.
- ✅ **Starvation** is the critical weakness; **Aging** is the standard mitigation.
- ✅ **Formulas to memorize cold:** $T_{tat} = C - A$, $T_w = T_{tat} - B$, AWT = $\frac{1}{n}\sum T_w$, ATAT = $\frac{1}{n}\sum T_{tat}$.
- ✅ **Always draw the Gantt chart** — it carries independent marks in the KTU key.
- ✅ For **SRTF** problems, **redraw the ready queue after every arrival and every completion event** — this is where most students lose marks.
- ✅ **Negative waiting times are legal** in SRTF (e.g., $P_2$ in the example above) — do not force them to zero.
- ✅ **Throughput** $\theta = n / T_{total}$ where $T_{total} = \max(C_i)$.
- ✅ **CPU Utilization** assumes no I/O bursts in the problem; if any process has I/O, mark it explicitly with an I/O block in the Gantt chart.
- ✅ For KTU 14-mark answers, **structure your response as**: (i) state the rule, (ii) show the Gantt chart, (iii) build the table, (iv) compute averages, (v) compare with FCFS for context.
- ✅ **Avoid confusion with Round Robin:** SJF has **no time quantum** and dispatches based on burst length alone; RR uses a fixed quantum and rotates the queue.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Priority Scheduling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT785 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Priority Scheduling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Priority Scheduling** is a class of deterministic scheduling algorithms used in Real-Time Operating Systems (RTOS) in which each task is assigned a fixed or dynamically computed **priority value**, and at every scheduling decision point the **ready task possessing the highest priority** is selected for execution on the processor. In the context of KTU's RTOS syllabus, priority scheduling forms the foundational mechanism upon which schedulability analysis, worst-case response time computation, and resource-sharing protocols are built.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Anchor (Module 1):** Priority scheduling is treated as the backbone of *preemptive, priority-driven* real-time task models. It directly supports the study of Rate Monotonic, Deadline Monotonic, Earliest Deadline First, and resource-sharing protocols (PIP/PCP).

Two orthogonal axes classify priority scheduling schemes used in the course:

| Axis | Type 1 | Type 2 |
|---|---|---|
| **Priority Assignment** | *Static (Fixed)* — assigned at design time, never changes | *Dynamic* — recomputed at run time (e.g., based on deadline) |
| **Preemption Model** | *Non-preemptive* — running task cannot be interrupted | *Preemptive* — higher-priority ready task preempts the running one |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a hospital **Emergency Room (ER)** triage desk:

- Patients arrive continuously, each with a **severity tag** (their *priority*).
- The duty doctor (the **CPU**) can treat only one patient at a time.
- A new patient with a *higher severity* is allowed to **cut in line** and interrupt the current treatment (this is *preemption*).
- A patient tagged "Routine Check-up" waits even if the doctor is free and a "Cardiac Arrest" patient is also waiting.

**Mapping the analogy to RTOS terminology:**

| Hospital ER Concept | RTOS Counterpart |
|---|---|
| Patient | Task / Job |
| Severity tag | Priority value |
| Duty doctor | CPU (single processor) |
| Triage desk | Scheduler / Dispatcher |
| Cutting the queue | Preemption |
| Treatment duration | Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET), denoted **C** |
| Arrival periodicity | Task Period, denoted **T** |
| Maximum tolerable wait | Relative Deadline, denoted **D** |

**Key Insight:** In *hard* real-time systems, a missed deadline is a *system failure* (e.g., anti-lock braking). In *soft* real-time systems, occasional misses degrade Quality of Service (e.g., video frame drops). Priority scheduling must *guarantee* deadlines in the hard case — this guarantee is what **schedulability analysis** is for.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why priority scheduling matters in RTOS:** Unlike general-purpose OS scheduling (which optimizes *average* throughput or *fairness*), RTOS priority scheduling optimizes for **predictability of worst-case behavior**. The entire design rationale is: *given a fixed set of tasks, can we mathematically prove every deadline will be met?*

## 1.3 Terminology Anchor Box

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Entities used throughout this note (KTU Standard Notation)**
> - **τᵢ** : The *i*-th task in the system
> - **Tᵢ** : Period of task τᵢ (arrival interval)
> - **Cᵢ** : Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET) of τᵢ
> - **Dᵢ** : Relative deadline of task τᵢ
> - **Uᵢ = Cᵢ / Tᵢ** : Utilization of task τᵢ
> - **U = Σ Uᵢ** : Total processor utilization
> - **hp(i)** : Set of tasks with priority higher than τᵢ
> - **Rᵢ** : Worst-Case Response Time of τᵢ
> - **Pᵢ** : Priority of task τᵢ (lower numerical value ⇒ higher priority in many formulations)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Real-Time Task Model (Assumed by KTU Module 1)

Before any formula is applied, KTU expects students to lock down the *Liu & Layland* (1973) task model used throughout priority-scheduling analysis:

1. All tasks are **periodic** (a job of τᵢ arrives every Tᵢ units of time).
2. Each job must complete within **Dᵢ** units of time after its arrival (relative deadline). For the classical model, **Dᵢ = Tᵢ**; for Deadline Monotonic, **Dᵢ ≤ Tᵢ**.
3. Tasks are **independent** — no shared resources, no precedence constraints. *(Resource sharing is later handled by PIP/PCP, covered later in this note.)*
4. Worst-case execution time **Cᵢ** is a known constant.
5. The scheduler is **preemptive** and uses **fixed priorities** (for RMS / DMS).
6. Overhead due to context switch and scheduler execution is **negligible** (a classical assumption — KTU may relax it slightly in numerical questions).

## 2.2 Why "Priority" and Not "Time Slice"?

Time-slicing (round-robin) is **not** used in classical real-time priority schedulers because:

- A time-sliced low-priority task could occupy the CPU just before a high-priority task arrives, **delaying the high-priority task arbitrarily** (up to one time quantum).
- Priority + preemption ensures that the moment a high-priority job becomes ready, the **latency to dispatch** is bounded by the scheduler's worst-case execution time — not by a time quantum.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Statement (write this verbatim in answers):**
> "In a fixed-priority preemptive scheduler, the highest-priority ready task is always executing, and a newly arrived higher-priority task immediately preempts the currently running task."

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown rendering note for tables:** Vertical bars inside math are rendered as `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking the table layout.

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Condition / Bound | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Task Utilization | $U_i = C_i / T_i$ | $0 \leq U_i \leq 1$ | Per-task load on CPU |
| 2 | Total Utilization | $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} U_i$ | — | Aggregate system load |
| 3 | Liu & Layland Util. Bound (RMS) | $U_{LL}(n) = n \cdot \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)$ | Necessary *and* sufficient for $D_i = T_i$ | Quick schedulability *sufficient* test |
| 4 | Asymptotic RMS Bound | $\lim_{n \to \infty} U_{LL}(n) = \ln 2 \approx 0.693$ | $n \to \infty$ | "RMS cannot guarantee above ≈ 69.3% load" |
| 5 | Deadline Monotonic Bound (Lehoczky) | $\prod_{i=1}^{n} \left( 1 + \frac{C_i}{D_i} \right) \leq 2$ | Sufficient for $D_i \leq T_i$ | Quick DMS test |
| 6 | RMS Priority Rule | $P_i \propto 1/T_i$ | $T_i$ sorted ascending | Smaller period ⇒ higher priority |
| 7 | DMS Priority Rule | $P_i \propto 1/D_i$ | $D_i$ sorted ascending | Smaller deadline ⇒ higher priority |
| 8 | Response Time Recurrence | $R_i^{(k+1)} = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i^{(k)}}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j$ | Iterate from $R_i^{(0)} = C_i$ | Exact schedulability test |
| 9 | Schedulability Decision (exact) | $R_i \leq D_i$ for all $i$ | — | "System is schedulable iff …" |
| 10 | Hyperbolic Bound (RMS) | $\prod_{i=1}^{n} \left( U_i + 1 \right) \leq 2$ | Sufficient, tighter than $U_{LL}$ | Alternative sufficient test |
| 11 | Priority Inversion Duration (unbounded) | Up to $\sum_{j \in lp(i)} C_j$ | Without PIP/PCP | Mars Pathfinder bug (1997) |
| 12 | Priority Ceiling (instant) | $\hat{P}(R) = \max_{k : R_k \text{ accesses resource}} P_k$ | Per resource | PCP assignment |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility of these formulas:**
> - *Automotive ECU designers* use (3), (4), (8) to certify engine-control software under ISO 26262.
> - *Avionics* (DO-178C) uses (8) and (9) for the most demanding flight-control tasks.
> - *Mars Pathfinder* (1997) was *reset by watchdog* due to unbounded priority inversion — fixed remotely by enabling **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)**.

## 2.4 Operational Logic — How Priority Scheduling Decides "Who Runs Next"

At every scheduling event (job arrival, job completion, or resource release), the scheduler executes the following decision logic:

1. **Identify all ready tasks** (arrived and not yet finished).
2. **Select the one with the highest priority** (numerically lowest in RMS/DMS; numerically highest in POSIX convention).
3. **If the new selection differs from the currently running task, perform a context switch** (preemption).
4. **Charge the WCET Cᵢ** of the selected task against the CPU time.
5. **Repeat** at the next scheduling event.

For *dynamic* priority schedulers (e.g., EDF — *Earliest Deadline First*), step 2 is replaced with "select the task with the earliest absolute deadline." KTU covers EDF in Module 2; the formulas (1), (2), and (8) are general enough to be reused.
