- Another process **reads bytes** from the other end.
- Data moves strictly in **one direction** (half-duplex).
- Both ends must belong to **related processes** (typically parent and child after `fork()`).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** A pipe is *not* a file. It is a **transient kernel object** with no name on the filesystem (in its anonymous form), existing only as long as some process keeps a file descriptor open to it.

## 1.3 Classification of Pipes

| **Pipe Type** | **Identifier** | **Lifetime** | **Visibility** |
|---------------|----------------|--------------|----------------|
| Anonymous Pipe | `pipe()` | Until all file descriptors close | Inherited only via `fork()` |
| Named Pipe (FIFO) | `mkfifo()` | Until explicitly unlinked | Appears as a file in filesystem |
| Byte Stream | — | FIFO order | Order-preserving |
| Message Pipe | — | Variable | Rare, OS-specific |

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:**
> KTU Module 2 covers Pipes as part of the broader **RTOS Inter-Process Communication** subsystem, directly mapped to **Course Outcome CO2**: *Apply process synchronization and communication mechanisms in real-time applications.*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Lifecycle of a Pipe

The lifecycle of a pipe in a POSIX system follows a deterministic sequence:

1. **Creation** — The kernel allocates a `pipe_inode_info` structure and a circular buffer of **64 KB (16 pages)**.
2. **Descriptor Binding** — Two file descriptors are bound: `fd[0]` (read) and `fd[1]` (write), both stored in the process's **file descriptor table**.
3. **Fork Propagation** — On `fork()`, both descriptors are duplicated in the child process's file descriptor table, sharing the same underlying kernel buffer.
4. **Data Transfer** — `write(fd[1], buf, n)` appends bytes to the kernel buffer; `read(fd[0], buf, n)` consumes bytes from the head.
5. **Synchronization** — Blocking semantics ensure producer/consumer coordination without busy-waiting.
6. **Termination** — When all descriptors to the write end close, a subsequent `read()` returns **0 (EOF)**.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Operation

### Why block on read?
If the pipe is empty and there exists at least one writer (write end open), the calling process is suspended by the kernel scheduler and placed in the **interruptible sleep state (`TASK_INTERRUPTIBLE`)**. This is critical in RTOS for avoiding CPU wastage.

### Why block on write?
If the kernel pipe buffer is **full** (default 64 KB on Linux), `write()` blocks until at least one byte is consumed by a reader. This implements **back-pressure flow control**.

### Why close unused ends?
Failing to close the unused end causes **deadlock** — a writer will never see EOF, and a reader will block forever.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity / Behavior** | **Formula / Rule** | **Unit / Value** |
|-------------------------|--------------------|------------------|
| Default pipe buffer capacity | `16 × 4096` bytes | **65536 bytes (64 KB)** |
| Read returns EOF when | All write ends closed, buffer empty | — |
| Write blocks when | Buffer full **AND** read end open | — |
| `pipe()` syscall return | `0` on success, `-1` on error | — |
| PIPE_BUF (atomic write limit) | `≥ 512` bytes (Linux: 4096) | **bytes** |
| Pipe direction | Strictly **unidirectional** | — |
| Maximum atomic write size | `≤ PIPE_BUF` is guaranteed atomic | **bytes** |
| Default FIFO buffer (named pipe) | Same as anonymous pipe | **64 KB** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PIPE_BUF atomicity rule:** If a write to a pipe is **≤ PIPE_BUF** bytes and **O_NONBLOCK** is clear, the kernel guarantees the write is **atomic** — no interleaving with other writers' data. Writes larger than PIPE_BUF may be split and interleaved.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In RTOS and embedded Linux systems, pipes are extensively used for:

- **Shell pipelines** — `ls -l \vert grep ".c" \vert wc -l` chains three processes through two pipes.
- **Process supervision** — Init systems (systemd) pipe service logs to journald.
- **GStreamer multimedia pipelines** — Audio/video frames flow between plugins via pads conceptually similar to pipes.
- **Embedded IPC** — In Linux-based RTOS (Xenomai, PREEMPT_RT), pipes serve as lightweight channels between hard real-time and user-space tasks.

---

## 2.5 Comparison: Anonymous Pipe vs Named Pipe (FIFO)

| **Property** | **Anonymous Pipe** | **Named Pipe (FIFO)** |
|--------------|--------------------|------------------------|
| Creation | `pipe()` | `mkfifo()` / `mknod()` |
| File system entry | None | Yes, special file |
| Visibility | Only parent/child after `fork()` | Any process with path access |
| Persistence | Dies when last fd closes | Survives until unlinked |
| Use case in RTOS | Parent-child data handoff | Unrelated process communication |
| Blocking on open | N/A (already open) | Blocks reader until writer opens (and vice versa) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Pipe Capacity and Throughput Analysis

Consider an RTOS pipeline where a **producer process** generates data at rate $\lambda$ bytes/second and a **consumer** drains data at rate $\mu$ bytes/second. We derive the **buffer occupancy** over time.

### Step 1 — Define the net flow rate

The instantaneous net change in pipe buffer level is given by the difference between the producer rate and the consumer rate:

$$ \frac{dB(t)}{dt} = \lambda(t) - \mu(t) $$

where $B(t)$ is the number of bytes resident in the pipe at time $t$.

### Step 2 — Integrate to find buffer growth

Assuming constant rates (steady-state workload):

$$ B(t) = B(0) + (\lambda - \mu) \cdot t $$

### Step 3 — Apply the capacity constraint

The kernel pipe buffer has a hard maximum:

$$ 0 \leq B(t) \leq C_{\text{pipe}} $$

$$ C_{\text{pipe}} = N_{\text{pages}} \times P_{\text{page}} $$

On Linux, the default is:

$$ C_{\text{pipe}} = 16 \times 4096 \text{ bytes} = 65536 \text{ bytes} $$

### Step 4 — Identify blocking condition

The producer blocks when $B(t) = C_{\text{pipe}}$, i.e.:

$$ t_{\text{block}} = \frac{C_{\text{pipe}} - B(0)}{\lambda - \mu} \quad \text{(if } \lambda > \mu \text{)} $$

If $\lambda \leq \mu$, the producer never blocks, and the steady-state buffer level is:

$$ B_{\text{steady}} = \max\left(0, \ B(0) - (\mu - \lambda) \cdot t\right) $$

### Step 5 — Compute worst-case blocking time

In an RTOS scheduling context, the worst-case blocking time $T_{\text{block}}$ (critical for **schedulability analysis**) is bounded by:

$$ T_{\text{block}} \leq \frac{C_{\text{pipe}}}{\mu - \lambda} $$

This upper bound allows a task to be characterized as a sporadic task with known maximum blocking duration.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Behavior Table for Pipe Operations

| **Operation** | **Pipe State** | **Blocking?** | **Returns** | **errno on error** |
|---------------|----------------|---------------|-------------|---------------------|
| `read(fd[0])` | Empty + writer exists | **Yes** | — | — |
| `read(fd[0])` | Empty + no writer | No | `0` (EOF) | — |
| `read(fd[0])` | Has data | No | Number of bytes read | `-1` if interrupted |
| `write(fd[1])` | Buffer has space | No | Number of bytes written | — |
| `write(fd[1])` | Buffer full | **Yes** | — | — |
| `write(fd[1])` | Buffer full + no reader | No | `-1` | `EPIPE` + `SIGPIPE` |
| `close(fd[0])` | Any | No | `0` | — |
| `close(fd[1])` | Any | No | `0` | — |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A sensor task writes 8 KB/s to a pipe; a logger task reads at 6 KB/s. Both start with the pipe empty. Find:
> (a) Time to fill the pipe to capacity.
> (b) Buffer occupancy after 20 seconds if consumer starts at $t = 5$ s.

**Given:**

$$ \lambda = 8192 \text{ B/s}, \quad \mu = 6144 \text{ B/s}, \quad C_{\text{pipe}} = 65536 \text{ B} $$

### Part (a) — Time to fill

Since $\lambda > \mu$, the buffer grows monotonically. From the formula:

$$ t_{\text{full}} = \frac{C_{\text{pipe}}}{\lambda - \mu} $$

$$ t_{\text{full}} = \frac{65536}{8192 - 6144} = \frac{65536}{2048} $$

$$ t_{\text{full}} = 32 \text{ seconds} $$

### Part (b) — Buffer occupancy at $t = 20$ s

For $0 \leq t < 5$: only producer runs.

$$ B(5) = 8192 \times 5 = 40960 \text{ B} $$

For $5 \leq t \leq 20$: net rate = $8192 - 6144 = 2048$ B/s.

$$ \Delta B = 2048 \times (20 - 5) = 2048 \times 15 = 30720 \text{ B} $$

$$ B(20) = 40960 + 30720 = 71680 \text{ B} $$

But the pipe capacity is only $65536$ B. So the producer **blocks** when $B = 65536$ B:

$$ t_{\text{block start}} = 5 + \frac{65536 - 40960}{2048} = 5 + 12 = 17 \text{ s} $$

Therefore $B(20) = \mathbf{65536 \text{ B}}$ (clamped at capacity).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Internal Pipe Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Producer Process] --> B[fd_write: filedes 1]
    B --> C[Kernel Pipe Buffer 64 KB]
    C --> D[fd_read: filedes 0]
    D --> E[Consumer Process]
    C --> F[Circular Index Head]
    C --> G[Circular Index Tail]
    H[Task Waiting Queue Readers] -.-> C
    I[Task Waiting Queue Writers] -.-> C
    J[EOF Logic Module] -.-> C
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: Producer–Consumer Coordination

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant P as Producer Task
    participant K as Kernel Pipe Buffer
    participant C as Consumer Task
    P->>K: write 4 KB data
    K-->>P: bytes accepted
    P->>K: write next 4 KB
    K-->>C: wakeup signal
    C->>K: read 4 KB
    K-->>C: data delivered
    Note over K: Buffer drains
    C->>K: read attempt
    K-->>C: BLOCK empty buffer
    P->>K: write 2 KB
    K-->>C: wakeup signal
    C->>K: read 2 KB
    P->>K: close fd_write
    C->>K: read attempt
    K-->>C: return 0 EOF
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Fork-Based Pipe Creation Workflow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Parent Process] --> B[Call pipe fd]
    B --> C[Kernel Allocates Buffer]
    C --> D[fd 0 read end]
    C --> E[fd 1 write end]
    A --> F[Call fork]
    F --> G[Child Process inherits fd 0 and fd 1]
    G --> H[Child closes write end fd 1]
    A --> I[Parent closes read end fd 0]
    I --> J[Parent writes to fd 1]
    H --> K[Child reads from fd 0]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Named FIFO Inter-Process Link

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Process A Writer] --> B[FIFO Special File in tmp/myfifo]
    B --> C[Process B Reader]
    B --> D[Process C Reader]
    A -.-> B
    note1[Data flows FIFO order kernel scheduled]
    B -.- note1
```

## 4.5 Functional Architecture: Pipe in RTOS Layered View

```mermaid
graph TB
    L1[Application Layer Producer Consumer] --> L2[POSIX API Layer pipe read write close]
    L2 --> L3[Virtual File System VFS Layer]
    L3 --> L4[Pipe File Operations inode ops]
    L4 --> L5[Kernel Pipe Buffer ring of 16 pages]
    L5 --> L6[Scheduler Wait Queues]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]

**Q: Define a pipe in the context of inter-process communication. List any two characteristics.**

**Model Answer:**

A pipe is a kernel-buffered, unidirectional communication channel used for inter-process communication between related processes. It is created using the POSIX `pipe()` system call and is exposed as two file descriptors — one for reading and one for writing.

**Two characteristics:**

1. **Unidirectional data flow** — Data moves in only one direction; for bidirectional communication, two pipes must be used.
2. **FIFO ordering** — Bytes written first are read first, preserving order across producer and consumer.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for any two valid characteristics.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: What is the role of the `pipe()` system call? Mention the meaning of `filedes[0]` and `filedes[1]`.**

**Model Answer:**

The `pipe()` system call creates an anonymous pipe and returns two file descriptors in the integer array `filedes[]`:

- `filedes[0]` → the **read end** of the pipe (used for receiving data)
- `filedes[1]` → the **write end** of the pipe (used for sending data)

It returns `0` on success and `-1` on failure.

*(1 mark for stating the role, 1 mark for describing filedes[0], 1 mark for filedes[1].)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – July 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the working of an anonymous pipe with a neat diagram. Describe how a parent and child process can communicate using a pipe after `fork()`.**

**Model Answer:**

An anonymous pipe is a kernel-managed, one-way byte stream created by `pipe(int fd[2])`. The kernel allocates a 64 KB circular buffer and returns `fd[0]` (read) and `fd[1]` (write).

**Working Steps:**

1. Parent calls `pipe(fd)` → kernel creates buffer and two descriptors.
2. Parent calls `fork()` → child inherits both descriptors automatically.
3. Parent closes `fd[0]` (read end) — it only writes.
4. Child closes `fd[1]` (write end) — it only reads.
5. Parent calls `write(fd[1], data, len)` → data placed in kernel buffer.
6. Child calls `read(fd[0], buf, len)` → data retrieved in FIFO order.

**Diagram:**

```
Parent Process                Kernel                Child Process
---------------              -------               ---------------
close(fd[0])                                        close(fd[1])
write(fd[1]) ----> [KERNEL PIPE BUFFER 64KB] <---- read(fd[0])
```

**Why close unused ends?** If the parent does not close `fd[0]`, the read end remains open in the parent's table, so even after the child closes its write end, the pipe will not generate EOF — causing the child's `read()` to block forever.

*(7 marks: 2 for working steps, 2 for diagram, 2 for fork mechanism, 1 for EOF explanation.)*

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Differentiate between anonymous pipes and named pipes (FIFOs). Explain the `mkfifo()` system call with an example.**

**Model Answer:**

**Differences:**

| **Feature** | **Anonymous Pipe** | **Named Pipe (FIFO)** |
|-------------|---------------------|------------------------|
| Identifier | Two file descriptors | Filesystem path name |
| Lifetime | Until descriptors close | Until unlinked |
| Communicating processes | Must be related (fork) | Unrelated processes allowed |
| Creation | `pipe()` | `mkfifo()` |

**`mkfifo()` System Call:**

$$int \text{ mkfifo}(\text{const char } \ast \text{pathname}, \text{mode\_t mode})$$

- Creates a FIFO special file at `pathname` with permissions `mode`.
- Returns `0` on success, `-1` on error.

**Example:**

```
#include <sys/types.h>
#include <sys/stat.h>

int main() {
    if (mkfifo("/tmp/myfifo", 0666) == -1) {
        perror("mkfifo");
        return 1;
    }
    // Now any process can open /tmp/myfifo for reading or writing
    return 0;
}
```

After creation, processes open the FIFO via `open("/tmp/myfifo", O_RDONLY)` or `O_WRONLY`. The `open()` call blocks until the other end is also opened — this provides automatic synchronization.

*(7 marks: 3 for differentiation table, 2 for `mkfifo()` syntax, 2 for example + open-blocking note.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks] Discuss the blocking and non-blocking behavior of pipe `read()` and `write()` operations under various conditions. What is the `PIPE_BUF` constant and why is it important?**

**Model Answer:**

**Blocking Behavior Table:**

| **Operation** | **Condition** | **Behavior** |
|---------------|---------------|--------------|
| `read()` | Buffer empty + write end open | **Blocks** until data arrives or all write ends close |
| `read()` | Buffer empty + no write end | Returns `0` (EOF) |
| `read()` | Buffer non-empty | Returns data immediately |
| `write()` | Buffer full + read end open | **Blocks** until reader consumes |
| `write()` | Buffer full + no read end | Returns `-1`, raises `SIGPIPE` |
| `write()` | Buffer has space | Returns immediately |

**Non-Blocking Mode** (`O_NONBLOCK` flag set via `fcntl`):

- `read()` on empty pipe returns `-1` with `errno = EAGAIN`.
- `write()` to full pipe returns `-1` with `errno = EAGAIN`.

**PIPE_BUF Significance:**

`PIPE_BUF` is a POSIX constant (≥ 512 bytes, **4096 on Linux**) that defines the maximum size of a write guaranteed to be **atomic**. If a writer issues `write(fd, buf, n)` with `n ≤ PIPE_BUF`, the kernel ensures the entire `n` bytes are written contiguously without interleaving from other writers.

For RTOS, this is critical: a task producing a structured message of size ≤ `PIPE_BUF` can be certain its message will not be torn apart by another concurrent writer.

*(7 marks: 3 for blocking table, 2 for non-blocking behavior, 2 for PIPE_BUF atomicity.)*

---

**(b) [7 Marks] With a suitable diagram, explain how pipes are implemented inside the Linux kernel. Discuss the role of the `pipe_inode_info` structure.**

**Model Answer:**

**Linux Kernel Pipe Implementation:**

In the Linux kernel, a pipe is represented by the structure:

```c
struct pipe_inode_info {
    wait_queue_head_t wait;     // readers waiting
    wait_queue_head_t wr_wait;  // writers waiting
    unsigned int nrbufs;        // current buffer usage
    unsigned int curbuf;        // current head buffer index
    unsigned int buffers;       // total buffer slots (16)
    struct pipe_buffer bufs[16]; // ring of 16 page-sized buffers
};
```

**Diagram — Internal Kernel View:**

```
+----------------------------------------+
| struct pipe_inode_info                 |
|----------------------------------------|
| bufs[0] -> page (4096 B)  head/tail    |
| bufs[1] -> page (4096 B)               |
| bufs[2] -> page (4096 B)               |
|  ...                                   |
| bufs[15] -> page (4096 B)              |
|----------------------------------------|
| wait_queue: blocked READERS            |
| wr_wait_queue: blocked WRITERS         |
+----------------------------------------+
        ^                       ^
        |                       |
   fd[0] read             fd[1] write
   descriptor             descriptor
```

**Role of `pipe_inode_info`:**

1. **Buffer Management** — Maintains 16 page-sized buffers as a ring.
2. **Wait Queue Coordination** — Holds lists of processes blocked on `read()` and `write()` for wakeup when conditions change.
3. **Index Tracking** — `curbuf` and `nrbufs` track current head and number of used slots.
4. **Synchronization** — Uses a per-pipe mutex (`pipe->mutex`) to protect concurrent access in SMP systems.

For RTOS work, this design shows why pipes are **deterministic in behavior**: the bounded buffer size (64 KB) gives an upper bound on data pending, and the kernel's blocking semantics map directly to task state transitions.

*(7 marks: 3 for diagram, 2 for `pipe_inode_info` fields, 2 for RTOS relevance.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing `filedes[0]` and `filedes[1]`** — Many students write the wrong file descriptor for read/write. **Always remember: index 0 = read, index 1 = write.**
> 2. **Forgetting to close unused ends after `fork()`** — This is a **2-mark deduction** in KTU valuation if your code example does not show `close()` calls.
> 3. **Mixing pipes with sockets** — Pipes are local-only; sockets support network. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 4. **Writing > PIPE_BUF without acknowledging atomicity loss** — Always mention the atomic write guarantee.
> 5. **Failing to mention 64 KB buffer size** — This is a frequently asked **fill-in-the-blank** and **1-mark** question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Pipe definition:** Kernel-buffered, unidirectional, FIFO byte-stream IPC channel.
- **`pipe(fd)` syscall:** Creates anonymous pipe; `fd[0]` = read, `fd[1]` = write.
- **Default buffer capacity:** **65536 bytes (64 KB) = 16 × 4096 B pages.**
- **Unidirectional only:** For bidirectional, use **two pipes**.
- **Inheritance:** Both descriptors inherited by child after `fork()`.
- **Close unused ends:** Mandatory to avoid deadlock and to generate EOF.
- **EOF condition:** All write ends closed **AND** buffer empty → `read()` returns `0`.
- **SIGPIPE:** Writing to a pipe with no reader → kernel sends `SIGPIPE` (default action: terminate).
- **PIPE_BUF:** Atomic write limit (≥ 512 B, **4096 B on Linux**); writes ≤ PIPE_BUF are atomic.
- **Non-blocking mode:** `O_NONBLOCK` via `fcntl`; returns `-1` with `errno = EAGAIN`.
- **Named pipe (FIFO):** Created with `mkfifo(path, mode)`; visible as a filesystem special file; allows **unrelated** processes to communicate.
- **Open behavior of FIFO:** `open()` blocks until the other end opens — provides implicit synchronization.
- **Kernel structure:** `struct pipe_inode_info` with 16 page-sized buffers and wait queues.
- **RTOS relevance:** Bounded buffer + blocking semantics = deterministic producer/consumer with bounded latency — directly usable in real-time task design.
- **Comparison with sockets:** Pipes are local-only, lighter weight; sockets support network and bidirectional flow natively.
- **Comparison with message queues:** Pipes are byte-stream; message queues preserve message boundaries.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Event Registers and Signals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT785 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Event Registers and Signals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Event Registers — Formal Definition
An **Event Register** (also called an **Event Flag Group** or **Event Word**) is a kernel-managed, multi-bit data structure (commonly **8-bit, 16-bit, or 32-bit**) used in Real-Time Operating Systems (RTOS) to synchronize multiple tasks or Interrupt Service Routines (ISRs). Each bit within the register represents a discrete **event flag**, and tasks can set, clear, wait for, or query these bits using dedicated kernel primitives such as `OSFlagPost()`, `OSFlagPend()`, and `OSFlagAccept()` (uC/OS-II nomenclature).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Standard:** An Event Register is a synchronization object in which **multiple binary conditions (flags)** are grouped into a single kernel-controlled word, allowing a task to wait on the logical AND/OR combination of several events simultaneously.

### 1.2 Signals — Formal Definition
A **Signal** in an RTOS is a **software interrupt** mechanism (analogous to hardware interrupts) used to deliver asynchronous notifications from an ISR or another task to a specific task. Upon receipt, the task's normal execution flow is suspended, the context is saved, and a pre-registered **Signal Handler / ISR Routine** is invoked. Each signal is identified by a unique integer value (e.g., **1 to 32** in uC/OS-II; **1 to 64** in POSIX real-time extensions).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Signals vs. Event Registers — Key Distinction:**
> - **Event Register** = Synchronous, multi-bit, AND/OR-waitable.
> - **Signal** = Asynchronous, single-event, handler-based notification.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
**Imagine a hospital ICU waiting lounge:**
- **Event Register** is like a **digital status board** with 16 small LED bulbs. Each bulb represents a medical test result (e.g., "Blood Report Ready", "X-Ray Done"). The doctor's task only **proceeds** when the desired combination of bulbs is lit (e.g., ALL three must glow → AND condition) — this is **synchronous polling**.
- **Signal** is like the **emergency beeper** a nurse presses when a critical patient arrives. The doctor doesn't keep watching; he is **interrupted immediately** by the beeper, leaves whatever he is doing, attends the emergency, and returns — this is **asynchronous notification**.

### 1.4 Key Terminology
| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **Flag Bit** | Single binary bit in the event register. |
| **Flag Group** | Collection of related flag bits treated as one unit. |
| **Pend** | Wait (block) for one or more flags to be set. |
| **Post** | Set or clear flags from ISR/task. |
| **Signal Handler** | Function executed in response to a signal. |
| **Signal Mask** | Bit-mask preventing selected signals from delivery. |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Footprint Standard:** Event flags are typically declared as `OS_FLAGS` which is a `INT16U` or `INT32U` depending on the RTOS configuration (uC/OS-II/III convention). The kernel maintains the group as part of its **OS_FLAG_GRP** control block.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Event Register — Operational Logic (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1 — Creation:** The system designer declares a flag group using `OSFlagCreate()`. The kernel allocates an `OS_FLAG_GRP` structure, initializes all bits to 0, and stores the group's address in the Event Flag Group Table.

**Step 2 — Posting (Setting Bits):** A producer task or ISR calls `OSFlagPost(grp, bits, opt)` where:
- `bits` = bit-mask of the flags to be modified.
- `opt` = `OS_FLAG_SET` or `OS_FLAG_CLR`.

**Step 3 — Pending (Waiting):** A consumer task calls `OSFlagPend(grp, bits, wait_type, timeout, perr)`. The wait_type determines the synchronization condition:

> **Why AND/OR?** A task may need ALL events to occur (e.g., "start motor ONLY if both safety-door-closed AND pressure-OK") or ANY event to occur (e.g., "wake up if EITHER over-temperature OR over-current"). The kernel evaluates the pending condition against the current flag state in a single atomic operation.

**Step 4 — Resolution:** If the condition is satisfied, the task is moved from the **wait list** to the **ready list** and the flags are optionally consumed (cleared). If not satisfied, the task is blocked in the flag group's wait list with a timeout.

**Step 5 — Cleanup:** On `OSFlagDel()`, the kernel removes the group, wakes all waiting tasks with error code `OS_ERR_DEL`, and frees the ECB.

### 2.2 Signal — Operational Logic (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1 — Registration:** A task calls `signal(SIGTYPE, handler)` to associate a C function with a specific signal number. The kernel stores this in the task's **signal handler table**.

**Step 2 — Generation:** An ISR or another task issues `kill(taskID, signalNumber)` or `OSSemPost()`-equivalent signal post. The signal is queued in the target task's **signal pending bitmask**.

**Step 3 — Delivery:** The signal is delivered at the next **scheduling point** of the target task (i.e., when the task is about to resume execution after a context switch or system call return). The kernel saves the task's CPU context, pushes the handler's address, and dispatches the handler.

**Step 4 — Handler Execution:** The handler runs in the **context of the receiving task** (not in ISR context). It may perform minimal work and return.

**Step 5 — Return / Re-execution:** Upon return, the kernel restores the original context and the task continues from where it was interrupted.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Real-Time Constraint:** Signal handlers must be **short, bounded, and non-blocking** to preserve determinism. Long operations should defer work to a task via a semaphore or message queue.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Symbol | Description | Unit |
|---------|------------------|-------------|------|
| Event Register Size | $N_{bits} = \log_2(M)$ | M = number of distinct events | bits |
| AND-Wait Condition | $\bigwedge_{i \in S} F_i = 1$ | All flags in set S must be set | Boolean |
| OR-Wait Condition | $\bigvee_{i \in S} F_i = 1$ | Any flag in set S must be set | Boolean |
| Flag Mask | $M_k = 1 \ll k$ | Bit-mask for flag k | — |
| Wait Time Bound | $T_{wait} \leq D - C$ | Deadline − Computation time | ms |
| Signal Latency | $L_s = T_{interrupt} + T_{context\_save} + T_{dispatch}$ | Time from signal post to handler start | µs |
| Context Save Size | $S_{ctx} = R \cdot W$ | R = registers, W = word size | bytes |
| Handler Worst-Case Time | $T_{handler} \leq T_{deadline} - T_{overhead}$ | Bounded execution | µs |
| Number of Signals | $N_{sig} \in [1, 32]$ | uC/OS-II standard range | integer |
| Pending Signals | $P = \sum_{i=1}^{N_{sig}} s_i$ | Sum of pending signal bits | integer |

> [!WARNING]
> **Note on table syntax:** The `\bigwedge`, `\bigvee`, and `\sum` operators are mathematical symbols used to avoid breaking markdown tables. Do not use raw `\wedge` inside a row.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Automotive ECUs:** Event registers coordinate sensor readiness (e.g., ABS uses OR-wait for any wheel-slip flag; airbag uses AND-wait for crash-detected AND seatbelt-buckled).
- **Industrial PLCs:** Signals deliver fault notifications (e.g., SIG_OVERHEAT, SIG_ESTOP).
- **Avionics (DO-178C):** Bounded signal handlers ensure deterministic response to flight-critical events.
- **Medical Devices:** Event flags synchronize multi-sensor fusion (e.g., ECG + SpO2 + blood pressure) before triggering an alarm.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Event Register vs. Signals vs. Semaphores

| Parameter | Event Register | Signal | Semaphore |
|-----------|----------------|--------|-----------|
| **Granularity** | Multi-bit (1–32 flags) | Single event | Binary/Counting |
| **Synchronization Mode** | AND, OR, Mask-wait | One-shot asynchronous | Single resource lock |
| **Producer Count** | Multiple (tasks + ISRs) | Multiple (tasks + ISRs) | Multiple |
| **Consumer Count** | Multiple | One task per signal | One task per take |
| **Storage of State** | Persistent flag bits | Consumed on delivery | Counter value |
| **Blocking Type** | Pend with timeout | Non-blocking (queued) | Pend with timeout |
| **ISR Safe?** | Yes (Post only) | Yes (Post only) | Yes (Post only) |
| **Use Case** | Multi-condition sync | Exception/Alarm | Mutual exclusion |

### 3.2 Mathematical Derivation — Wait Condition Satisfaction

**Given:** A task is waiting on event register group $G$ with mask $M$ and wait type $W \in \{AND, OR\}$.

**Current register state:** $R = (b_{N-1}, b_{N-2}, \ldots, b_0)$ where $b_i \in \{0,1\}$.

**To determine** if the task should be unblocked:

$$
\text{AND Condition:} \quad \text{result} = (R \;\&\; M) \;==\; M
$$

**Step-by-step expansion for a 4-bit register example:**
Suppose $R = 1011_2$ and $M = 1001_2$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R \;\&\; M &= (1011)_2 \;\&\; (1001)_2 \\
&= (1\cdot 1, \;0\cdot 0, \;1\cdot 0, \;1\cdot 1)_2 \\
&= (1, 0, 0, 1)_2 \\
&= 1001_2
\end{aligned}
$$

Now check equality:

$$
(R \;\&\; M) \;==\; M \;\Longrightarrow\; 1001_2 \;==\; 1001_2 \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{TRUE}
$$

**Therefore**, if wait type is AND, the task is unblocked. The flags are optionally consumed by clearing them.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{new} &= R \;\&\; \sim M \\
&= 1011_2 \;\&\; \sim(1001_2) \\
&= 1011_2 \;\&\; (0110_2) \\
&= 0010_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**OR Condition derivation:**

$$
\text{OR Condition:} \quad \text{result} = (R \;\&\; M) \;\neq\; 0
$$

Using the same example:

$$
R \;\&\; M = 1001_2 \;\neq\; 0000_2 \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{TRUE}
$$

So the task is unblocked in OR mode as well (since at least one bit matches).

### 3.3 Signal Latency Derivation

**Given** a periodic real-time task with deadline $D_i$ and computation time $C_i$. A signal is posted by an ISR at time $t_0$.

$$
L_s = t_{handler\_start} - t_0
$$

Breaking down the components:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_s &= T_{interrupt} + T_{context\_save} + T_{dispatch} + T_{queue} \\
T_{interrupt} &\leq T_{arch} \quad \text{(architecture-dependent)} \\
T_{context\_save} &= \frac{N_{regs} \cdot W_{reg}}{f_{CPU}} \quad \text{(register save time)} \\
T_{dispatch} &= \text{constant kernel overhead} \\
T_{queue} &= 0 \quad \text{(signals are delivered at scheduling points)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Example (ARM Cortex-M4 @ 168 MHz, 16 registers, 32-bit):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{context\_save} &= \frac{16 \cdot 4 \text{ bytes}}{168 \times 10^6 \text{ bytes/s}} \\
&= \frac{64}{168 \times 10^6} \text{ s} \\
&= 0.381 \, \mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

Adding typical overheads $T_{interrupt} = 12$ cycles and $T_{dispatch} = 20$ cycles:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_s &= 0.381 + \frac{12 + 20}{168 \times 10^6} \times 10^6 \\
&= 0.381 + 0.190 \\
&\approx 0.571 \, \mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

This is well within typical hard-real-time deadlines of **1 ms** for control loops.

### 3.4 Bit-Mask Construction for Multi-Flag AND-Wait

Suppose we need to wait for flags at bit positions $k_1, k_2, \ldots, k_n$ simultaneously. The required mask $M$ is:

$$
M = \sum_{j=1}^{n} 2^{k_j} = 2^{k_1} + 2^{k_2} + \ldots + 2^{k_n}
$$

**Example:** Wait for bits 0, 3, and 5.

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= 2^0 + 2^3 + 2^5 \\
&= 1 + 8 + 32 \\
&= 41_{10} = 0x29 = (0010\,1001)_2
\end{aligned}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Event Register — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ISR1[ISR Timer Tick]:::isr -->|OSFlagPost bit0| REG[Event Register 16-bit]
    ISR2[ISR Sensor Ready]:::isr -->|OSFlagPost bit3| REG
    TASK1[Producer Task A]:::task -->|OSFlagPost bit5| REG
    REG -->|AND-wait satisfied| WAITQ[Wait List - Tasks Blocked]
    WAITQ -->|Unblock to Ready| READY[Ready Queue]
    READY -->|Scheduler picks| CPU[CPU Execution]
    REG -.->|OSFlagQuery| MON[Monitor Task]

    classDef isr fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#c0392b,color:#000
    classDef task fill:#d6e9ff,stroke:#2c3e50,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Signal Generation and Delivery Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Producer Task/ISR] -->|kill taskID, signalNum| B[Kernel Signal Manager]
    B -->|Set bit in pending mask| C[Target Task Pending Mask]
    C -->|At next scheduling point| D{Scheduler Dispatch}
    D -->|Save context| E[Stack Push CPU State]
    E -->|Lookup handler table| F[Signal Handler Table]
    F -->|Call handler| G[Execute Handler]
    G -->|Return| H[Restore CPU State]
    H -->|Resume original task| I[Task Continues]

    classDef critical fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
    class G critical
```

### 4.3 Event Register Internal Structure (Bit-Level View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph REG16[16-bit Event Register - 0x00A5]
        B15[Bit 15: Reserved]:::reserved
        B14[Bit 14: Reserved]:::reserved
        B13[Bit 13: Power OK]:::set
        B12[Bit 12: Motor Ready]:::set
        B11[Bit 11: Reserved]:::reserved
        B10[Bit 10: Sensor 3 OK]:::reserved
        B9[Bit 9: Sensor 2 OK]:::reserved
        B8[Bit 8: Sensor 1 OK]:::reserved
        B7[Bit 7: CAN Msg]:::reserved
        B6[Bit 6: DMA Done]:::reserved
        B5[Bit 5: UART RX]:::set
        B4[Bit 4: SPI TX Complete]:::reserved
        B3[Bit 3: Timer Expired]:::reserved
        B2[Bit 2: ADC Converted]:::reserved
        B1[Bit 1: User Button]:::reserved
        B0[Bit 0: System Tick]:::set
    end

    B0 --> HEX0[0x0021]
    B5 --> HEX1[0x0020]
    B12 --> HEX2[0x1000]
    B13 --> HEX3[0x2000]

    classDef set fill:#2ecc71,stroke:#1e8449,color:#fff
    classDef reserved fill:#ecf0f1,stroke:#7f8c8d,color:#000
```

### 4.4 Task Signal Mask Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TCB[Task Control Block - TCB]
        SIGMASK[Signal Mask: 0xFFFF FFFF]:::mask
        SIGHAND[Signal Handlers: 32 entries]:::handler
        SIGPEND[Pending Signals: 0x0000 0005]:::pending
    end

    SIGMASK -.->|Blocks delivery| SIGHAND
    SIGPEND -->|Unmasked bit set| SIGHAND
    SIGHAND -->|Dispatch| EXEC[Execute Handler]

    classDef mask fill:#d6e9ff,stroke:#2c3e50,color:#000
    classDef handler fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
    classDef pending fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#c0392b,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define an Event Register. How is it different from a binary semaphore?**

**Model Answer:**
An **Event Register** (or Event Flag Group) is a kernel-managed data structure consisting of multiple binary flag bits (typically 8/16/32 bits) used to synchronize tasks on multiple conditions simultaneously.

| Feature | Event Register | Binary Semaphore |
|---------|----------------|------------------|
| Granularity | Multi-bit (multiple conditions) | Single bit (locked/unlocked) |
| Wait Modes | AND, OR, Mask-wait | Only "available" (count = 1) |
| Information | Multiple event states encoded | Only resource availability |
| State Persistence | Flags persist after consumption | Consumed on `take()` |

(3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] What is a Signal in an RTOS? List any two applications of signals in embedded systems.**

**Model Answer:**
A **Signal** is a software-interrupt mechanism in an RTOS used to deliver asynchronous notifications from an ISR or another task to a specific task. Each signal has a unique identifier and triggers a pre-registered signal handler.

**Two Applications:**
1. **Alarm/Exception Notification:** An over-temperature ISR sends `SIG_OVERTEMP` to the control task to initiate shutdown.
2. **Asynchronous I/O Completion:** A DMA controller posts a signal to a task when a data block transfer completes, allowing the task to be awakened without polling.

(3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Event Registers Focus**

**Q.A(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the structure of an Event Register in an RTOS. With a neat diagram, describe the AND-wait and OR-wait synchronization mechanisms. Provide one real-time example for each.

**Model Answer:**

**Event Register Structure:**
An Event Register is a group of N binary flags stored as bits in a single kernel-managed word (commonly `OS_FLAGS` of type `INT16U` or `INT32U`). Each bit position $i$ represents one event. A task can pend on the register with a **mask** $M$ specifying which bits are of interest, and a **wait type** specifying AND or OR logic.

**Internal Layout (16-bit example):**

| Bit 15 | Bit 14 | … | Bit 5 | Bit 4 | Bit 3 | Bit 2 | Bit 1 | Bit 0 |
|--------|--------|---|-------|-------|-------|-------|-------|-------|
| Event H | Event G | … | Event F | Event E | Event D | Event C | Event B | Event A |

**AND-Wait Mechanism:**
A task unblocks **only when ALL the bits specified in the mask are set to 1**.

$$
\text{Unblock if} \quad (R \;\&\; M) \;==\; M
$$

**OR-Wait Mechanism:**
A task unblocks **when AT LEAST ONE of the masked bits is set to 1**.

$$
\text{Unblock if} \quad (R \;\&\; M) \;\neq\; 0
$$

**Real-Time Examples:**

- **AND-Wait — Airbag Deployment:** The airbag ECU waits for `Crash_Detected AND Seatbelt_Buckled AND Passenger_Present`. Only when all three flags are set will the deploy task run.
- **OR-Wait — Multi-Sensor Alarm:** A fire alarm task wakes if `Smoke_Detected OR Heat_Exceeded OR Gas_Leak`. Any one flag suffices.

**Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the functional block diagram.

(7 marks)

---

**Q.A(b) [7 Marks]** Write the step-by-step algorithm for the `OSFlagPost()` and `OSFlagPend()` kernel primitives. Show how priority inversion is avoided in flag-based synchronization.

**Model Answer:**

**`OSFlagPost(grp, flags, opt, perr)` Algorithm:**

1. Disable interrupts (or use a mutex lock).
2. If `opt == OS_FLAG_SET`, compute `grp->OSFlagFlags |= flags`.
3. If `opt == OS_FLAG_CLR`, compute `grp->OSFlagFlags &= ~flags`.
4. Scan `grp->OSFlagWaitList` for all tasks waiting on this group.
5. For each waiting task, evaluate its `(flags_wanted, wait_type)` against the new `OSFlagFlags`.
6. If condition satisfied:
    - Remove task from the wait list.
    - Optionally clear the consumed flags.
    - Place task in the ready list (priority-based).
7. Re-enable interrupts.
8. Call the scheduler if preemption is enabled.
9. Return `perr`.

**`OSFlagPend(grp, flags, wait_type, timeout, perr, flags_consumed)` Algorithm:**

1. If `(grp->OSFlagFlags & flags) satisfies wait_type` immediately, return with current flags consumed.
2. Else, place current task in the flag group's wait list with the requested mask and wait type.
3. Set task state to `OS_STAT_PEND` with timeout value.
4. Invoke the scheduler (current task is blocked).
5. Upon wake-up (post or timeout), check `perr`:
    - If `OS_ERR_NONE`: continue with flags consumed.
    - If `OS_ERR_TIMEOUT`: handle timeout case.

**Avoiding Priority Inversion:**
Event registers do not inherently cause **unbounded** priority inversion (unlike mutexes) because they are not owned by a task — flags can be set by any task/ISR. However, to avoid **bounded** priority inversion, the kernel:
- Uses **disabling of interrupts** (not spin-locks) during the post operation, ensuring bounded critical-section time.
- Employs **priority inheritance** when the flag group is used in conjunction with mutex-protected resources.
- Ensures that the post operation has a deterministic $O(N)$ scan time over the wait list, where N is bounded by the number of tasks.

(7 marks)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Signals Focus**

**Q.B(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the Signal mechanism in an RTOS. Describe the signal lifecycle with a state diagram. Differentiate between synchronous and asynchronous signal delivery.

**Model Answer:**

**Signal Definition:**
A signal is a kernel-managed asynchronous notification mechanism characterized by a small integer identifier (signal number) and an associated handler function. When a signal is delivered to a task, the kernel interrupts the task, saves its context, executes the handler, and restores the task to continue from the interrupted point.

**Signal Lifecycle (State Diagram):**

1. **IDLE State:** Signal not generated, not pending.
2. **GENERATED State:** ISR/task calls signal post. Kernel sets the corresponding bit in the target task's pending signal mask.
3. **PENDING State:** Signal bit is set in `TCB->SigPend`. Signal awaits delivery.
4. **DELIVERED State:** At the target task's next scheduling point, the kernel dispatches the handler.
5. **HANDLED State:** Handler executes and returns. Signal bit is cleared from `TCB->SigPend`.

**Synchronous vs. Asynchronous Signals:**

| Aspect | Synchronous Signal | Asynchronous Signal |
|--------|--------------------|---------------------|
| Delivery Time | Immediately after a system call completes | At the next scheduling point, regardless of current state |
| Predictability | High | Lower (jitter possible) |
| Use Case | Software exceptions (e.g., divide-by-zero) | External events (e.g., ISR-posted alarm) |
| Example in uC/OS-II | Task-level error trap | ISR `OSTaskSignalPost()` |

**Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.2 for the signal flow diagram.

(7 marks)

---

**Q.B(b) [7 Marks]** Write a comparative study of Event Registers, Signals, and Semaphores as RTOS synchronization primitives. Justify which is most suitable for a multi-sensor data-fusion application in an industrial controller.

**Model Answer:**

**Comparative Study:**

| Parameter | Event Register | Signal | Semaphore |
|-----------|----------------|--------|-----------|
| Synchronization Type | Multi-condition (AND/OR) | One-shot notification | Resource lock/count |
| Data Encoded | Multiple binary states | Single event type | Counter (0/1 or N) |
| Producer Flexibility | Multiple tasks/ISRs | Multiple tasks/ISRs | Multiple posters |
| State After Consumption | Persistent or consumed | Consumed automatically | Decremented |
| Suitable For | Multi-sensor readiness | Asynchronous alarms | Mutual exclusion |

**Justification for Multi-Sensor Data-Fusion:**

In an industrial controller, the data-fusion task must wait for data from **multiple sensors** (e.g., temperature, pressure, flow, vibration) before computing the control output. This requires waiting on **all sensors to be ready (AND-wait)** and also handling **alarm conditions from any sensor (OR-wait)**.

**Event Registers are most suitable** because:
1. **AND-Wait** allows the fusion task to pend until ALL sensors have provided fresh data, eliminating the need for multiple semaphores.
2. **OR-Wait** allows alarm tasks to wake on ANY abnormal sensor reading.
3. The **persistent flag bits** allow tracking of which sensor is still pending.
4. **Low overhead** compared to message queues for simple status signaling.
5. **ISR-safe posting** allows direct sensor ISRs to set flags without complex post operations.

Signals would be unsuitable because they are one-shot and cannot express multi-condition AND logic. Semaphores would require N semaphores for N sensors, increasing kernel memory and complexity.

(7 marks)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deduction Points in KTU 2024 Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing Event Register with Semaphore:** Event registers are for **multi-condition sync**, not resource locking. Writing semaphore code for an event register question loses 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting AND vs. OR distinction:** Marks are reserved for clearly stating the boolean condition with the correct operator.
> 3. **Signal handler design violations:** Examiners deduct marks if you suggest long blocking operations inside a signal handler. Always state: *handler must be short and non-blocking*.
> 4. **Missing ISR-safety argument:** When asked about posting from ISRs, you MUST mention **critical section / interrupt disable** for atomicity.
> 5. **No diagram in 7-mark sub-questions:** KTU 2024 valuation key awards **1 mark** for a labeled diagram in long-answer questions. Always include one.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Event Register** = multi-bit kernel object for multi-condition synchronization.
- [x] **AND-wait:** $(R \;\&\; M) == M$ → unblock only when all masked bits are set.
- [x] **OR-wait:** $(R \;\&\; M) \neq 0$ → unblock when at least one masked bit is set.
- [x] **Mask formula:** $M = \sum 2^{k_j}$ over bits of interest.
- [x] **Signal** = asynchronous software interrupt with unique ID and handler.
- [x] **Signal delivery** happens at the target task's next scheduling point.
- [x] **Signal handlers** must be short, bounded, and non-blocking.
- [x] **Event registers are ISR-safe for posting only**; pends from ISRs are not allowed.
- [x] **Event Register size** typically 8/16/32 bits (declared as `OS_FLAGS`).
- [x] **Flag persistence:** Flags can optionally be cleared on consumption (`OS_FLAG_CONSUME` option).
- [x] **uC/OS-II signal range:** Signal numbers 1 to 32; signal 0 is reserved.
- [x] **Priority inversion:** Bounded in event registers (interrupt-disable atomicity); unbounded in mutexes (use priority inheritance).
- [x] **Context save time:** $T_{ctx} = N_{regs} \cdot W_{reg} / f_{CPU}$ — keep small for low latency.
- [x] **Real-time use cases:** AND-wait for safety-critical synchronization, OR-wait for alarm aggregation, signals for fault notification.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Real-Time Task Scheduling and Constraints
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT785 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Real-Time Task Scheduling and Constraints

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Real-Time Task Scheduling

**Real-Time Task Scheduling** is the process of determining the execution order of computational tasks on a processor such that all temporal (timing) constraints — namely **deadlines**, **release times**, and **periods** — are satisfied to a degree mandated by the system's criticality class. In the KTU 2024 Scheme, this is formally positioned as a **constrained optimization problem** under the framework of *temporal correctness*, where the scheduler's job is to construct a valid **schedule** — a mapping $\sigma : \mathbb{R}^+ \to \mathcal{T}$ from time to the set of tasks $\mathcal{T}$ — that respects all task constraints.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Real-time scheduling is distinct from throughput-oriented OS scheduling. The **metric of success is not average performance, but the worst-case guarantee of deadline satisfaction**. A schedule that is statistically 99% correct is fundamentally useless for a hard real-time system.

## 1.2 Classification of Real-Time Tasks (Constraint Taxonomy)

Real-time tasks are classified based on the **consequence of a missed deadline**:

| Task Class | Deadline Consequence | Tolerance | KTU Example System |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hard Real-Time** | Catastrophic / System failure | **Zero tolerance** (absolute) | Anti-lock Braking System (ABS), Pacemaker, Flight Control |
| **Firm Real-Time** | Degraded result, but no system harm | No value after deadline | Financial Trading, Weather Data Sampling |
| **Soft Real-Time** | Performance degrades gradually | Acceptable occasional miss | Audio/Video Streaming, UI Animations |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mathematical Form:** For a hard real-time task $\tau_i$, the constraint $d_i \le D_i^{max}$ is non-negotiable. For a soft task, an *expectation* $\mathbb{E}[d_i - D_i] \ge 0$ is sufficient.

## 1.3 Task Parameters — The J/C/D/T Model

Every real-time task $\tau_i$ is characterized by four fundamental temporal parameters:

- **Release Time ($r_i$):** The instant at which the task becomes available for execution.
- **Computation Time ($C_i$):** Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET) on the given hardware. Symbolically $C_i = \max_{k}\{c_i^{(k)}\}$.
- **Deadline ($d_i$ or $D_i$):** The instant by which execution must complete.
- **Period ($T_i$):** For periodic tasks, the inter-arrival time between successive activations.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The Air Traffic Control Tower

Imagine an **Air Traffic Control Tower (ATC)** managing landings at a busy airport:

- **Tasks** = Aircraft awaiting landing clearance.
- **Constraints** = Fuel levels (deadlines), runway availability (resources), and scheduled time slots (release times).
- **Hard Real-Time Rule:** A plane with critically low fuel **must** land immediately — failure is catastrophic (crash).
- **Firm Real-Time Rule:** A cargo plane past its slot loses priority — late landing is wasteful but harmless.
- **Soft Real-Time Rule:** A small private jet can be slightly delayed — passengers will be annoyed, not endangered.
- **Scheduler** = The ATC officer who must sequence landings under all these constraints simultaneously.

> [!NOTE]
> This analogy directly maps to **Fixed Priority Preemptive Scheduling (FPS)**. The ATC officer assigns priorities (just as Rate Monotonic assigns them) and preempts lower-priority operations when emergencies arrive.

## 1.5 Types of Deadlines — Constraint Geometry

The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically requires understanding of deadline relationships:

$$\boxed{\text{Deadline types defined relative to Period } T_i \text{ and Release Time } r_i}$$

1. **Relative Deadline ($D_i$):** A time window measured from release. Absolute deadline: $d_i = r_i + D_i$.
2. **Implicit Deadline:** $D_i = T_i$ — deadline equals period.
3. **Constrained Deadline:** $D_i \le T_i$ — deadline may be earlier than the next period.
4. **Arbitrary Deadline:** $D_i$ can be any value $\ge T_i$ (more general, harder to schedule).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Scheduling Algorithm Classification

The scheduler is governed by two orthogonal design decisions:

**(a) Static vs Dynamic Priority**
- **Static (Fixed) Priority:** Priority assigned *a priori* and never changes. Example: **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)**.
- **Dynamic Priority:** Priority may change at runtime. Example: **Earliest Deadline First (EDF)**.

**(b) Preemptive vs Non-Preemptive**
- **Preemptive:** A higher-priority task can immediately interrupt a running lower-priority task.
- **Non-Preemptive:** Once a task starts, it runs to completion. Simpler, but suffers from **priority inversion** blocks.

## 2.2 Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) — The Foundational Algorithm

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Liu & Layland (1973) Result:** RMS is the **optimal static-priority algorithm** for preemptive, independent, periodic tasks with implicit deadlines. Optimality means: *if any static-priority algorithm can schedule a task set, RMS can also schedule it.*

### RMS Assumptions (Liu & Layland Model)
1. All tasks are **periodic** with period $T_i$.
2. Tasks are **independent** (no shared resources, no precedence).
3. $D_i = T_i$ (implicit deadline).
4. $C_i$ is **constant and known** (WCET).
5. Preemptive scheduling is permitted.
6. Context switch overhead is negligible.

### RMS Priority Assignment
**Shorter period = Higher priority.** Mathematically:

$$P(\tau_i) > P(\tau_j) \iff T_i < T_j$$

### Utilization Bound Theorem (RMS Feasibility Sufficient Test)

For a task set $\Gamma = \{\tau_1, \tau_2, \ldots, \tau_n\}$ sorted by increasing period, a **sufficient** (but not necessary) feasibility condition is:

$$\boxed{U(\Gamma) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \le n\left(2^{1/n} - 1\right) = U_{RMS}(n)}$$

| $n$ (tasks) | $U_{RMS}(n)$ Bound |
|---|---|
| 1 | 1.000 (100.0%) |
| 2 | 0.828 (82.8%) |
| 3 | 0.780 (78.0%) |
| 4 | 0.757 (75.7%) |
| 5 | 0.743 (74.3%) |
| $\infty$ | $\ln(2) \approx 0.693$ (69.3%) |

> [!NOTE]
> A utilization *above* the bound does **not** mean infeasibility — only that the test is inconclusive. A **time-demand analysis** (response time test) is required for exact feasibility.

## 2.3 Earliest Deadline First (EDF) — Optimal Dynamic Algorithm

EDF assigns the **highest priority to the task with the earliest absolute deadline** at every scheduling decision point.

$$\boxed{\text{EDF is optimal for preemptive, independent tasks: } U(\Gamma) \le 1.0 \text{ is both necessary and sufficient.}}$$

**Implication:** EDF can achieve 100% processor utilization theoretically, but suffers from higher runtime overhead (constant priority recalculation) and is harder to implement in commercial kernels.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Quick Reference)

| Formula | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|
| $U = \sum \frac{C_i}{T_i}$ | Processor Utilization | Total CPU fraction consumed |
| $U_{RMS}(n) = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ | RMS Utilization Bound | Sufficient test for RMS feasibility |
| $U_{EDF} \le 1$ | EDF Feasibility | Necessary and sufficient |
| $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$ | Response Time Equation | Exact RMS feasibility test |
| $D_i = T_i$ | Implicit Deadline | Standard RMS model |
| $f = 1 - U$ | Processor Slack (Free CPU) | Scheduling headroom metric |
| $M_i = \lfloor T_i / T_{i-1} \rfloor$ | Job count in hyperperiod | Hyperperiod analysis |
| $H = \text{lcm}(T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_n)$ | Hyperperiod | Total schedule repetition interval |

> [!WARNING]
> In the KTU exam, do not confuse the **utilization bound** (sufficient) with the **response time equation** (exact necessary and sufficient). The bound may give false negatives (rejecting feasible sets).

## 2.5 Response Time Analysis (Exact Feasibility Test)

For a task $\tau_i$ under fixed-priority preemptive scheduling, the **worst-case response time** $R_i$ is the smallest positive solution of:

$$\boxed{R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j}$$

Where $hp(i)$ is the set of tasks with priority higher than $\tau_i$. The task set is feasible iff $R_i \le D_i$ for every $\tau_i$.

This equation is solved by **fixed-point iteration**:

$$R_i^{(0)} = C_i, \quad R_i^{(k+1)} = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i^{(k)}}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$$

The iteration terminates when $R_i^{(k+1)} = R_i^{(k)}$ (fixed point found) or when $R_i^{(k)} > D_i$ (infeasible).

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive (AUTOSAR):** RMS-derived scheduling used in OSEK/VDX operating systems for engine control units.
- **Aerospace (ARINC 653):** Time-partitioned scheduling ensures strict isolation between avionics applications.
- **Industrial Robotics:** EDF variants (e.g., sporadic servers) handle mixed hard/soft workloads.
- **Telecommunications (5G NR):** Hybrid scheduling in baseband processing uses priority ceilings derived from RMS.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: RMS Utilization Bound (Liu & Layland)

**Goal:** Derive $U_{RMS}(n) = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$.

**Step 1 — The Critical Instant Concept**
The worst-case response time of a task under fixed-priority scheduling occurs at the **critical instant**, when the task is released simultaneously with all higher-priority tasks. Mathematically, the worst-case interference from a higher-priority task $\tau_j$ in any interval of length $t$ is:

$$I_j(t) = \left\lceil \frac{t}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$$

**Step 2 — Worst-Case Response Time Formulation**
The total interference suffered by $\tau_i$ in an interval of length $t$ is:

$$W_i(t) = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{t}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$$

The worst-case response time $R_i$ is the smallest $t$ such that $W_i(t) \le t$ has no solution before $D_i$.

**Step 3 — Utilization Derivation via Period Ordering**
For a task set with utilization $U$ and the worst case, assign $T_1 \le T_2 \le \ldots \le T_n$. The critical constraint is:

$$C_i \le T_i - \sum_{j=1}^{i-1} C_j$$

Summing the interference bound and applying the harmonic period analysis (substituting $T_i = 2^{(n-i)/n} \cdot T_n$ for the boundary case), the Liu-Layland bound emerges:

$$U_{RMS}(n) = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$$

**Step 4 — Asymptotic Behavior**

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} n(2^{1/n} - 1) = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{2^{1/n} - 1}{1/n} = \ln(2) \approx 0.6931$$

by applying L'Hôpital's rule to the form $\frac{0}{0}$, where $\frac{d}{dn}(2^{1/n} - 1) = -\frac{\ln 2}{n^2} 2^{1/n}$.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem: RMS Feasibility Test

> **Problem (KTU Pattern):** Given task set:
> $\tau_1: C_1 = 1, T_1 = 4$
> $\tau_2: C_2 = 2, T_2 = 6$
> $\tau_3: C_3 = 3, T_3 = 10$
> Test feasibility using (a) utilization bound, (b) response time analysis.

### Part (a): Utilization Bound Test

$$U = \frac{1}{4} + \frac{2}{6} + \frac{3}{10}$$

$$= 0.250 + 0.333 + 0.300 = 0.883$$

$$U_{RMS}(3) = 3(2^{1/3} - 1) = 3(1.2599 - 1) = 0.7798$$

Since $U = 0.883 > U_{RMS}(3) = 0.780$, the **bound test is inconclusive** — we cannot reject nor confirm feasibility.

### Part (b): Response Time Analysis

**Task $\tau_1$ (highest priority, no higher-priority tasks):**

$$R_1 = C_1 = 1 \le D_1 = T_1 = 4 \quad \checkmark$$

**Task $\tau_2$ (interference from $\tau_1$):**

$$R_2^{(0)} = C_2 = 2$$

$$R_2^{(1)} = 2 + \left\lceil \frac{2}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 2 + 1 = 3$$

$$R_2^{(2)} = 2 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 2 + 1 = 3 \quad \text{(Fixed point)}$$

$R_2 = 3 \le D_2 = 6 \quad \checkmark$

**Task $\tau_3$ (interference from $\tau_1, \tau_2$):**

$$R_3^{(0)} = C_3 = 3$$

$$R_3^{(1)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 1 + 2 = 6$$

$$R_3^{(2)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 2 + 2 = 7$$

$$R_3^{(3)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{7}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{7}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 2 + 4 = 9$$

$$R_3^{(4)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{9}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{9}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 3 + 4 = 10$$

$$R_3^{(5)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{10}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{10}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 3 + 4 = 10 \quad \text{(Fixed point)}$$

$R_3 = 10 \le D_3 = 10 \quad \checkmark$ (just meets the deadline)

**Conclusion:** The task set is **feasible** under RMS, demonstrating that the utilization bound is conservative.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: RMS vs EDF

| Parameter | RMS (Fixed Priority) | EDF (Dynamic Priority) |
|---|---|---|
| **Optimality Class** | Optimal static-priority | Optimal (any-priority) |
| **Utilization Bound** | $n(2^{1/n}-1) \to 0.693$ | $1.0$ (full) |
| **Implementation Cost** | Low (priority is constant) | High (priority recomputed) |
| **Runtime Overhead** | $O(1)$ per release | $O(\log n)$ per scheduling point |
| **Transient Overload** | Predictable (low-priority tasks miss first) | Unpredictable (cascading misses) |
| **Hardware Support** | Trivial | Needs high-resolution timer |
| **KTU Use Case** | AUTOSAR, VxWorks defaults | Research, theoretical benchmarks |

## 3.4 Priority Inversion Phenomenon

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Priority Inversion** occurs when a lower-priority task blocks a higher-priority task by holding a shared resource, allowing an intermediate-priority task to preempt the lower-priority one — effectively inverting priorities.

**Standard Solution: Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)**
If task $\tau_L$ blocks $\tau_H$, then $\tau_L$ temporarily inherits $\tau_H$'s priority until it releases the resource.

**Duration Bound under PIP (bounded blocking):**

$$B_i = \max_{k: P(k) < P(i)} \min_{r \in RS(k, i)} (\text{length of critical section})$$

The total response time under PIP becomes:

$$R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j + B_i$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Scheduling Algorithm Taxonomy (Mermaid Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Real-Time Scheduling"] --> B["Priority Assignment Type"]
    A --> C["Preemption Mode"]
    A --> D["Time Trigger Type"]

    B --> B1["Static Priority Fixed"]
    B --> B2["Dynamic Priority Adaptive"]

    B1 --> E1["Rate Monotonic RMS"]
    B1 --> E2["Deadline Monotonic DMS"]
    B1 --> E3["Audsley Algorithm"]

    B2 --> F1["Earliest Deadline First EDF"]
    B2 --> F2["Least Laxity First LLF"]
    B2 --> F3["Maximum Urgency First MUF"]

    C --> C1["Preemptive"]
    C --> C2["Non Preemptive"]
    C --> C3["Cooperative"]

    D --> D1["Clock Driven Table Based"]
    D --> D2["Event Driven Priority Based"]
    D --> D3["Hybrid Round Robin"]

    E1 --> G["Liu Layland Bound 69 percent"]
    F1 --> H["Optimal Bound 100 percent"]
```

## 4.2 Task Lifecycle & Scheduling State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Released : Arrival at r i
    Released --> Ready : Placed in queue
    Ready --> Running : Scheduler dispatch
    Running --> Ready : Preempted by higher priority
    Running --> Blocked : Resource unavailable
    Blocked --> Ready : Resource acquired
    Running --> Completed : Execution finished
    Completed --> [*] : Before deadline hit
    Running --> DeadlineMiss : Time exceeds D i
    DeadlineMiss --> [*] : System failure for hard tasks
```

## 4.3 RMS vs EDF Decision Flow (Feasibility Test)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S["Start Task Set Gamma"] --> U["Compute Utilization U"]
    U --> Q1{"U less than or equal 1.0?"}
    Q1 -- "No" --> INFEAS["Infeasible Reject"]
    Q1 -- "Yes" --> Q2{"Algorithm RMS or EDF?"}
    Q2 -- "RMS" --> Q3{"U less than or equal n(2^1/n - 1)?"}
    Q3 -- "Yes" --> FEAS["Feasible Guaranteed"]
    Q3 -- "No" --> RTA["Apply Response Time Analysis"]
    Q2 -- "EDF" --> FEAS
    RTA --> Q4{"R i less than or equal D i for all i?"}
    Q4 -- "Yes" --> FEAS
    Q4 -- "No" --> INFEAS
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Real-Time Scheduler

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Input["Input Layer"]
        IT1["Task Arrival Queue"]
        IT2["Timer Interrupt Handler"]
        IT3["Resource Manager"]
    end

    subgraph Core["Scheduler Core"]
        SC1["Admission Control Module"]
        SC2["Priority Assignment Unit"]
        SC3["Preemption Logic"]
        SC4["Dispatch Table"]
    end

    subgraph Output["Execution Layer"]
        OUT1["CPU Dispatch"]
        OUT2["Context Save Restore"]
        OUT3["Deadline Watchdog"]
    end

    IT1 --> SC1
    IT2 --> SC2
    IT3 --> SC1
    SC1 --> SC2
    SC2 --> SC3
    SC3 --> SC4
    SC4 --> OUT1
    OUT1 --> OUT2
    OUT2 --> OUT3
    OUT3 --> SC1
```

## 4.5 Timeline Diagram (Gantt-Style Mermaid View)

```mermaid
gantt
    title RMS Schedule Example (3 Tasks, Hyperperiod = 12)
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat %s
    section High Priority Tau1
    Tau1 Job 1   :a1, 0, 1
    Tau1 Job 2   :a2, 4, 1
    Tau1 Job 3   :a3, 8, 1
    section Medium Priority Tau2
    Tau2 Job 1   :b1, 1, 2
    Tau2 Job 2   :b2, 7, 2
    section Low Priority Tau3
    Tau3 Job 1   :c1, 3, 3
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the diagram above, the X-axis represents time units. Note how $\tau_1$ (period 4) preempts $\tau_3$ at $t=4$ and $t=8$, demonstrating the fixed-priority preemptive nature of RMS.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Differentiate between hard real-time and soft real-time systems with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Attribute | Hard Real-Time | Soft Real-Time |
|---|---|---|
| **Deadline Nature** | Absolute; system fails if missed | Soft; degradation acceptable |
| **Consequence** | Catastrophic (loss of life/property) | Reduced QoS or user dissatisfaction |
| **Examples** | Anti-lock Braking System, Pacemaker, Missile Guidance | Video Streaming, Online Gaming, Email |
| **Statistical Guarantee** | Deterministic worst-case | Probabilistic/average-case |
| **Utility Function** | Step (0 or 1) | Linear/Exponential decay |

A hard real-time system **must** produce correct results within deadlines — the system is useless if even one deadline is missed. A soft real-time system aims to meet deadlines but tolerates occasional misses (allocation: **1 mark** definition, **1 mark** example, **1 mark** comparison/contrast).

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**State the Rate Monotonic Scheduling utilization bound theorem. What is its significance?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Rate Monotonic Scheduling utilization bound theorem states that a set of $n$ independent, preemptable periodic tasks scheduled under RMS will **always meet all deadlines** if:

$$U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \le n(2^{1/n} - 1)$$

**Significance:**
- Provides a **simple, O(n) test** for offline schedulability analysis (1 mark).
- As $n \to \infty$, the bound approaches $\ln 2 \approx 0.693$, meaning RMS can guarantee feasibility up to ~69.3% utilization in the worst case (1 mark).
- Sufficient but not necessary — a task set with higher utilization may still be feasible (verified via response time analysis) (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Explain the priority inheritance protocol. How does it solve the priority inversion problem? Provide the bounded blocking time formula.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** The Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP), proposed by Sha, Rajkumar, and Lehoczky, is a resource access control protocol for real-time systems. When a higher-priority task $\tau_H$ attempts to acquire a resource held by a lower-priority task $\tau_L$, the protocol **dynamically raises** $\tau_L$'s priority to that of $\tau_H$ (and to the highest priority of any task blocked by $\tau_L$). The inheritance is transitive.

**Mechanism (2 marks):**
- $\tau_L$ inherits $\tau_H$'s priority only while holding the resource.
- Upon releasing the resource, $\tau_L$ reverts to its original priority.
- This prevents intermediate-priority tasks from preempting $\tau_L$ and indirectly blocking $\tau_H$.

**Bounded Blocking Formula (2 marks):**
For a task $\tau_i$ under PIP, the worst-case blocking time is:

$$B_i = \max_{k: P(k) < P(i)} \min_{r \in RS(k, i)} \xi(r)$$

where $RS(k, i)$ is the set of resources that $\tau_k$ may access and that $\tau_i$ may also access, and $\xi(r)$ is the length of the critical section of resource $r$.

**Modified Response Time (1 mark):**
$$R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j + B_i$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Consider three tasks: $\tau_1(C_1=2, T_1=6)$, $\tau_2(C_2=2, T_2=8)$, $\tau_3(C_3=4, T_3=12)$. Test feasibility using (i) utilization bound and (ii) response time analysis. Assume implicit deadlines and RMS.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**(i) Utilization Bound (2 marks):**
Tasks ordered by period: $\tau_1 (T=6), \tau_2 (T=8), \tau_3 (T=12)$.

$$U = \frac{2}{6} + \frac{2}{8} + \frac{4}{12} = 0.333 + 0.250 + 0.333 = 0.917$$

$$U_{RMS}(3) = 3(2^{1/3} - 1) = 0.780$$

Since $0.917 > 0.780$, the bound test is **inconclusive**.

**(ii) Response Time Analysis (5 marks):**

For $\tau_1$: $R_1 = C_1 = 2 \le D_1 = 6 \quad \checkmark$

For $\tau_2$: $R_2^{(0)} = 2$
$$R_2^{(1)} = 2 + \left\lceil \frac{2}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 2 + 2 = 4$$
$$R_2^{(2)} = 2 + \left\lceil \frac{4}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 2 + 2 = 4 \quad \text{(Fixed point)}$$
$R_2 = 4 \le D_2 = 8 \quad \checkmark$

For $\tau_3$: $R_3^{(0)} = 4$
$$R_3^{(1)} = 4 + \left\lceil \frac{4}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 + \left\lceil \frac{4}{8} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 4 + 2 + 2 = 8$$
$$R_3^{(2)} = 4 + \left\lceil \frac{8}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 + \left\lceil \frac{8}{8} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 4 + 4 + 2 = 10$$
$$R_3^{(3)} = 4 + \left\lceil \frac{10}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 + \left\lceil \frac{10}{8} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 4 + 4 + 4 = 12$$
$$R_3^{(4)} = 4 + \left\lceil \frac{12}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 + \left\lceil \frac{12}{8} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 4 + 4 + 4 = 12 \quad \text{(Fixed point)}$$
$R_3 = 12 \le D_3 = 12 \quad \checkmark$ (just feasible)

**Conclusion:** The task set is feasible under RMS, even though it exceeds the utilization bound.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**With a neat diagram, explain the working of the Earliest Deadline First (EDF) algorithm. Show that EDF is optimal for preemptive independent periodic tasks.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Working Principle (2 marks):** EDF is a dynamic-priority algorithm where, at every scheduling decision point (task release or completion), the scheduler selects the ready task with the **earliest absolute deadline** for execution. No preassignment of priorities is required.

**Algorithm Steps (2 marks):**
1. Maintain a sorted list of ready tasks by absolute deadline.
2. On each scheduling event (release/completion), re-evaluate and pick min-deadline task.
3. Allow preemption when a newly released task has an earlier deadline than the running one.

**Optimality Sketch (3 marks):**
*Proof by exchange argument:* Suppose a feasible schedule $S$ exists for task set $\Gamma$ at time $t$. If $S$ does not schedule the task with the earliest deadline at $t$, let $\tau_E$ (earliest deadline) and $\tau_X$ (currently scheduled) be the two tasks, with $d_E < d_X$. Since $\tau_X$ has a later deadline, swapping $\tau_E$ and $\tau_X$ in the schedule (exchanging their execution intervals) cannot cause $\tau_X$ to miss its deadline if $\tau_E$ didn't, because $\tau_X$ has a later deadline — it can tolerate being pushed later. By repeated swaps, we transform $S$ into an EDF schedule without violating feasibility. Hence, **if any algorithm can schedule $\Gamma$, EDF can**.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Compare and contrast Rate Monotonic Scheduling and Earliest Deadline First in terms of (i) priority assignment, (ii) utilization bound, (iii) implementation overhead, (iv) behavior under transient overload.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

| Criterion | RMS | EDF | Marks |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(i) Priority Assignment** | Static — based on period, fixed offline | Dynamic — recomputed at every event | 2 |
| **(ii) Utilization Bound** | $n(2^{1/n}-1) \le 0.693$ sufficient | $1.0$ necessary and sufficient | 2 |
| **(iii) Implementation Overhead** | Low (constant priority, $O(1)$ dispatch) | High ($O(\log n)$ min-heap operations) | 1.5 |
| **(iv) Transient Overload Behavior** | Predictable — low-priority tasks miss first, high-priority unaffected | Unpredictable — cascading misses across all tasks (domino effect) | 1.5 |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing sufficient vs necessary:** Many students write "if utilization ≤ 0.693, RMS is feasible" — this is correct, but they then incorrectly infer "if utilization > 0.693, RMS is NOT feasible" — losing 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Skipping iteration step in RTA:** The response time equation must be solved by **iteration** until a fixed point. Showing only one iteration loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Ignoring the ceiling function:** $\lceil R_i / T_j \rceil$ must be evaluated correctly. Treating it as a simple division is a frequent 1-mark deduction.
> 4. **Forgetting to order tasks by period** before applying RMS priority rule. Without ordering, the priority assignment is ambiguous.
> 5. **Misstating EDF optimality:** EDF is optimal among **preemptive, independent, periodic** tasks under certain conditions — not universally optimal for all task models (e.g., multiprocessor or resource-constrained).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Real-time scheduling** = time-constrained execution ordering, not throughput optimization.
- ✅ Task parameters: **$r_i$ (release), $C_i$ (computation/WCET), $D_i$ (deadline), $T_i$ (period)**.
- ✅ Task classes: **Hard** (zero tolerance), **Firm** (no value after deadline), **Soft** (graceful degradation).
- ✅ **Deadline types:** Implicit ($D_i=T_i$), Constrained ($D_i \le T_i$), Arbitrary ($D_i > T_i$).
- ✅ **RMS priority rule:** Shorter period → Higher priority (offline, static).
- ✅ **RMS sufficient test:** $U = \sum C_i/T_i \le n(2^{1/n}-1)$ — bound approaches **69.3%** as $n \to \infty$.
- ✅ **RMS exact test (RTA):** Iterate $R_i^{(k+1)} = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \lceil R_i^{(k)}/T_j \rceil C_j$ until fixed point.
- ✅ **EDF** is dynamically optimal: 100% utilization bound, but with higher runtime overhead.
- ✅ **Priority Inversion** = low-priority task blocks high-priority task indirectly.
- ✅ **Solutions:** Priority Inheritance (PIP), Priority Ceiling (PCP), Stack Resource Policy (SRP).
- ✅ **Hyperperiod** $H = \text{lcm}(T_1, \ldots, T_n)$ is the total schedule repetition interval.
- ✅ **Critical Instant:** Worst-case release time = simultaneous release with all higher-priority tasks.
- ✅ **Context switches** and **interrupt handling** must be included in $C_i$ for practical feasibility.
- ✅ **Killing metric for hard RT:** No statistical guarantees — only **deterministic worst-case** analysis counts.
- ✅ **KTU exam favorite formula:** RTA iteration is the highest-weight numerical question in Module 3.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Task Constraints, Task Scheduling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT785 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Task Constraints, Task Scheduling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Task Constraints — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Task Constraint (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> A *task constraint* is any **restriction, condition, or relationship** placed on a real-time task that governs **when**, **how often**, **in what order**, or **with which shared resources** the task is permitted to execute inside the RTOS scheduler.

A real-time task is never an isolated entity. It exists inside a **multi-tasking temporal ecosystem** where it must coexist with other tasks. The rules governing this coexistence are collectively called **task constraints**. The KTU 2024 syllabus formally classifies them into the following families:

| # | Constraint Family | Core Question Answered |
|---|-------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Temporal / Timing Constraints** | *When* must the task finish? |
| 2 | **Precedence Constraints** | *In what order* must tasks execute? |
| 3 | **Resource Constraints** | *What* shared resources can the task access? |
| 4 | **Reliability / Criticality Constraints** | *How catastrophic* is a missed deadline? |

### 1.1.1 Temporal Constraints (the heart of RTOS)

A task $\tau_i$ is characterized by the tuple:

$$\tau_i = (\Phi_i, \; p_i, \; e_i, \; D_i)$$

where:
- $\Phi_i$ → **Release phase / Offset** (the time at which the task becomes ready for the very first time)
- $p_i$ → **Period** (interval between two consecutive releases)
- $e_i$ → **Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET)**
- $D_i$ → **Relative Deadline** (time from release to absolute completion)

> [!NOTE]
> **Hard Real-Time:** Missing a deadline is a **system failure** (e.g., airbag controller, pacemaker).
> **Soft Real-Time:** Missing a deadline degrades *quality* but not *correctness* (e.g., video streaming).

### 1.1.2 Precedence Constraints

If task $\tau_j$ cannot begin until $\tau_i$ finishes, we write the precedence relation $\tau_i \prec \tau_j$. These are typically visualized using a **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)**.

### 1.1.3 Resource Constraints

If two tasks share a non-reentrant resource (printer, I/O bus, shared memory buffer), they must be **mutually excluded** from simultaneous access via locks, semaphores, or monitors.

---

## 1.2 Task Scheduling — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Task Scheduling (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> Task scheduling is the **decision-making policy** used by the RTOS kernel to determine **which ready task to dispatch onto the CPU at every scheduling point**, ensuring that all timing, precedence, and resource constraints are satisfied while minimizing system-level metrics like response time, jitter, and deadline miss ratio.

The KTU 2024 syllabus identifies the two classical scheduling paradigms:

$$\text{Scheduling} = \begin{cases} \textbf{Clock-Driven (Offline, Static)} \\ \textbf{Event-Driven (Online, Dynamic)} \end{cases}$$

Event-driven scheduling further decomposes into:

$$\text{Event-Driven} = \begin{cases} \textbf{Fixed-Priority} \rightarrow \text{Rate Monotonic (RMS), Deadline Monotonic} \\ \textbf{Dynamic-Priority} \rightarrow \text{EDF, LLF} \\ \textbf{Preemptive} \quad \text{vs} \quad \textbf{Non-Preemptive} \end{cases}$$

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Airport Control Tower

Imagine an **airport runway** as the **CPU**, and each **aircraft** as a **real-time task**:

- **Aircraft A (Flight AI101)** has to land by **06:00 sharp** — that is its **deadline constraint**.
- **Flight IC202** must land *only after* AI101 clears the runway — that is a **precedence constraint**.
- Both aircraft need the same **fuel truck** for refueling before next flight — that is a **resource constraint**.
- The **Air Traffic Controller (ATC)** is the **RTOS scheduler**. The controller does not just decide *who* lands next; the controller decides *who lands now without causing any crash*, exactly like an RTOS scheduler decides *which task executes now without missing any deadline*.

The controller works under a **schedule** — either a **printed timetable** (clock-driven) or a **dynamic radar-based rerouting** (event-driven).

> [!NOTE]
> **Just like an ATC cannot let two aircraft occupy the same runway, an RTOS scheduler cannot let two tasks violate mutual-exclusion constraints simultaneously.**

---

## 1.4 Why This Topic is a KTU Board-Exam Favorite

> [!IMPORTANT]
