> KTU examiners test this topic under **Module 3 (high-weightage 14-mark and 7-mark questions)** because it requires *both* theoretical definitions *and* quantitative analysis (utilization bounds, response time computation). Mastering both halves is essential for a full score.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Anatomy of a Real-Time Task

A periodic task $\tau_i$ generates an **infinite sequence of jobs** $J_{i,1}, J_{i,2}, J_{i,3}, \dots$ where each job $J_{i,k}$ is released at time $r_{i,k} = \Phi_i + (k-1)\,p_i$ and must finish by its absolute deadline $d_{i,k} = r_{i,k} + D_i$.

**Key temporal identities every KTU student must internalize:**

- **Job release time (a.k.a. arrival time):** $r_{i,k} = \Phi_i + (k-1) \cdot p_i$
- **Absolute deadline:** $d_{i,k} = r_{i,k} + D_i$
- **Laxity / Slack at time $t$:** $L_i(t) = D_i - (t - r_{i,k}) - e_i^{\text{remaining}}$
- **Response time of job $k$:** $R_{i,k} = f_{i,k} - r_{i,k}$ where $f_{i,k}$ is the finishing time
- **Schedulability test (necessary condition):** $R_{i,k} \leq D_i$

## 2.2 The Three Hard Laws of RTOS Scheduling

### Law 1 — Law of Critical Instant
> [!IMPORTANT]
> A task's **worst-case response time** occurs when it is released **simultaneously with all higher-priority tasks**. This moment is called the **critical instant**.

### Law 2 — Precedence Constraint Transitivity
> If $\tau_i \prec \tau_j$ and $\tau_j \prec \tau_k$, then $\tau_i \prec \tau_k$. Precedence relations form a **partial order**.

### Law 3 — Mutual Exclusion Inevitability
> If two tasks share a non-reentrant resource, the OS *must* enforce **mutual exclusion**, often via priority inheritance to prevent unbounded priority inversion.

## 2.3 Classification of Scheduling Algorithms (KTU 2024 Taxonomy)

| Scheduling Algorithm | Priority Type | Preemption | KTU Typical Question |
|----------------------|---------------|------------|----------------------|
| **FCFS** | Static (by arrival) | Non-preemptive | Definition |
| **Round Robin** | Dynamic (cyclic) | Preemptive | Time quantum analysis |
| **Rate Monotonic (RMS)** | Static (fixed by period) | Preemptive | Feasibility via Liu-Layland |
| **Deadline Monotonic (DMS)** | Static (fixed by deadline) | Preemptive | Used when $D_i < p_i$ |
| **Earliest Deadline First (EDF)** | Dynamic | Preemptive | Optimal for uniprocessor |
| **Least Laxity First (LLF)** | Dynamic (by laxity) | Preemptive | Laxity computation |
| **Proportional Share (CFS-like)** | Weighted | Preemptive | Brief mention |

## 2.4 The KTU Formula Sheet — All Key Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER THIS TABLE — it covers ~90% of all numerical KTU questions on this topic.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Application |
|---|-------------------|-------------------------|-------------|
| 1 | **CPU Utilization** | $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{e_i}{p_i}$ | Total load on processor |
| 2 | **Liu-Layland Utilization Bound (RMS)** | $U(n) = n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)$ | Sufficient feasibility test |
| 3 | **Asymptotic RMS Bound** | $\lim_{n \to \infty} U(n) = \ln 2 \approx 0.693$ | Theoretical maximum |
| 4 | **Hyperbolic Bound (necessary & sufficient)** | $\prod_{i=1}^{n} \left( 1 + \dfrac{e_i}{p_i} \right) \leq 2$ | Tighter than Liu-Layland |
| 5 | **EDF Optimality Condition** | $U \leq 1$ | Necessary & sufficient |
| 6 | **Response Time Recursion (RTA)** | $R_i^{(k+1)} = e_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \dfrac{R_i^{(k)}}{p_j} \right\rceil e_j$ | Iterate to fixed-point |
| 7 | **Critical Instant Starting Value** | $R_i^{(0)} = e_i$ | Initial guess for RTA |
| 8 | **Schedulability of $\tau_i$** | $R_i \leq D_i$ | If true, task is schedulable |
| 9 | **Laxity (Slack)** | $L_i(t) = D_i - \text{remaining work}(t)$ | Used by LLF scheduler |
| 10 | **Maximum number of tasks (RMS feasibility)** | $n \leq \dfrac{\ln 2}{\ln U}$ | Reverse calculation |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU answer sheets, **never use vertical pipes inside table cells.** Always write $\vert x \vert$ or use absolute value notation explicitly.

## 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These scheduling principles are deployed in:
> - **Aerospace** → NASA CFS avionics (preemptive EDF)
> - **Automotive** → AUTOSAR OS uses fixed-priority preemptive scheduling (RMS-derived)
> - **Medical devices** → Pacemakers use clock-driven scheduling for predictable timing
> - **Industrial robotics** → LLF-style scheduling for sensor-actuator coordination
> - **Smartphones** → Android uses CFS (Completely Fair Scheduler) — a proportional-share variant
> - **5G base stations** → EDF scheduling for HARQ timing budgets

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Liu-Layland Utilization Bound for RMS

This is **the most asked derivation in KTU Module 3**. We derive the maximum CPU utilization below which any set of $n$ periodic tasks will be **guaranteed schedulable** under Rate Monotonic Scheduling.

### 3.1.1 Setup & Assumptions

- We have $n$ periodic tasks $\tau_1, \tau_2, \dots, \tau_n$ with periods $p_1 < p_2 < \dots < p_n$.
- Tasks are ordered by **decreasing priority** (so $\tau_1$ has the *highest* priority).
- All tasks are **independent**, **preemptible**, **periodic**, and have $D_i = p_i$.
- The critical instant occurs at $t = 0$ when all tasks are released simultaneously.

### 3.1.2 Constructing the Worst-Case Scenario

Consider the time interval $[0, \, p_1]$. Since $\tau_1$ has the highest priority, it consumes its full $e_1$ without preemption delay. For the lowest-priority task $\tau_n$, the processor is busy with:

$$\text{Time spent on higher-priority tasks in } [0, p_1] = \left\lceil \frac{p_1}{p_i} \right\rceil \cdot e_i$$

For the bound, we use the **pessimistic ceiling** approximation $\lceil x \rceil \leq x + 1$:

$$\left\lceil \frac{p_1}{p_i} \right\rceil \leq \frac{p_1}{p_i} + 1 - \epsilon$$

where $\epsilon \to 0^+$.

### 3.1.3 Sufficient Feasibility Inequality

The processor demand from higher-priority tasks during $[0, p_1]$ must leave enough time for $\tau_1$:

$$\sum_{i=2}^{n} \left\lceil \frac{p_1}{p_i} \right\rceil \cdot e_i + e_1 \leq p_1$$

Divide throughout by $p_1$:

$$\sum_{i=2}^{n} \frac{e_i}{p_i} + \sum_{i=2}^{n} \frac{e_i}{p_1} + \frac{e_1}{p_1} \leq 1$$

Define $U_i = e_i / p_i$. We require the **worst-case $U_i$ allocation** where each task uses its bound maximally:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} U_i + \sum_{i=2}^{n} \frac{e_i}{p_1} \leq 1$$

To maximize $\sum U_i$, we let $e_i \to p_i$ (extreme case):

$$U_{\max} = 1 - \sum_{i=2}^{n} \frac{p_i}{p_1}$$

But to maximize, we want each $p_i$ to be as close to $p_1$ as possible. The worst-case ratio occurs when periods are packed as:

$$\frac{p_2}{p_1} = \frac{p_3}{p_2} = \dots = R \quad \text{(a constant ratio)}$$

### 3.1.4 Geometric Series Solution

$$\frac{p_i}{p_1} = R^{i-1}$$

The utilization bound $U(n)$ for $n$ tasks then satisfies:

$$1 = U(n) + \frac{1}{R} + \frac{1}{R^2} + \dots + \frac{1}{R^{n-1}} - \frac{1}{R^{n-1}}$$

Wait — using the geometric series and the critical instant condition precisely:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} U_i = U(n), \qquad \sum_{i=2}^{n} \frac{1}{R^{i-1}} = \frac{R - R^{2-n}}{R - 1}$$

Liu & Layland (1973) showed the closed form:

$$U(n) = n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)$$

### 3.1.5 Verification for Small $n$

| $n$ | $U(n)$ Value | Percentage |
|-----|--------------|------------|
| 1 | $1 \cdot (2^1 - 1) = 1.000$ | **100%** |
| 2 | $2 \cdot (\sqrt{2} - 1) = 0.828$ | **82.8%** |
| 3 | $3 \cdot (2^{1/3} - 1) \approx 0.779$ | **77.9%** |
| 4 | $4 \cdot (2^{1/4} - 1) \approx 0.756$ | **75.6%** |
| 5 | $5 \cdot (2^{1/5} - 1) \approx 0.743$ | **74.3%** |
| $\infty$ | $\ln 2$ | **69.3%** |

### 3.1.6 Limit to Infinity

As $n \to \infty$:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right) = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{2^{1/n} - 1}{1/n}$$

Apply L'Hôpital's rule (substitute $x = 1/n$, so $x \to 0$):

$$\lim_{x \to 0} \frac{2^x - 1}{x} = \ln 2 \approx 0.6931$$

$$\boxed{\lim_{n \to \infty} U(n) = \ln 2 \approx 0.693}$$

This is the **fundamental theoretical limit** of RMS on a uniprocessor — beyond 69.3% utilization, RMS *cannot guarantee* schedulability, though individual task sets above this bound *may* still be schedulable.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Scheduling Analysis

This matrix is **frequently asked** as a 7-mark KTU question:

| Property | RMS (Rate Monotonic) | EDF (Earliest Deadline First) |
|----------|---------------------|-------------------------------|
| **Priority Assignment** | Static (fixed by period) | Dynamic (by nearest deadline) |
| **Optimality** | Optimal among fixed-priority | Optimal among all (uniprocessor) |
| **Feasibility Test** | $U \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ | $U \leq 1$ |
| **Implementation Cost** | Low (lookup table) | High (priority recompute per event) |
| **Predictability** | High | Moderate |
| **Overload Behaviour** | Predictable (low-priority misses first) | Domino-effect deadline misses |
| **KTU Industry Usage** | AUTOSAR, VxWorks default | Aerospace (NASA), some research OS |

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — RMS Feasibility Check

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style Question Pattern]**

**Problem:** Three periodic tasks are given:

$$\tau_1: p_1 = 20, \, e_1 = 4 \qquad \tau_2: p_2 = 30, \, e_2 = 6 \qquad \tau_3: p_3 = 50, \, e_3 = 10$$

**Step 1 — Sort by Period (highest priority first):**
Already sorted since $20 < 30 < 50$. So $\tau_1$ has highest priority.

**Step 2 — Compute Individual Utilizations:**

$$U_1 = \frac{e_1}{p_1} = \frac{4}{20} = 0.20$$

$$U_2 = \frac{e_2}{p_2} = \frac{6}{30} = 0.20$$

$$U_3 = \frac{e_3}{p_3} = \frac{10}{50} = 0.20$$

**Step 3 — Total Utilization:**

$$U_{total} = 0.20 + 0.20 + 0.20 = 0.60$$

**Step 4 — Liu-Layland Bound for $n=3$:**

$$U(3) = 3 \cdot \left( 2^{1/3} - 1 \right) \approx 3 \cdot (1.2599 - 1) = 3 \cdot 0.2599 \approx 0.7798$$

**Step 5 — Feasibility Decision:**

$$U_{total} = 0.60 \leq U(3) \approx 0.7798 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \textbf{SCHEDULABLE} \;\checkmark$$

**Step 6 — Hyperbolic Bound Check (tighter):**

$$\prod_{i=1}^{3} \left(1 + U_i\right) = 1.20 \times 1.20 \times 1.20 = 1.728 \leq 2 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \textbf{SCHEDULABLE} \;\checkmark$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Response Time Analysis (RTA) Iteration

For the same task set above, compute $R_3$ (response time of $\tau_3$):

**Iteration $k=0$:**

$$R_3^{(0)} = e_3 = 10$$

**Iteration $k=1$:**

$$R_3^{(1)} = e_3 + \sum_{j=1}^{2} \left\lceil \frac{R_3^{(0)}}{p_j} \right\rceil e_j = 10 + \left\lceil \frac{10}{20} \right\rceil \cdot 4 + \left\lceil \frac{10}{30} \right\rceil \cdot 6$$

$$R_3^{(1)} = 10 + (1)(4) + (1)(6) = 20$$

**Iteration $k=2$:**

$$R_3^{(2)} = 10 + \left\lceil \frac{20}{20} \right\rceil \cdot 4 + \left\lceil \frac{20}{30} \right\rceil \cdot 6 = 10 + (1)(4) + (1)(6) = 20$$

**Fixed point reached:** $R_3 = 20 \leq D_3 = 50$ → **Schedulable** ✓

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Architecture: RTOS Task Constraint & Scheduling Flow

This flowchart visualizes **how an RTOS kernel handles a task from arrival to completion**, while enforcing all constraint families.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([External Event / Interrupt Arrives]) --> B{Is Task Periodic or Aperiodic?}
    B -->|Periodic| C[Compute Release Time r sub i,k]
    B -->|Aperiodic| D[Add to Ready Queue Immediately]
    C --> E[Place Job in Ready Queue]
    D --> E
    E --> F{Check Precedence Constraints}
    F -->|Predecessor Not Finished| G[Block / Wait in Pending Queue]
    F -->|No Precedence or Predecessor Done| H{Check Resource Constraints}
    H -->|Resource Locked by Lower Priority| I[Apply Priority Inheritance]
    H -->|Resource Available| J[Insert into Scheduler's Ready List]
    G --> H
    I --> J
    J --> K{Scheduling Algorithm Active}
    K -->|Clock-Driven| L[Lookup Time-Triggered Schedule Table]
    K -->|RMS| M[Select Highest Priority Smallest Period Task]
    K -->|EDF| N[Select Task with Earliest Absolute Deadline]
    K -->|LLF| O[Select Task with Minimum Laxity]
    L --> P[Dispatch Task onto CPU]
    M --> P
    N --> P
    O --> P
    P --> Q{Deadline Reached before Completion?}
    Q -->|Yes Hard RT| R[System Failure / Exception Handler]
    Q -->|Yes Soft RT| S[Log Miss, Continue Best-Effort]
    Q -->|No| T[Task Completion Achieved]
    T --> U[Release Held Resources and Update Stats]
    U --> V([Return to Idle Loop or Next Event])
```

---

## 4.2 Precedence DAG — Task Dependency Graph

This diagram shows a sample **precedence-constrained task graph** for a robotic arm controller with three sensors feeding into two actuators:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1[tau1: Read Sensor1] --> T3[tau3: Compute Trajectory]
    T2[tau2: Read Sensor2] --> T3
    T3 --> T5[tau5: Actuate Joint B]
    T4[tau4: Read Sensor3] --> T5
    T3 --> T6[tau6: Actuate Joint A]
    T5 --> T7[tau7: Safety Check]
    T6 --> T7
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Precedence Interpretation:** An edge $A \to B$ means task $B$ cannot begin until task $A$ has completed. This is **enforced by the kernel** via semaphores or task synchronization primitives.

---

## 4.3 RMS Scheduling Timeline (Gantt Chart)

For the worked example ($\tau_1, \tau_2, \tau_3$ with $p=\{20,30,50\}$ and $e=\{4,6,10\}$), the first 50 ms execution under RMS is:

```mermaid
gantt
    title Rate Monotonic Scheduling Timeline (Time in ms)
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat %S
    section CPU
    tau1 (prio highest, p=20) :a1, 0, 4
    tau2 (prio medium, p=30) :a2, 4, 6
    tau3 (prio lowest, p=50) :a3, 10, 10
    tau1 (second release) :a4, 20, 4
    tau2 (continuation) :a5, 24, 6
    tau1 (third release) :a6, 40, 4
    tau3 (continuation) :a7, 44, 6
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice how $\tau_1$ **preempts** the others at every multiple of 20 ms because it has the shortest period (highest RMS priority). This is the **hallmark behavior** of fixed-priority preemptive scheduling.

---

## 4.4 EDF vs RMS Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Choose Scheduling Algorithm]) --> Q1{Are task deadlines\nconstrained to be equal\nto periods?}
    Q1 -->|Yes, D sub i = p sub i| Q2{Is task set small\nand utilization below 70%?}
    Q1 -->|No, arbitrary deadlines| DMS[DMS - Deadline Monotonic]
    Q2 -->|Yes| RMS[RMS - Rate Monotonic]
    Q2 -->|No, but utilization below 100%| Q3{Need guaranteed predictability?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| EDF1[EDF - Optimal but runtime cost]
    Q3 -->|No, want simplicity| RMS2[RMS - Simpler and sufficient]
    Q3 -->|Want minimum CPU slack| LLF[LLF - Least Laxity First]
```

---

## 4.5 State Diagram of a Real-Time Task

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Dormant: Task Created
    Dormant --> Ready: Release Time Arrives
    Ready --> Running: Scheduler Dispatches
    Running --> Ready: Preempted by Higher Priority
    Running --> Blocked: Waits for Resource or I/O
    Blocked --> Ready: Resource / I/O Available
    Running --> Completed: Execution Finished
    Completed --> [*]: Task Destroyed
    Running --> DeadlineMiss: WCET exceeded D sub i
    DeadlineMiss --> [*]: System Fault
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Define the term "task constraint" and list any four types of constraints applicable to real-time tasks.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A task constraint is a restriction imposed on the execution of a real-time task governing its timing, ordering, or resource access. The four types are:

1. **Timing/Temporal constraints** — deadlines, release times, periods.
2. **Precedence constraints** — task execution ordering (DAG-based).
3. **Resource constraints** — exclusive access to shared resources.
4. **Criticality/Reliability constraints** — hard vs soft real-time classification.

*(Each type: 0.5 mark. Definition: 1 mark. Total 3 marks.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** State and explain the Liu-Layland utilization bound for Rate Monotonic Scheduling.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Statement (1 mark):** For a set of $n$ independent periodic tasks scheduled under RMS on a uniprocessor, a sufficient condition for schedulability is:

$$U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{e_i}{p_i} \leq n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)$$

**Explanation (2 marks):**
- The bound decreases monotonically as $n$ increases.
- For $n=1$, bound = 100%; for $n=2$, bound = 82.8%; for $n \to \infty$, bound approaches $\ln 2 \approx 69.3\%$.
- It is **sufficient but not necessary** — task sets with utilization slightly above the bound may still be schedulable.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Instruction (per KTU 2024 Scheme):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

#### **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Module 3 Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the classification of real-time task scheduling algorithms with a neat diagram. Differentiate clearly between **clock-driven** and **event-driven** scheduling. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the following three periodic tasks, determine whether the task set is schedulable under Rate Monotonic Scheduling using (i) Liu-Layland bound, (ii) Hyperbolic bound, and (iii) Response Time Analysis. Show all steps. **(7 marks)**

$$\tau_1: (p_1 = 10, e_1 = 2) \qquad \tau_2: (p_2 = 15, e_2 = 4) \qquad \tau_3: (p_3 = 30, e_3 = 5)$$

---

#### 📘 Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Classification of Scheduling Algorithms (7 marks):**

The KTU taxonomy divides real-time scheduling into two major branches:

**1. Clock-Driven (Offline / Static):**
- Schedule is computed **a priori** at design time.
- Stored in a **schedule table** indexed by time.
- Used when tasks are **strictly periodic** and the task set is known in advance.
- Example: Cyclic Executive, Time-Triggered Architecture (TTA).
- **Advantages:** Predictable, minimal runtime overhead, no priority inversion.
- **Disadvantages:** Inflexible to aperiodic events, large schedule table for many tasks.

**2. Event-Driven (Online / Dynamic):**
- Decisions made **at runtime** triggered by task arrivals/completions.
- Sub-categories:
    - **Fixed Priority** (e.g., RMS, DMS) — priority assigned once.
    - **Dynamic Priority** (e.g., EDF, LLF) — priority recomputed at each event.

**Comparison Table (must include in answer):**

| Aspect | Clock-Driven | Event-Driven |
|--------|--------------|--------------|
| Decision time | Design phase | Runtime |
| Flexibility | Low | High |
| Overhead | Very low | Moderate to high |
| Aperiodic handling | Poor | Good |
| Predictability | Highest | High |

**Diagram:** *(Draw the classification tree as shown in Section 4.4 of these notes — full marks only with diagram.)*

**(b) Feasibility Analysis (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Sort by Period (RMS priority):**
$p_1 = 10 < p_2 = 15 < p_3 = 30$ → Already sorted.

**Step 2 — Compute Individual Utilizations:**

$$U_1 = \frac{2}{10} = 0.20, \quad U_2 = \frac{4}{15} \approx 0.267, \quad U_3 = \frac{5}{30} \approx 0.167$$

**Step 3 — Total Utilization:**

$$U_{total} = 0.20 + 0.267 + 0.167 = 0.633$$

**(i) Liu-Layland Bound Test (2 marks):**

$$U(3) = 3 \cdot (2^{1/3} - 1) \approx 0.7798$$

Since $0.633 \leq 0.7798$ → **SCHEDULABLE** ✓

**(ii) Hyperbolic Bound Test (2 marks):**

$$\prod_{i=1}^{3} (1 + U_i) = (1.20)(1.267)(1.167) \approx 1.774 \leq 2$$

→ **SCHEDULABLE** ✓

**(iii) Response Time Analysis (3 marks):**

Iterate for $\tau_3$ (the lowest priority task):

$R_3^{(0)} = e_3 = 5$

$R_3^{(1)} = 5 + \lceil 5/10 \rceil \cdot 2 + \lceil 5/15 \rceil \cdot 4 = 5 + 2 + 4 = 11$

$R_3^{(2)} = 5 + \lceil 11/10 \rceil \cdot 2 + \lceil 11/15 \rceil \cdot 4 = 5 + 4 + 4 = 13$

$R_3^{(3)} = 5 + \lceil 13/10 \rceil \cdot 2 + \lceil 13/15 \rceil \cdot 4 = 5 + 4 + 4 = 13$

**Fixed point:** $R_3 = 13 \leq D_3 = p_3 = 30$ → **SCHEDULABLE** ✓

**Conclusion:** The task set is schedulable under RMS by all three tests.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Module 3 Pattern]**

**(a)** Discuss the concept of **precedence constraints** in real-time systems with a suitable example. Explain how they are represented using a **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)** and **Earliest Deadline First (EDF)** scheduling algorithms under the following heads: priority assignment, optimality, feasibility test, implementation cost, and overload behaviour. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 📘 Model Solution to Question B

**(a) Precedence Constraints (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** A precedence constraint exists when a task $\tau_j$ cannot start execution until task $\tau_i$ has completed. Symbolically: $\tau_i \prec \tau_j$ (read as "$\tau_i$ precedes $\tau_j$").

**Example (3 marks):** Consider a flight control system with three tasks:
- $\tau_1$: Read altitude sensor
- $\tau_2$: Compute thrust correction
- $\tau_3$: Actuate engine valve

The constraint: $\tau_2$ needs altitude data → $\tau_1 \prec \tau_2$; the actuator needs thrust value → $\tau_2 \prec \tau_3$.

**DAG Representation (2 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N1[tau1: Altitude] --> N2[tau2: Thrust]
    N2 --> N3[tau3: Actuate]
```

The kernel enforces this DAG using **semaphores** (e.g., $\tau_2$ executes `sem_wait(S1)` where $\tau_1$ did `sem_post(S1)`).

**(b) RMS vs EDF Comparison (7 marks):**

| Criterion | RMS | EDF |
|-----------|-----|-----|
| **Priority Assignment** (1 mark) | Static; based on period (shorter period = higher priority) | Dynamic; based on absolute deadline |
| **Optimality** (2 marks) | Optimal **only within fixed-priority class** | **Optimal overall** for uniprocessor (Horn 1974) |
| **Feasibility Test** (2 marks) | Sufficient: $U \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)$; exact via RTA | Necessary & sufficient: $U \leq 1$ |
| **Implementation Cost** (1 mark) | Low — simple priority lookup | High — priority recomputed on every job arrival |
| **Overload Behaviour** (1 mark) | Predictable — only low-priority tasks miss deadlines | Domino effect — can cascade to high-priority tasks |

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Module 3 Answers:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to sort tasks by period** before applying RMS — full marks lost.
> 2. **Confusing $D_i$ with $p_i$** — write explicitly that the Liu-Layland bound assumes $D_i = p_i$.
> 3. **Using Liu-Layland bound as a NECESSARY condition** — it is only SUFFICIENT. A task set with $U > U(n)$ may still be schedulable.
> 4. **Skipping the diagram** in classification questions — KTU examiners allocate **1.5–2 marks** strictly for the diagram.
> 5. **Not showing iteration convergence** in RTA — write at least 3 iterations even if the fixed-point arrives earlier.
> 6. **Writing $\vert$ symbols inside LaTeX tables** — breaks the table; use $\lvert \cdot \rvert$ instead.
> 7. **Forgetting to state units** ($ms$ or $\mu s$) in numerical answers.
> 8. **Missing the critical instant assumption** in derivation questions.

---

## 📝 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — Task Constraints & Scheduling:**

- ☐ A real-time task is the tuple $(\Phi_i, p_i, e_i, D_i)$.
- ☐ **Temporal constraints** define deadlines; **Precedence constraints** define order; **Resource constraints** define mutual exclusion; **Criticality constraints** classify hard vs soft.
- ☐ **Clock-driven** scheduling is offline and predictable; **Event-driven** is online and flexible.
- ☐ **RMS** assigns fixed priority inversely proportional to period (preemptive).
- ☐ **Liu-Layland bound** = $n(2^{1/n} - 1)$; asymptotic limit = $\ln 2 \approx 0.6931$.
- ☐ **EDF** is provably optimal on uniprocessors; feasibility iff $U \leq 1$.
- ☐ **Response Time Analysis** iterates $R_i^{(k+1)} = e_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \lceil R_i^{(k)} / p_j \rceil \cdot e_j$ until fixed point.
- ☐ **Schedulability condition** in RTA: $R_i \leq D_i$ for all $i$.
- ☐ **Critical instant** = simultaneous release of all tasks at $t=0$.
- ☐ **Hyperbolic bound** $\prod_i (1 + U_i) \leq 2$ is a tighter necessary-and-sufficient feasibility test.
- ☐ **Priority inversion** is mitigated via **Priority Inheritance Protocol** (Sha, Rajkumar, Lehoczky 1990).
- ☐ **KTU trademark question**: "Determine if task set is schedulable" → always show all three tests (Liu-Layland, Hyperbolic, RTA).
- ☐ **Diagrams are mandatory** — never skip them in classification and DAG-based questions.
- ☐ **Use $\lvert \cdot \rvert$ or $\mid$** instead of `|` inside any markdown table to avoid formatting breakage.
- ☐ **One blank line** before and after every `$$` LaTeX display block.

topics.title: Aperiodic Task Scheduling (Jackson's Algorithm and Horn's Algorithm), Scheduling with Precedence Constraints
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT785 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Aperiodic Task Scheduling (Jackson's Algorithm and Horn's Algorithm), Scheduling with Precedence Constraints

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Aperiodic Task Scheduling — Foundational Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Aperiodic Task**: A task that arrives at irregular, non-periodic intervals and has a hard deadline by which it must complete. It executes **at most once** (or in a sporadic, non-cyclic pattern) and is characterized by an arrival time ($a_i$), worst-case execution time ($C_i$ or $e_i$), and an absolute deadline ($d_i$).

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, **Aperiodic Task Scheduling** refers to the offline (or sometimes online) allocation of a finite set of independent, non-recurring jobs on a **single processor** such that **every job completes by its deadline**. The two cornerstone algorithms in this domain are:

1. **Jackson's Rule (1955)** — an *Earliest-Deadline-First (EDF)* discipline for independent aperiodic tasks.
2. **Horn's Algorithm (1974)** — an optimal scheduler for **unit-execution-time tasks subject to precedence constraints**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Single-Processor Optimality**: On a single processor, Jackson's algorithm minimizes the **maximum lateness** ($L_{max}$) and produces a feasible schedule whenever one exists for independent tasks with deadlines.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Airport Runway" Intuition

> [!TIP]
> Think of a **single airport runway** shared by multiple aircraft holding at the gate, each with a hard "wheels-stop" deadline.

- **Jackson's Algorithm (EDF)**: The air traffic controller always lets the plane with the *earliest landing slot* take off first, regardless of when it arrived or how long it needs the runway. If the controller always picks the most urgent plane, no plane will ever be late — this is exactly the EDF logic.
- **Horn's Algorithm**: Now imagine some planes **cannot land until others have refueled (precedence)** and every plane needs the runway for **exactly the same duration** (1 minute). Horn's algorithm finds the smallest number of 1-minute slots so that the dependency graph is satisfied. It's like a tightly choreographed "follow-the-leader" landing sequence.
- **Precedence Constraints**: A pilot (task $T_2$) cannot start her pre-flight checklist (task $T_1$) until the cargo (task $T_0$) is loaded — a strict **happens-before** relationship.

This single-runway, fixed-urgency world is precisely the model KTU examiners love to test.

---

## 1.3 Formal Notation Used Throughout This Module

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $n$ | Number of aperiodic jobs in the system | dimensionless |
| $r_i$ | Release (arrival) time of job $i$ | time units |
| $C_i$ (or $e_i$) | Worst-case execution time of job $i$ | time units |
| $d_i$ | Absolute deadline of job $i$ | time units |
| $f_i$ | Finishing time of job $i$ | time units |
| $L_{max}$ | Maximum lateness: $\max_i (f_i - d_i)$ | time units |
| $\tau$ | Task set | set |
| $H(\tau)$ | Length of longest chain in DAG $\tau$ | dimensionless |
| $L^*$ | Optimal schedule length | time units |

---

## 1.4 Syllabus Highlights for KTU 2024

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 3 — High-Yield Topics (Direct Exam Relevance):**
> - Jackson's Rule: construction, feasibility test, optimality proof
> - Horn's Algorithm: topological sort + unit-time scheduling
> - Feasibility inequalities for EDF
> - Precedence constraints and the **longest path / critical chain** lower bound
> - Complexity: $O(n \log n)$ for Jackson, $O(n^2)$ or $O(n + e)$ for Horn

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Jackson's Algorithm — Operational Logic

Jackson's algorithm is a **greedy, deadline-driven scheduler** for a set of $n$ independent, aperiodic jobs on a single processor. The rule is deceptively simple:

> **At every scheduling decision point, execute the ready job with the earliest absolute deadline.**

### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Input Assembly**: Gather the entire set of jobs $\tau = \{J_1, J_2, \dots, J_n\}$ with parameters $(r_i, C_i, d_i)$.
2. **Pre-sorting**: Sort the jobs in **non-decreasing order of deadline**: $d_{(1)} \le d_{(2)} \le \dots \le d_{(n)}$.
3. **Sequential Construction**: Schedule job $J_{(k)}$ at the earliest time interval of length $C_{(k)}$ that does not overlap any previously scheduled interval. With all jobs available at time $0$ (or after release), this reduces to a contiguous back-to-back construction.
4. **Feasibility Check**: At every step, verify that the cumulative processing time up to job $J_{(k)}$ does not exceed its deadline.
5. **Output**: The earliest-deadline-first schedule. If a feasible schedule exists, Jackson's rule finds it.

### 2.1.2 Why Does It Work? — The "Why" Behind the Greedy Choice

> [!TIP]
> **Exchange Argument (Sketch)**: Suppose an optimal schedule $S^*$ disagrees with Jackson's schedule $J$ at the first inversion. There exist two adjacent jobs $J_a$ (with $d_a > d_b$) and $J_b$ such that $J_a$ is scheduled before $J_b$ in $S^*$. Swapping $J_a$ and $J_b$ does not increase $L_{max}$ because $J_b$ finishes no later than before, and $J_a$ is given a slot it could tolerate. Iterating this exchange reduces $S^*$ to $J$ with $L_{max}$ unchanged — proving optimality.

---

## 2.2 Horn's Algorithm — Operational Logic

Horn's algorithm addresses a more constrained problem:

- All jobs have **unit execution time** ($C_i = 1$ for all $i$).
- Jobs are subject to **precedence constraints** expressed as a **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)**.
- Goal: minimize the **schedule length** (or **makespan** $L^*$).

### 2.2.1 Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Build the DAG**: For each precedence $T_i \prec T_j$, add a directed edge $i \to j$.
2. **Initialize**: Compute the **longest path** (in number of edges) from any source to each node $T_i$. This gives the *latest possible time slot* $t_i$ at which $T_i$ can be scheduled, namely $t_i = H(\tau) - 1 - h_i$, where $h_i$ is the height (longest path) of $T_i$.
3. **Schedule from the Last Slot Backwards**: Consider time slots $L^* - 1, L^* - 2, \dots, 0$.
4. At each time slot $s$, find an **unscheduled ready job** $T_i$ whose $t_i = s$. Schedule it.
5. **Terminate** when all jobs are scheduled. The result is a valid, optimal unit-time schedule of length $L^* = H(\tau)$.

### 2.2.2 Horn's Theorem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Horn's Theorem (1974)**: For a set of $n$ unit-time tasks with precedence constraints expressed as a partial order $\prec$ on $\tau$, the **minimum schedule length** is exactly the **size of the largest chain** in the partial order:
>
> $$L^* = H(\tau) = \text{length of the longest chain}$$
>
> where a *chain* is a totally ordered subset of $\tau$.

---

## 2.3 Scheduling with Precedence Constraints — The General View

When execution times are **not** unit and tasks have **precedence constraints**, the problem becomes **NP-hard** in general (Lawler's theorem). The classical results KTU expects you to know are:

- **Critical Path / Lower Bound**: $L^* \ge \max\limits_{C \in \text{chains}} \sum_{i \in C} C_i$.
- **Critical Path Length (CPL)**: For jobs with arbitrary processing times, the longest path through the DAG (summing processing times) is a lower bound.
- **List Scheduling**: A greedy heuristic that maintains a ready list; at any time, schedule the ready job with the highest priority. Worst-case bound: $L_{list} \le (2 - 1/m) \cdot L_{opt}$ for $m$ machines.
- **Latest Deadline First (LDF)** with precedence: for arbitrary processing times, LDF + feasibility test is optimal if and only if a feasible schedule exists.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Favorite**: When a precedence-constrained scheduling problem is given, always compute the **longest chain length** and compare it to the schedule's makespan. If they match, the schedule is optimal.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> Memorize the table below. Every KTU Part B question on this module draws from these formulas.

| # | Formula / Inequality | Meaning | When to Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $L_{max} = \max\limits_{1 \le i \le n} (f_i - d_i)$ | Maximum lateness | Jackson's optimality metric |
| 2 | $\sum_{i: d_i \le t} C_i \le t, \quad \forall t$ | Feasibility inequality for EDF (when $r_i = 0$) | Checking EDF schedule feasibility |
| 3 | $\sum_{i: d_i \le t} C_i \le t, \quad \forall t \ge 0$ | Hall-type condition for feasibility | General aperiodic feasibility |
| 4 | $L^* = H(\tau)$ | Optimal length for unit-time + precedence | Horn's algorithm |
| 5 | $L^* \ge \max\limits_{P \in \text{paths}} \sum_{i \in P} C_i$ | Critical path lower bound | General precedence scheduling |
| 6 | $f_i \le d_i$ | Job $i$ meets its deadline | Validity of a schedule |
| 7 | $L_{EDF} = L_{opt}$ for single processor | EDF optimality | Justification in proofs |
| 8 | $\vert S \vert$ = number of jobs in subset $S$ | Subset counting | Hall's theorem applications |
| 9 | $N_q(t)$ = number of jobs with $d_i \le t$ | Cumulative deadline count | Bound checking |
| 10 | $t_i = H(\tau) - 1 - h_i$ | Latest slot for task $i$ | Horn's backward scheduling |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where these algorithms ship in industry:**
> - **Jackson's EDF**: Foundation of every modern **RTOS scheduler** (FreeRTOS, VxWorks, ThreadX) for aperiodic/sporadic workloads like interrupt service routines and event handlers.
> - **Horn's Algorithm**: Used in **instruction scheduling** of compilers for VLIW and EPIC architectures (e.g., Intel Itanium) where each operation takes exactly one cycle and data dependencies are explicit.
> - **Precedence-Constrained Scheduling**: Powers **digital signal processing (DSP) code generation**, **CAD tool placement and routing**, and **real-time workflow orchestration** in SCADA systems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Jackson's Rule Minimizes $L_{max}$ — Full Exchange Argument

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the most-asked KTU Part B derivation. We reconstruct the proof rigorously.

**Theorem (Jackson, 1955)**: For a set of $n$ independent jobs on a single processor with release time $r_i = 0$, the **Earliest Deadline First (EDF)** schedule minimizes $L_{max}$.

**Proof**:

**Step 1 — Setup**.
Let $S^*$ be any schedule that minimizes $L_{max}$. Index jobs such that in $S^*$ the jobs execute in order $J_{s(1)}, J_{s(2)}, \dots, J_{s(n)}$. Let Jackson's EDF order be $J_{e(1)}, J_{e(2)}, \dots, J_{e(n)}$ where $d_{e(1)} \le d_{e(2)} \le \dots \le d_{e(n)}$.

**Step 2 — Define Inversion**.
An **inversion** in $S^*$ is a pair of indices $(k, k+1)$ such that $d_{s(k)} > d_{s(k+1)}$ — i.e., a later-deadline job precedes an earlier-deadline job.

**Step 3 — Effect of Swapping an Inversion**.
Consider the first inversion in $S^*$ between jobs $J_a = J_{s(k)}$ and $J_b = J_{s(k+1)}$. Let the finish time of $J_a$ in $S^*$ be $F_a$, and the start time of $J_b$ be $S_b$. After swapping, $J_b$ now finishes at $F_a$ and $J_a$ now finishes at $F_a + C_a$.

- New lateness of $J_b$ is $F_a - d_b$. Old lateness was $S_b - d_b$. Since $F_a > S_b$ (because $J_a$ precedes $J_b$), the new lateness could be **larger**.

Wait — we must be careful. The exchange argument in the **continuous-time** setting is more subtle. The classic proof (Horn 1974) handles this with **idle-time insertion**:

**Step 3 (Refined) — Insertion Argument**.
If $J_a$ and $J_b$ are inverted and $C_a \le C_b$, then swap them; the swap does not increase $L_{max}$ because $J_b$ (the earlier deadline) finishes no later, and $J_a$ is delayed by at most $C_b - C_a$, but since $d_a > d_b$, the increase in $J_a$'s lateness is bounded by the decrease in $J_b$'s lateness.

**Step 4 — Full Resolution via Hall's Theorem**.
The strongest modern statement uses **Hall's Marriage Theorem**: A feasible EDF schedule exists iff

$$\sum_{i \,:\, d_i \le t} C_i \le t, \quad \forall \, t \ge 0$$

This is the **Holt-Kozen / Lawler** feasibility condition. We now derive it.

---

### 3.1.1 Derivation of the EDF Feasibility Inequality

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently ask: *"Prove that EDF is feasible iff $\sum_{i: d_i \le t} C_i \le t$ for all $t$."*

**Forward Direction ($\Rightarrow$)**:
Assume EDF produces a feasible schedule where every $J_i$ finishes by $d_i$. Let $t$ be arbitrary. Define the set $S(t) = \{i : d_i \le t\}$.

All jobs in $S(t)$ must finish by time $t$, because each has deadline $\le t$. They are scheduled consecutively by EDF (since deadlines are sorted) starting at the latest release time among them. The total processor time consumed by $S(t)$ is $\sum_{i \in S(t)} C_i$. Since the processor is single and the jobs cannot finish later than $t$:

$$\sum_{i \in S(t)} C_i \le t$$

**Backward Direction ($\Leftarrow$)**:
Assume the inequality holds for all $t$. Construct the EDF schedule. We prove by induction on the scheduled prefix.

**Inductive Hypothesis**: After scheduling jobs $J_{(1)}, \dots, J_{(k)}$ (in EDF order), the finish time of $J_{(k)}$ is

$$F_{(k)} = \sum_{j=1}^{k} C_{(j)}$$

**Inductive Step**: We need to show $F_{(k)} \le d_{(k)}$.

Let $t = d_{(k)}$. Then $S(t) = \{i : d_i \le d_{(k)}\} = \{J_{(1)}, \dots, J_{(k)}\}$ (since the list is deadline-sorted and $d_{(k)}$ is the largest deadline among the first $k$).

By the feasibility inequality:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{k} C_{(i)} \le d_{(k)}$$

But $\sum_{i=1}^{k} C_{(i)} = F_{(k)}$, so $F_{(k)} \le d_{(k)}$.

This completes the proof. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Horn's Algorithm Yields $L^* = H(\tau)$

**Setup**: We have $n$ unit-time jobs with precedence DAG $G = (\tau, E)$ and no release times.

**Definition**: A *chain* in $G$ is a sequence of jobs $J_{i_1} \prec J_{i_2} \prec \dots \prec J_{i_k}$ where each $J_{i_j} \prec J_{i_{j+1}}$ is a precedence edge (or transitive). The *length* of a chain is its cardinality $k$.

**Lower Bound**: Any chain of length $k$ requires at least $k$ time slots (one per job), so

$$L^* \ge \max_{\text{chain } C} \vert C \vert = H(\tau)$$

**Upper Bound (Constructive)**: We show Horn's algorithm achieves $L^* = H(\tau)$.

For each job $J_i$, define $h_i$ as the length of the longest chain ending at $J_i$ (in node count, with $h_i = 1$ if $J_i$ has no predecessors). The "level" of $J_i$ is

$$t_i = H(\tau) - h_i$$

This places $J_i$ in a slot such that if $J_i \prec J_j$ (an edge), then $h_j \ge h_i + 1$, so $t_j = H(\tau) - h_j \le H(\tau) - h_i - 1 < t_i$. Hence $J_i$ is scheduled **strictly before** $J_j$.

**Construction proceeds from $t = H(\tau) - 1$ down to $0$**:
At each time $t$, schedule any unscheduled ready job $J_i$ with $t_i = t$. Since $H(\tau)$ levels cover all jobs (every job has some $h_i$ between $1$ and $H(\tau)$), and within each level the number of jobs is at most the number of available slots, the schedule completes in exactly $H(\tau)$ slots.

**Conclusion**:

$$L_{Horn} = H(\tau) = L^* \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Jackson's Rule

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024-style numerical problem.** Work through this with the derivation above.

**Given**: Aperiodic jobs on a single processor, all available at $t = 0$.

| Job $J_i$ | $C_i$ | $d_i$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $J_1$ | 1 | 4 |
| $J_2$ | 2 | 2 |
| $J_3$ | 1 | 8 |
| $J_4$ | 3 | 7 |
| $J_5$ | 1 | 5 |

**Step 1 — Sort by Deadline (Non-Decreasing)**:

$$J_2 (d=2), \; J_1 (d=4), \; J_5 (d=5), \; J_4 (d=7), \; J_3 (d=8)$$

**Step 2 — Build EDF Schedule**:

| Interval | Job | Cumulative $C$ | $d_i$ | Feasible? |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $[0, 2)$ | $J_2$ | 2 | 2 | $2 \le 2$ ✓ |
| $[2, 3)$ | $J_1$ | 3 | 4 | $3 \le 4$ ✓ |
| $[3, 4)$ | $J_5$ | 4 | 5 | $4 \le 5$ ✓ |
| $[4, 7)$ | $J_4$ | 7 | 7 | $7 \le 7$ ✓ |
| $[7, 8)$ | $J_3$ | 8 | 8 | $8 \le 8$ ✓ |

**Step 3 — Feasibility Inequality Check**:
For all $t \in \{2, 4, 5, 7, 8\}$:

$$\sum_{i: d_i \le t} C_i \le t$$

This is satisfied with equality. The schedule is **feasible and optimal** with $L_{max} = 0$.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Horn's Algorithm

**Given**: 6 unit-time tasks with precedences:
$T_1 \prec T_3, T_1 \prec T_4, T_2 \prec T_4, T_2 \prec T_5, T_3 \prec T_6, T_4 \prec T_6, T_5 \prec T_6$.

**Step 1 — Build DAG & Find Longest Chain**:

A longest chain: $T_1 \to T_3 \to T_6$ (length 3), or $T_1 \to T_4 \to T_6$ (length 3), or $T_2 \to T_4 \to T_6$ (length 3), or $T_2 \to T_5 \to T_6$ (length 3). Thus

$$H(\tau) = 3$$

**Step 2 — Compute $h_i$ (longest chain ending at $T_i$)**:
- $h_1 = 1$ (no predecessors)
- $h_2 = 1$ (no predecessors)
- $h_3 = h_1 + 1 = 2$
- $h_4 = \max(h_1, h_2) + 1 = 2$
- $h_5 = h_2 + 1 = 2$
- $h_6 = \max(h_3, h_4, h_5) + 1 = 3$

**Step 3 — Compute $t_i = H(\tau) - h_i$**:
- $t_1 = 3 - 1 = 2$
- $t_2 = 3 - 1 = 2$
- $t_3 = 3 - 2 = 1$
- $t_4 = 3 - 2 = 1$
- $t_5 = 3 - 2 = 1$
- $t_6 = 3 - 3 = 0$

**Step 4 — Schedule from $t = 2$ Downwards**:
- Slot 2: $T_1, T_2$ (both ready, both at level 2)
- Slot 1: $T_3, T_4, T_5$ (all at level 1, all predecessors done)
- Slot 0: $T_6$ (at level 0, all predecessors done)

| Slot 0 | Slot 1 | Slot 2 |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $T_6$ | $T_3, T_4, T_5$ | $T_1, T_2$ |

**Makespan** $L^* = 3 = H(\tau)$. Optimal. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Algorithm to Use?

> [!TIP]
> This matrix is gold for KTU Part B answer-writing. Examiners award full marks when you **state and justify** the choice.

| Problem Characteristic | Recommended Algorithm | Justification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Independent jobs, $C_i$ arbitrary, $r_i = 0$ | **Jackson's EDF** | Minimizes $L_{max}$ in $O(n \log n)$ |
| Independent jobs, $r_i$ arbitrary | EDF with release times (Horn's variant) | Feasibility via Hall-type condition |
| Unit $C_i = 1$, precedence DAG present | **Horn's Algorithm** | $L^* = H(\tau)$ exactly |
| Arbitrary $C_i$, precedence DAG present | **List Scheduling / LDF + feasibility** | NP-hard; use critical-path heuristic |
| Preemptive, independent | **EDF (preemptive)** | Optimal even with release times |
| Multiprocessor ($m > 1$), unit tasks, precedence | **Hu's Algorithm** | $L^* = \lceil H(\tau) / m \rceil$ lower bound |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Jackson's Algorithm — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Task Set tau] --> B[Extract tuples: r_i, C_i, d_i]
    B --> C{All r_i = 0?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Sort by non-decreasing d_i]
    C -- No --> E[Use online EDF with release times]
    D --> F[Initialize: time = 0]
    F --> G[Pick earliest-deadline ready job J_k]
    G --> H[Schedule J_k in slot time, time+C_k]
    H --> I{sum C_i for d_i <= t <= t?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Mark J_k as scheduled]
    I -- No --> K[INFEASIBLE - Return failure]
    J --> L{All jobs scheduled?}
    L -- No --> G
    L -- Yes --> M[Output: Feasible EDF schedule]
    E --> N[At time t, schedule job with min d_i among ready]
    N --> L
```

## 4.2 Horn's Algorithm — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Unit-time tasks with precedence DAG] --> B[Construct Directed Acyclic Graph G]
    B --> C[Compute h_i: longest path ending at task i]
    C --> D[Find H_tau: maximum h_i over all tasks]
    D --> E[Compute t_i = H_tau - h_i for each task]
    E --> F[Initialize empty schedule of length H_tau]
    F --> G[For slot s from H_tau-1 down to 0]
    G --> H[Find unscheduled task J_i with t_i = s]
    H --> I{All predecessors of J_i scheduled in earlier slots?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Assign J_i to slot s]
    I -- No --> K[Defer J_i to next available slot]
    J --> L{All tasks scheduled?}
    L -- No --> G
    L -- Yes --> M[Output: Optimal schedule with makespan H_tau]
    K --> G
```

## 4.3 Precedence Constraint Scheduling — Architectural View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S0[Slot 0]
        S6[T6]
    end
    subgraph S1[Slot 1]
        S3[T3]
        S4[T4]
        S5[T5]
    end
    subgraph S2[Slot 2]
        S1[T1]
        S2[T2]
    end
    S1 --> S3
    S1 --> S4
    S2 --> S4
    S2 --> S5
    S3 --> S6
    S4 --> S6
    S5 --> S6
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram**: This is the Horn-scheduled Gantt for the 3.4 example. Arrows are precedence edges. Vertical grouping by slot shows that **all predecessors of a task lie in earlier slots**, validating feasibility.

## 4.4 EDF vs. Horn Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[New Scheduling Problem] --> B{Are tasks independent?}
    B -- Yes --> C{All jobs available at t=0?}
    C -- Yes --> D{Any processing time constraint?}
    D -- Arbitrary C_i --> E[Use JACKSON's EDF]
    D -- C_i = 1 --> F[Use EARLIEST DEADLINE FIRST]
    C -- No, varying r_i --> G[Online EDF with feasibility check]
    B -- No, precedence exists --> H{Unit processing times?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Use HORN's ALGORITHM]
    H -- No, arbitrary C_i --> J[Use List Scheduling + Critical Path Bound]
    J --> K{Number of processors = 1?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Apply LDF with precedence test]
    K -- No, m > 1 --> M[Use HU's Algorithm m processor variant]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]
**"State Jackson's rule for scheduling independent aperiodic tasks. Under what conditions is it optimal?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

Jackson's rule states: *"At any scheduling instant, execute the ready job with the earliest absolute deadline."*

It is optimal in the sense of **minimizing the maximum lateness** $L_{max}$ for:

1. A set of $n$ independent jobs (no precedence constraints),
2. A single processor,
3. All jobs available at $t = 0$ (or more generally, when release times are not constraints, or under preemptive EDF with arbitrary release times).

Formally, $L_{max} = 0$ whenever a feasible schedule exists, since EDF satisfies the Hall-type feasibility condition:

$$\sum_{i: d_i \le t} C_i \le t \quad \forall t \ge 0$$

**(3 marks: rule 1M, conditions 1M, formula 1M)**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]
**"Define Horn's algorithm. State Horn's theorem."**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

**Horn's Algorithm**: An optimal scheduling procedure for **$n$ unit-time tasks** subject to precedence constraints (expressed as a DAG), producing a schedule of minimum makespan.

**Horn's Theorem**: The minimum schedule length equals the size of the **largest chain** (longest totally ordered subset) in the precedence DAG:

$$L^* = H(\tau) = \max_{\text{chain } C \subseteq \tau} \vert C \vert$$

**Construction**: For each task $T_i$, compute the longest chain length $h_i$ ending at $T_i$. Schedule $T_i$ in slot $t_i = H(\tau) - h_i$, iterating from the highest-numbered slot downward.

**(3 marks: definition 1M, theorem 1M, construction 1M)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A — Jackson's Algorithm (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted]

Consider the following set of 5 independent aperiodic jobs on a single processor, all available at $t = 0$:

| Job | $C_i$ | $d_i$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $J_1$ | 2 | 5 |
| $J_2$ | 1 | 2 |
| $J_3$ | 3 | 7 |
| $J_4$ | 1 | 4 |
| $J_5$ | 2 | 6 |

#### (a) Construct Jackson's (EDF) schedule and verify feasibility. (7 marks)

**Model Solution**:

**Step 1 — Sort by non-decreasing deadline**:

Order: $J_2 (d=2), J_4 (d=4), J_1 (d=5), J_5 (d=6), J_3 (d=7)$.

**Step 2 — Build schedule sequentially**:

- Schedule $J_2$ in $[0, 1)$. Cumulative $C = 1 \le d_2 = 2$. ✓
- Schedule $J_4$ in $[1, 2)$. Cumulative $C = 2 \le d_4 = 4$. ✓
- Schedule $J_1$ in $[2, 4)$. Cumulative $C = 4 \le d_1 = 5$. ✓
- Schedule $J_5$ in $[4, 6)$. Cumulative $C = 6 \le d_5 = 6$. ✓
- Schedule $J_3$ in $[6, 9)$. Cumulative $C = 9 \le d_3 = 7$? **NO! $9 > 7$.**

**Step 3 — Try EDF by re-sorting considering insertion**:

The pure sorted order fails at $J_3$. Apply **Jackson's algorithm with feasibility check at each prefix**:

For $t = 7$: $\sum_{i: d_i \le 7} C_i = 1+1+2+2+3 = 9 > 7$. **INFEASIBLE**.

**Conclusion**: No feasible schedule exists. The set $\tau$ is **not schedulable** on a single processor.

**However**, the EDF order produces the *best possible* schedule with **minimum maximum lateness**:

- $f_2 = 1, L_2 = 1 - 2 = -1$
- $f_4 = 2, L_4 = 2 - 4 = -2$
- $f_1 = 4, L_1 = 4 - 5 = -1$
- $f_5 = 6, L_5 = 6 - 6 = 0$
- $f_3 = 9, L_3 = 9 - 7 = +2$

$$L_{max} = +2 \text{ time units (achieved by } J_3\text{)}$$

**(7 marks: sorting 1M, schedule construction 2M, feasibility check 2M, $L_{max}$ computation 2M)**

#### (b) State and prove the EDF optimality theorem using the exchange argument. (7 marks)

**Model Solution**:

**Theorem**: For a set of $n$ independent jobs (all available at $t=0$) on a single processor, the EDF schedule minimizes $L_{max}$.

**Proof**:

**Step 1**: Let $S^*$ be any schedule minimizing $L_{max}$, expressed as a job sequence $\pi^* = (J_{p(1)}, J_{p(2)}, \dots, J_{p(n)})$.

**Step 2**: An *inversion* in $\pi^*$ is a pair of consecutive jobs $(J_{p(k)}, J_{p(k+1)})$ such that $d_{p(k)} > d_{p(k+1)}$.

**Step 3 — Exchange Lemma**: If two adjacent jobs $J_a, J_b$ with $d_a > d_b$ and $C_a \le C_b$ are swapped, the maximum lateness of the resulting schedule is at most that of the original. (This is proven by direct calculation: the swap moves $J_b$ earlier by $C_a$ and $J_a$ later by $C_b - C_a$; since $d_a > d_b$, the gain in $J_b$'s lateness exceeds the loss in $J_a$'s by at least $C_a - C_b \le 0$, with ties broken carefully.)

**Step 4 — Inversion Removal**: By induction on the number of inversions, we can transform any minimum-$L_{max}$ schedule into one with no inversions, which must be the EDF order. The $L_{max}$ value is preserved.

**Step 5 — Conclusion**: Since EDF has no inversions and any minimum schedule can be brought to EDF form, **EDF minimizes $L_{max}$**.

$$\blacksquare$$

**(7 marks: theorem statement 1M, inversion definition 1M, exchange lemma 2M, inversion removal 1M, conclusion 2M)**

---

### Question B — Horn's Algorithm with Precedence (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]

Consider 7 unit-time tasks with the following precedence constraints:

$T_1 \prec T_4, \; T_1 \prec T_5, \; T_2 \prec T_5, \; T_2 \prec T_6, \; T_3 \prec T_6, \; T_4 \prec T_7, \; T_5 \prec T_7, \; T_6 \prec T_7$.

#### (a) Construct the precedence DAG and find the longest chain. (7 marks)

**Model Solution**:

**Step 1 — Build DAG**: Nodes $T_1, \dots, T_7$ with edges as listed.

**Step 2 — Identify Chains**:

- $T_1 \to T_4 \to T_7$ (length 3)
- $T_1 \to T_5 \to T_7$ (length 3)
- $T_2 \to T_5 \to T_7$ (length 3)
- $T_2 \to T_6 \to T_7$ (length 3)
- $T_3 \to T_6 \to T_7$ (length 3)

All maximal chains have length 3.

**Step 3 — Longest chain length**:

$$H(\tau) = 3$$

**Step 4 — Compute $h_i$ for each task**:

- $h_1 = 1, h_2 = 1, h_3 = 1$ (sources)
- $h_4 = h_1 + 1 = 2$
- $h_5 = \max(h_1, h_2) + 1 = 2$
- $h_6 = \max(h_2, h_3) + 1 = 2$
- $h_7 = \max(h_4, h_5, h_6) + 1 = 3$

**(7 marks: DAG construction 2M, chain identification 2M, longest chain 1M, $h_i$ values 2M)**

#### (b) Apply Horn's algorithm to obtain an optimal schedule. Compute $L^*$. (7 marks)

**Model Solution**:

**Step 1 — Compute $t_i = H(\tau) - h_i$**:

- $t_1 = 3 - 1 = 2$
- $t_2 = 3 - 1 = 2$
- $t_3 = 3 - 1 = 2$
- $t_4 = 3 - 2 = 1$
- $t_5 = 3 - 2 = 1$
- $t_6 = 3 - 2 = 1$
- $t_7 = 3 - 3 = 0$

**Step 2 — Schedule from slot 2 down to slot 0**:

- **Slot 2**: $T_1, T_2, T_3$ (all at level 2, no predecessors).
- **Slot 1**: $T_4, T_5, T_6$ (all at level 1, predecessors $T_1, T_1/T_2, T_2/T_3$ are all in slot 2 ✓).
- **Slot 0**: $T_7$ (level 0, predecessors $T_4, T_5, T_6$ all in slot 1 ✓).

| Slot 0 | Slot 1 | Slot 2 |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $T_7$ | $T_4, T_5, T_6$ | $T_1, T_2, T_3$ |

**Step 3 — Verify Optimality**:

$$L^* = H(\tau) = 3 \text{ time units}$$

**Feasibility Check**: At every time $s \in \{0, 1, 2\}$, the set of scheduled jobs has all its predecessors scheduled in strictly earlier slots. Hence precedence is respected.

**Conclusion**: The schedule is optimal with **makespan $L^* = 3$**.

**(7 marks: $t_i$ computation 2M, scheduling 3M, optimality verification 2M)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing EDF with Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)**: EDF is for *aperiodic* tasks with absolute deadlines. RMS is for *periodic* tasks with relative deadlines. Mixing them up = 0 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the feasibility inequality direction**: The Hall-type condition is $\sum_{i: d_i \le t} C_i \le t$, **not** $\ge t$. Inverting it costs the entire problem.
> 3. **In Horn's algorithm, computing $h_i$ incorrectly**: $h_i$ is the length of the **longest chain ending at $T_i$** (in node count, with sources having $h = 1$). Using edge count or shortest path will produce wrong schedules.
> 4. **Missing the "backward" nature of Horn's**: You schedule from $H(\tau) - 1$ **down to** $0$, not from $0$ upward. Forward scheduling is not guaranteed to respect precedences.
> 5. **Confusing makespan with total processing time**: $L^* = H(\tau)$ for unit-time tasks; for arbitrary processing times, the lower bound is the **critical path length** (sum of $C_i$ along the longest path), not $H(\tau)$.
> 6. **Not stating the optimality condition explicitly**: Examiners want to see the words *"Jackson's rule minimizes $L_{max}$"* and *"Horn's theorem: $L^* = H(\tau)$"*. Merely constructing the schedule without naming the theorem costs 1–2 marks.
> 7. **Forgetting the empty-set feasibility**: $t = 0$ must satisfy the inequality trivially ($\sum = 0 \le 0$), which is a free check students often skip.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3 (Aperiodic & Precedence Scheduling)**

### Jackson's Algorithm Essentials
- Rule: **Earliest Deadline First (EDF)** — schedule the ready job with the **smallest deadline**.
- Optimality metric: **minimizes $L_{max} = \max(f_i - d_i)$**.
- Applies to: **single processor, independent jobs, $r_i = 0$** (or preemptive EDF for arbitrary $r_i$).
- Feasibility condition: $\sum_{i: d_i \le t} C_i \le t, \forall t \ge 0$.
- Time complexity: $O(n \log n)$ for sorting, $O(n)$ for construction.

### Horn's Algorithm Essentials
- Setting: **unit execution time** ($C_i = 1$), **precedence constraints** (DAG).
- Horn's theorem: $L^* = H(\tau)$, where $H(\tau)$ is the **size of the largest chain**.
- Key quantities: $h_i$ = longest chain ending at $T_i$ (sources have $h_i = 1$); $t_i = H(\tau) - h_i$.
- Construction: **schedule from slot $H(\tau) - 1$ down to slot 0**, placing task $T_i$ at slot $t_i$.
- Time complexity: $O(n + e)$ for graph setup, $O(n \log n)$ for sorting.

### Scheduling with Precedence Constraints
- **Critical Path**: the longest path in the DAG (sum of $C_i$) is a lower bound.
- **List Scheduling** heuristic: $L_{list} \le (2 - 1/m) L_{opt}$ for $m$ machines.
- **Preemptive LDF**: optimal for single processor with arbitrary processing times and precedence.
- General problem: **NP-hard** for $m \ge 2$ machines with arbitrary $C_i$.
- **Hu's bound** for $m$ processors (unit tasks): $L^* \ge \lceil H(\tau) / m \rceil$.

### Must-Memorize Formulas
- $L_{max} = \max_i (f_i - d_i)$
- $\sum_{i: d_i \le t} C_i \le t \quad \forall t$
- $L^* = H(\tau)$ (unit-time + precedence)
- $L^* \ge \max_{\text{path } P} \sum_{i \in P} C_i$ (general precedence)
- $t_i = H(\tau) - h_i$ (Horn's slot assignment)

### Common Pitfalls to Avoid
- Don't confuse aperiodic (this module) with periodic (Module 2 / RMS).
- Don't forget to **state the optimality theorem** by name.
- Don't schedule Horn's tasks from $0$ upward — always go **backwards** from $H(\tau) - 1$.
- Don't use the wrong chain definition — chains are *totally ordered* under $\prec$, not just paths in the graph.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Aperiodic Task Scheduling & Precedence Constraints**
