# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Periodic Task Model — Foundation

In Real-Time Operating Systems (RTOS), a **periodic task** is a recurring computational job that is triggered at fixed regular intervals. Formally, a periodic task $\tau_i$ is characterized by a tuple of two parameters:

$$\tau_i \;=\; (p_i,\; e_i)$$

where:
- **$p_i$** → Period (the time interval between two successive activations/instances of the task). It also represents the **relative deadline** $D_i$ for hard real-time periodic tasks.
- **$e_i$** → Worst-case execution time (WCET) — the maximum CPU time required to complete one instance of the task without interruption.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A periodic task is said to be *schedulable* if the OS can guarantee that **every instance of the task completes its execution before its deadline**, even under worst-case load conditions. The processor utilization for task $\tau_i$ is therefore:
> $$U_i \;=\; \frac{e_i}{p_i}$$
> The total system utilization is the sum of all individual utilizations:
> $$U_{total} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{e_i}{p_i}$$
> For a single-processor system to be **theoretically feasible**, the necessary condition is $U_{total} \leq 1$ (i.e., the CPU cannot be loaded beyond **100%**).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Hospital Triage Analogy"

Imagine a hospital emergency room with a single doctor (the **CPU**) and many incoming patients (**periodic tasks**):
- A patient with a **heart attack** has the *earliest deadline* — they must be treated *immediately* (highest urgency).
- A patient with a **mild fever** has a far-off deadline — they can wait a long time.

The doctor's job is to ensure **no patient dies before being treated** — i.e., **no deadline is missed**. This is precisely the scheduling problem in RTOS.

## 1.3 What is EDF (Earliest Deadline First)?

**EDF** is a **dynamic-priority, preemptive** scheduling algorithm. At any instant in time, the task whose **absolute deadline is closest** is given the CPU.

- **Priority is assigned dynamically** — the task with the earliest upcoming deadline becomes the highest-priority task.
- **Preemptive** — if a new task arrives with an earlier deadline than the currently running task, the current task is immediately suspended and the new one starts.
- **Optimality:** EDF is *provably optimal* for **preemptive** scheduling on a **single processor** under the condition that all tasks are *independent* and the system utilization does not exceed 1.

## 1.4 What is LDF (Latest Deadline First)?

**LDF** is the **dual** of EDF. It is primarily a **non-preemptive, offline** scheduling strategy.

- The task with the **latest (farthest) deadline** is scheduled **first** in the available idle window.
- **Why use LDF?** When tasks cannot be preempted (e.g., due to hardware limitations, shared non-reentrant resources, or simple microcontrollers), EDF is no longer optimal. In such cases, LDF helps in *minimizing the maximum lateness*.
- **Schedule construction is offline** — the entire schedule is computed before runtime.

## 1.5 Comparison at a Glance

| Feature | EDF | LDF |
|---|---|---|
| Scheduling type | Dynamic-priority, preemptive | Non-preemptive (usually) |
| Selection rule | Task with earliest deadline first | Task with latest deadline first |
| Optimality | Optimal for preemptive uniprocessor | Minimizes max-lateness for non-preemptive |
| Runtime decisions | Online (at every arrival/completion) | Offline schedule generation |
| Use case | Complex RTOS (VxWorks, RTEMS) | Resource-constrained / no preemption |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** In board exams, students often confuse LDF and EDF. Remember the mnemonic — **"EDF saves lives" (urgent things first)** vs. **"LDF plans" (long-term time-table first)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Periodic Task Behavior — Step-by-Step Working

A periodic task $\tau_i = (p_i, e_i)$ releases a series of **jobs** (or task instances) at times $k \cdot p_i$ for $k = 0, 1, 2, \dots$.

- The $k^{th}$ job of $\tau_i$ arrives at time $r_{i,k} = k \cdot p_i$
- Its absolute deadline is $d_{i,k} = (k+1) \cdot p_i$
- It must receive $e_i$ units of CPU time before $d_{i,k}$

### 2.1.1 The "Critical Instance" Concept

A **critical instant** for a task $\tau_i$ is the instant of time at which the release of a job of $\tau_i$ will produce the **largest possible response time**. For independent periodic tasks, the critical instant occurs when all tasks release their jobs **simultaneously** (at $t = 0$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU students must remember: **the worst-case response time of any task is found by simulating the schedule starting at the critical instant**.

## 2.2 EDF — Detailed Operational Logic

1. At every scheduling decision point (task arrival or task completion), recompute priorities.
2. The ready queue is re-sorted in **ascending order of absolute deadlines**.
3. The job with the **smallest $d_{i,k}$** is dispatched to the CPU.
4. If a new job arrives with an earlier deadline than the currently running job, **preemption** occurs.

### 2.2.1 Schedulability Test for EDF

A set of independent preemptable periodic tasks scheduled by EDF on a single processor is **schedulable if and only if**:

$$U_{total} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{e_i}{p_i} \;\leq\; 1$$

This is a **necessary and sufficient** condition (Liu & Layland, 1973).

## 2.3 LDF — Detailed Operational Logic

1. The hyperperiod $H = \text{LCM}(p_1, p_2, \dots, p_n)$ is computed.
2. Within $[0, H]$, the scheduler *back-schedules* — it leaves a buffer equal to the *latest* deadline and places tasks right before that buffer.
3. Equivalent algorithmic rule: at each scheduling point, choose the task with the **largest** $(d_{i,k} - \text{current\_time})$.

### 2.3.1 LDF Optimality for Non-Preemptive Minimization of Lateness

LDF minimizes the maximum lateness $L_{max}$ where:

$$L_{max} \;=\; \max_{i,k} \left(f_{i,k} - d_{i,k}\right)$$

Here $f_{i,k}$ is the actual completion time of job $(i,k)$ and $d_{i,k}$ is its deadline. If $L_{max} \leq 0$, **all deadlines are met**.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Equation | Meaning / Condition |
|---|---|---|
| Processor utilization of task $\tau_i$ | $U_i = \dfrac{e_i}{p_i}$ | Fraction of CPU consumed by $\tau_i$ |
| Total system utilization | $U_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{e_i}{p_i}$ | Sum of utilizations of all tasks |
| EDF schedulability (single processor, preemptive) | $U_{total} \leq 1$ | Necessary **and** sufficient |
| Hyperperiod | $H = \text{LCM}(p_1, p_2, \dots, p_n)$ | The pattern repeats every $H$ units |
| Number of jobs of $\tau_i$ in hyperperiod | $N_i = \dfrac{H}{p_i}$ | Total instances generated in one cycle |
| Maximum lateness | $L_{max} = \max (f_{i,k} - d_{i,k})$ | Worst-case delay beyond any deadline |
| Critical instant condition | All tasks released at $t=0$ | Worst-case launch scenario |
| Deadline-miss condition | $L_{max} > 0$ | Schedule is **not feasible** |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace & Avionics:** Satellite control systems use **EDF** to guarantee that attitude-control tasks (which have very tight deadlines) always get the CPU before communication-handling tasks.
- **Automotive ECUs (Engine Control Units):** Fuel-injection tasks are periodic and must complete within milliseconds — EDF guarantees the worst-case behavior is bounded.
- **Industrial PLCs and Embedded Microcontrollers:** Many simple microcontrollers do **not support preemption**. Hence **LDF (non-preemptive, offline)** becomes the only viable deterministic scheduler.
- **Medical Devices:** Pacemakers and infusion pumps use EDF-derivatives to ensure life-critical deadlines are never missed.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Deriving the EDF Schedulability Bound

**Given:** A set of $n$ independent periodic tasks with parameters $(p_i, e_i)$ on a single preemptive processor.

**To prove:** If $U_{total} \leq 1$, then the system is schedulable under EDF. Conversely, if the system is schedulable, $U_{total} \leq 1$.

### 3.1.1 Sufficiency ($U_{total} \leq 1 \Rightarrow$ Schedulable)

**Step 1:** Define the demand $h(t)$ at time $t$ — the total execution time demanded by all task instances with deadline $\leq t$.

$$h(t) \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left\lfloor \frac{t + p_i - D_i}{p_i} \right\rfloor \cdot e_i$$

For periodic tasks with $D_i = p_i$, this simplifies to:

$$h(t) \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left\lfloor \frac{t}{p_i} \right\rfloor \cdot e_i$$

**Step 2:** A schedule is feasible **if and only if** for all $t \geq 0$:

$$h(t) \;\leq\; t$$

**Step 3:** The processor must service $h(t)$ within window $t$. Since the demand grows monotonically and the supply $t$ grows linearly, the *binding* constraint is at $t = H$ (hyperperiod). If $U_{total} \leq 1$, then:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{e_i}{p_i} \;\leq\; 1 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{e_i \cdot H}{p_i} \;\leq\; H \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; h(H) \;\leq\; H$$

Hence the schedule is feasible. $\blacksquare$

### 3.1.2 Necessity (Schedulable $\Rightarrow U_{total} \leq 1$)

**Step 1:** Suppose the schedule is feasible but $U_{total} > 1$.

**Step 2:** The cumulative work demanded in $[0, H]$ is:

$$W_{demand} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i \cdot e_i \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{H}{p_i} \cdot e_i \;=\; H \cdot U_{total}$$

**Step 3:** The maximum cumulative work the processor can do in $[0, H]$ is at most $H$ (since it has only one unit of time per unit time).

**Step 4:** Therefore $U_{total} \leq 1$ is required. Contradiction resolved. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Critical Instant for Periodic Tasks

**Given:** A periodic task set with all tasks released simultaneously at $t=0$.

**Step 1:** At $t=0$, the ready queue contains $n$ jobs, one of each task. The processor selects the one with the earliest deadline.

**Step 2:** A new job of $\tau_i$ arrives every $p_i$ units. The **$k^{th}$** arrival of $\tau_i$ is at $r_{i,k} = k \cdot p_i$.

**Step 3:** The response time $R_{i,k}$ of the $k^{th}$ job is:

$$R_{i,k} \;=\; f_{i,k} - r_{i,k}$$

**Step 4:** The maximum response time across all jobs of $\tau_i$ is the **worst-case response time**:

$$R_i^{max} \;=\; \max_{k \geq 0} R_{i,k}$$

**Step 5:** For simultaneous release (critical instant), the $0^{th}$ job of $\tau_i$ experiences the **most interference** because all other tasks also have pending jobs. Hence:

$$R_i^{max} \;=\; R_{i,0} \quad \text{(at the critical instant)}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example

**Problem:** Consider three periodic tasks:
- $\tau_1 = (p_1 = 4, e_1 = 1)$
- $\tau_2 = (p_2 = 5, e_2 = 2)$
- $\tau_3 = (p_3 = 20, e_3 = 3)$

**Step 1:** Compute individual utilizations.

$$U_1 \;=\; \frac{1}{4} \;=\; 0.25$$
$$U_2 \;=\; \frac{2}{5} \;=\; 0.40$$
$$U_3 \;=\; \frac{3}{20} \;=\; 0.15$$

**Step 2:** Compute total utilization.

$$U_{total} \;=\; 0.25 + 0.40 + 0.15 \;=\; 0.80$$

**Step 3:** Apply the EDF schedulability test.

$$U_{total} = 0.80 \;\leq\; 1 \quad \text{(Schedulable under EDF)}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the hyperperiod.

$$H \;=\; \text{LCM}(4, 5, 20) \;=\; 20 \text{ units}$$

**Step 5:** Determine the number of jobs per task in one hyperperiod.

$$N_1 \;=\; \frac{20}{4} \;=\; 5 \text{ jobs}$$
$$N_2 \;=\; \frac{20}{5} \;=\; 4 \text{ jobs}$$
$$N_3 \;=\; \frac{20}{20} \;=\; 1 \text{ job}$$

**Step 6:** Total CPU demand over the hyperperiod.

$$W_{demand} \;=\; (5 \times 1) + (4 \times 2) + (1 \times 3) \;=\; 5 + 8 + 3 \;=\; 16 \text{ units}$$

**Step 7:** Slack (idle time available) over the hyperperiod.

$$W_{slack} \;=\; H - W_{demand} \;=\; 20 - 16 \;=\; 4 \text{ units}$$

**Step 8:** Construct the EDF schedule for $[0, 20]$ — at every instant, pick the job with the **earliest deadline** (relative deadline = period for periodic tasks).

A typical EDF dispatch sequence would be: $\tau_2, \tau_1, \tau_1, \tau_2, \tau_1, \tau_1, \tau_2, \tau_3, \tau_2, \tau_1, \dots$ — the order adapts dynamically as new jobs arrive.

---

## 3.4 LDF Schedule Construction (Non-Preemptive, Offline)

**Same task set, but preemption is forbidden.**

**Step 1:** Generate all job instances in $[0, 20]$.

| Job | Arrival $r$ | Absolute Deadline $d$ | Execution $e$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\tau_{1,0}$ | 0 | 4 | 1 |
| $\tau_{2,0}$ | 0 | 5 | 2 |
| $\tau_{1,1}$ | 4 | 8 | 1 |
| $\tau_{1,2}$ | 8 | 12 | 1 |
| $\tau_{2,1}$ | 5 | 10 | 2 |
| $\tau_{1,3}$ | 12 | 16 | 1 |
| $\tau_{2,2}$ | 10 | 15 | 2 |
| $\tau_{2,3}$ | 15 | 20 | 2 |
| $\tau_{1,4}$ | 16 | 20 | 1 |
| $\tau_{3,0}$ | 0 | 20 | 3 |

**Step 2:** LDF rule: at each scheduling point, pick the job with the **largest** $d$.

**Step 3:** One valid LDF order (largest $d$ first): $\tau_{3,0}$ (3 units) → $\tau_{1,4}$ (1 unit) → $\tau_{2,3}$ (2 units) ... — built backwards from latest deadlines.

**Step 4:** Validate $L_{max} \leq 0$ to confirm feasibility. LDF minimizes (not necessarily eliminates) lateness.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Periodic Task Lifecycle — Job Generation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Periodic Task Tau i] --> B[Release Job at r = k * p sub i]
    B --> C[Place in Ready Queue]
    C --> D{Scheduler Decision Point}
    D -->|EDF| E[Select Job with min d sub i,k]
    D -->|LDF| F[Select Job with max d sub i,k]
    E --> G[Dispatch to CPU]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Execute for e sub i units]
    H --> I{Completed before deadline d sub i,k?}
    I -->|Yes| J[Success - Deadline Met]
    I -->|No| K[Deadline Miss - System Failure]
```

## 4.2 EDF vs. LDF — Comparative Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Ready Queue with n Jobs] --> B{Scheduling Algorithm}
    B -->|EDF Dynamic Priority| C[Sort by Ascending Absolute Deadline]
    C --> D[Pick Job with Smallest d]
    D --> E[Execute Preemptively]
    E --> F[Re-evaluate on Arrival or Completion]
    F --> B

    B -->|LDF Offline Schedule| G[Sort by Descending Absolute Deadline]
    G --> H[Pick Job with Largest d]
    H --> I[Execute Non Preemptively]
    I --> J[Move to Next Slot in Static Table]
    J --> K[Repeat Until Hyperperiod Complete]
```

## 4.3 Critical Instant and Worst-Case Response Time Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T0[Time t = 0: All Tasks Released Simultaneously] --> P1[Job 1 of Tau 1 Starts]
    T0 --> P2[Job 1 of Tau 2 Starts]
    T0 --> P3[Job 1 of Tau 3 Starts]
    P1 --> Q1{CPU Decides Priority}
    P2 --> Q1
    P3 --> Q1
    Q1 -->|EDF| R1[Earliest Deadline Job Runs]
    Q1 -->|LDF| R2[Latest Deadline Job Runs]
    R1 --> S[Response Times Computed]
    R2 --> S
    S --> T[Max Response Time is Worst Case R sub i sup max]
    T --> U{R sub i sup max is less than or equal to D sub i?}
    U -->|Yes| V[Schedulable - System Safe]
    U -->|No| W[Not Schedulable - Must Re-Design]
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture Block Diagram — EDF Scheduler

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Periodic Task Set Input] --> B[Task Parameter Table p sub i, e sub i]
    B --> C[Compute Total Utilization U total]
    C --> D{U total less than or equal to 1}
    D -->|No| E[Reject Task Set - Overload]
    D -->|Yes| F[Initialize Ready Queue]
    F --> G[Event: Task Arrival or Completion]
    G --> H[Insert Job into Ready Queue]
    H --> I[Sort Queue by Absolute Deadline Ascending]
    I --> J[Dispatch Head of Queue to CPU]
    J --> K[Update System Time]
    K --> G
    K --> L[End: Hyperperiod H Reached]
    L --> M[Output: Schedule Trace and Slack Time]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Define a periodic task. What are the parameters used to characterize a periodic task?**

**Model Answer:**
A periodic task is a real-time job that is activated repeatedly at regular intervals of time. It is characterized by two parameters:
- **Period ($p_i$):** The fixed time interval between two consecutive activations of the task. It also represents the relative deadline for hard real-time periodic tasks.
- **Worst-case execution time ($e_i$):** The maximum CPU time required to complete one instance (job) of the task.

The processor utilization of $\tau_i$ is $U_i = e_i / p_i$. A system of $n$ periodic tasks is feasible on a single processor if the total utilization $U_{total} = \sum e_i/p_i \leq 1$. (3 marks)

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Q: Distinguish between EDF and LDF scheduling algorithms.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | EDF | LDF |
|---|---|---|
| Full form | Earliest Deadline First | Latest Deadline First |
| Type | Dynamic priority, preemptive | Non-preemptive (typically), offline |
| Selection rule | Task with earliest absolute deadline | Task with latest absolute deadline |
| Optimality | Optimal for preemptive uniprocessor | Minimizes maximum lateness in non-preemptive case |
| Decision time | Online (at runtime) | Offline (schedule computed before runtime) |

(3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Consider three periodic real-time tasks with the following parameters:**
- $\tau_1 : p_1 = 4, e_1 = 1$
- $\tau_2 : p_2 = 5, e_2 = 1$
- $\tau_3 : p_3 = 20, e_3 = 2$

**(a)** Compute the total utilization and determine if the task set is **schedulable under EDF**. Justify your answer. (7 marks)

**(b)** Compute the hyperperiod and the number of jobs generated by each task in one hyperperiod. Draw the EDF schedule for the hyperperiod. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) Utilization Analysis:**

$$U_1 \;=\; \frac{e_1}{p_1} \;=\; \frac{1}{4} \;=\; 0.25$$
$$U_2 \;=\; \frac{e_2}{p_2} \;=\; \frac{1}{5} \;=\; 0.20$$
$$U_3 \;=\; \frac{e_3}{p_3} \;=\; \frac{2}{20} \;=\; 0.10$$
$$U_{total} \;=\; 0.25 + 0.20 + 0.10 \;=\; 0.55$$

**Step 2 — Apply EDF Schedulability Test:**

$$U_{total} = 0.55 \;\leq\; 1.00 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \text{Task set is SCHEDULABLE under EDF.}$$

Justification: EDF is optimal for preemptive uniprocessor scheduling of independent periodic tasks; the condition $U_{total} \leq 1$ is both necessary and sufficient. (7 marks)

---

**(b) Hyperperiod and Schedule Construction:**

**Step 1 — Hyperperiod:**

$$H \;=\; \text{LCM}(4, 5, 20) \;=\; 20 \text{ units}$$

**Step 2 — Job Counts:**

$$N_1 \;=\; \frac{20}{4} \;=\; 5 \text{ jobs of } \tau_1$$
$$N_2 \;=\; \frac{20}{5} \;=\; 4 \text{ jobs of } \tau_2$$
$$N_3 \;=\; \frac{20}{20} \;=\; 1 \text{ job of } \tau_3$$

**Step 3 — EDF Schedule for [0, 20]:**

| Time Window | Active Tasks (after arrival) | Earliest Deadline | Job Executed |
|---|---|---|---|
| $[0, 1)$ | $\tau_1, \tau_2, \tau_3$ | $\tau_1$ (d=4) | $\tau_1$ |
| $[1, 2)$ | $\tau_2, \tau_3$ | $\tau_2$ (d=5) | $\tau_2$ |
| $[2, 3)$ | $\tau_2, \tau_3$ | $\tau_2$ (d=5) | $\tau_2$ |
| $[3, 4)$ | $\tau_3$ | $\tau_3$ (d=20) | $\tau_3$ |
| $[4, 5)$ | $\tau_1, \tau_3$ | $\tau_1$ (d=8) | $\tau_1$ |
| $[5, 6)$ | $\tau_2, \tau_3$ | $\tau_2$ (d=10) | $\tau_2$ |
| $[6, 7)$ | $\tau_3$ | $\tau_3$ | $\tau_3$ |
| $[7, 8)$ | idle / $\tau_3$ if not done | – | – |
| $[8, 9)$ | $\tau_1$ (d=12) | $\tau_1$ | $\tau_1$ |
| $[10, 11)$ | $\tau_2$ (d=15) | $\tau_2$ | $\tau_2$ |
| $[12, 13)$ | $\tau_1$ (d=16) | $\tau_1$ | $\tau_1$ |
| $[15, 17)$ | $\tau_2$ (d=20) | $\tau_2$ | $\tau_2$ |
| $[16, 17)$ | $\tau_1$ (d=20) | $\tau_1$ | $\tau_1$ |
| $[19, 20)$ | idle | – | – |

(7 marks)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Q: (a)** What is a periodic task? Explain the **critical instant** concept with reference to periodic task scheduling. Why is the critical instant important for schedulability analysis? (7 marks)

**(b)** With a suitable example, explain the **LDF (Latest Deadline First)** scheduling algorithm. Mention one real-world scenario where LDF is preferred over EDF. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) Periodic Task and Critical Instant:**

A **periodic task** $\tau_i$ is defined by the tuple $(p_i, e_i)$ where $p_i$ is the period and $e_i$ is the worst-case execution time. A job of $\tau_i$ is released at $r_{i,k} = k \cdot p_i$ and has absolute deadline $d_{i,k} = (k+1) \cdot p_i$.

**Critical Instant Definition:** A critical instant of a task $\tau_i$ is the time instant at which the release of a job of $\tau_i$ results in the **largest response time** for that job.

For independent periodic tasks, the critical instant is proved to occur when **all higher-priority tasks release their jobs simultaneously** with $\tau_i$ at $t = 0$. The reason is that simultaneous release maximizes the cumulative interference that $\tau_i$'s job must endure before it gets the CPU.

**Importance for Schedulability:**
- The worst-case response time $R_i^{max}$ is found at the critical instant.
- A task is schedulable if and only if $R_i^{max} \leq D_i$ at the critical instant.
- It reduces an *infinite-horizon* problem to a *bounded simulation* over one hyperperiod.

(7 marks)

---

**(b) LDF Scheduling with Example:**

**LDF (Latest Deadline First)** is a non-preemptive scheduling algorithm. At each scheduling decision point, the scheduler chooses the job whose **absolute deadline is the farthest** in the future.

**Example:** Consider three jobs with execution times and deadlines:

| Job | Execution $e$ | Deadline $d$ |
|---|---|---|
| $J_1$ | 1 | 4 |
| $J_2$ | 2 | 6 |
| $J_3$ | 1 | 10 |

**LDF Order (latest deadline first):**
1. $J_3$ (d=10) at $t=0$ → finishes at $t=1$
2. $J_2$ (d=6) at $t=1$ → finishes at $t=3$
3. $J_1$ (d=4) at $t=3$ → finishes at $t=4$

**Validation:** All completion times $f_i \leq d_i$, hence $L_{max} = 0$. The schedule is feasible and optimal under LDF.

**Real-world scenario where LDF is preferred over EDF:**
- **Non-preemptive embedded microcontrollers** (e.g., 8051, low-end ARM Cortex-M0 in automotive sensor nodes). Such controllers lack hardware/OS support for preemption. Since EDF's optimality collapses without preemption, LDF (which constructs an offline, static schedule table) becomes the deterministic choice.
- **Industrial PLCs** controlling assembly lines where interrupting a motor-actuation task mid-way would cause physical damage — hence non-preemptability is mandatory.

(7 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing relative and absolute deadlines** — Relative deadline $D_i$ is the gap from arrival; absolute deadline $d_{i,k} = r_{i,k} + D_i$. For periodic tasks, $D_i = p_i$ by default.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert the LCM** — When computing the hyperperiod, students often write the GCD instead. Always remember: **hyperperiod = LCM**, not GCD.
> 3. **Applying the wrong optimality condition** — EDF is optimal for *preemptive* uniprocessor; it is **not** optimal for multiprocessor or non-preemptive cases.
> 4. **Skipping the LDF in board answers** — Even if you answer a question on EDF, mentioning a 1-line contrast with LDF impresses the examiner and earns bonus half-marks.
> 5. **Hyperperiod simulation** — In long questions, students often leave the schedule table empty. Always draw the Gantt chart (or a time-windowed table) — this is where 3–4 marks are scored.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **periodic task** is fully described by $(p, e)$ — period and worst-case execution time.
- **Processor utilization** of a single task: $U = e/p$. **System utilization:** $U_{total} = \sum e_i/p_i$.
- **Hyperperiod** $H = \text{LCM}(p_1, p_2, \dots, p_n)$ is the time after which the schedule repeats identically.
- **EDF (Earliest Deadline First):** dynamic-priority, preemptive, optimal for single processor; feasibility test is $U_{total} \leq 1$ (necessary **and** sufficient).
- **LDF (Latest Deadline First):** non-preemptive, offline; minimizes maximum lateness; used when preemption is impossible.
- **Critical instant:** simultaneous release of all tasks at $t=0$ produces the worst-case response time — fundamental to schedulability proofs.
- **Slack time** in a hyperperiod $= H - W_{demand} = H(1 - U_{total})$.
- EDF fails the moment $U_{total}$ exceeds 1 — this is a hard physical limit of a single CPU.
- Real-world applications: aerospace (EDF), automotive ECUs (EDF), industrial PLCs and microcontrollers (LDF).
- For KTU board exams, always state the **schedulability test equation** *before* writing the schedule table.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS), Deadline Monotonic Scheduling (DMS), Real-Time Kernel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEIT785 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS), Deadline Monotonic Scheduling (DMS), Real-Time Kernel ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)** is a **fixed-priority preemptive scheduling algorithm** designed for real-time systems. It assigns priorities to tasks **inversely proportional to their periods** — the task with the **shortest period (highest execution frequency)** receives the **highest priority**. RMS was formalized by **Liu and Layland (1973)** in their seminal paper *"Scheduling Algorithms for Multiprogramming in a Hard Real-Time Environment"*, which remains the cornerstone of fixed-priority real-time scheduling theory referenced universally in KTU and academic curricula. **Formal Properties of RMS:** - **Static (Fixed) Priority Assignment** — priorities are assigned at design time and never change at runtime. - **Preemptive** — a higher-priority task can interrupt a lower-priority task at any instant. - **Independent Tasks** — no shared resources, no precedence constraints. - **Periodic Tasks** — each task arrives with a fixed period $T_i$. - **Deterministic Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET)** — $C_i$ is known a priori. > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy — The Hospital Emergency Triage System** > Imagine a hospital emergency ward where patients arrive at different frequencies. A patient with a critical heart condition (arrives every few minutes) must always be checked before a routine check-up patient (arrives monthly). RMS works exactly like this — it permanently ranks tasks by how *frequently* they need attention. The most frequent task is like a critical patient; it gets top priority permanently because missing its deadline would be catastrophic. ### 1.2 Deadline Monotonic Scheduling (DMS) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Deadline Monotonic Scheduling (DMS)** is a **fixed-priority preemptive scheduling algorithm** where priorities are assigned based on the **relative deadlines** of tasks — the task with the **shortest relative deadline** is assigned the **highest priority**. DMS was proposed by **Leung and Whitehead (1982)** as a generalization of RMS. When the relative deadline $D_i$ equals the period $T_i$, DMS becomes equivalent to RMS. When $D_i < T_i$ (constrained deadlines), DMS is provably optimal among fixed-priority algorithms. **When to use DMS over RMS:** - When $D_i \neq T_i$ (i.e., deadline is shorter than the period). - When task deadlines are arbitrary but fixed. - When $D_i > T_i$ — neither RMS nor DMS applies; use **Earliest Deadline First (EDF)**. > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy — The Airline Boarding Gate** > At an airport boarding gate, passengers are sometimes sorted by boarding deadline (flight departure time) rather than arrival frequency. A passenger whose flight leaves in 30 minutes (shorter deadline) gets priority over one whose flight leaves in 5 hours, even if the latter checks in more frequently. DMS mirrors this boarding-call hierarchy: urgency of deadline — not frequency — dictates priority order. ### 1.3 Real-Time Kernel > [!IMPORTANT] > **A Real-Time Kernel** is the **minimal core of a real-time operating system (RTOS)** that provides **bounded and deterministic** task management services — including scheduling, interrupt handling, inter-task communication, and timing — with **guaranteed worst-case response times**. A kernel is called *"real-time"* not because it is fast, but because its **worst-case latency is bounded, predictable, and known**. A real-time kernel typically exposes services such as: - **Task creation, deletion, suspension, resumption** - **Preemptive priority-based scheduling** - **Timing services** (delays, timeouts, ticks) - **Interrupt service routine (ISR) management** - **Synchronization primitives** (semaphores, mutexes, event flags, message queues) > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy — The Air Traffic Control Tower** > A real-time kernel is like an air traffic control tower. It doesn't fly the planes (your application code does), but it ensures that every "request" (takeoff, landing) gets handled in a predictable, prioritized, and time-bound manner. If a control tower takes 2 hours to respond, no matter how sophisticated, it is *not* real-time. What matters is that the response happens *within the deadline — every single time*. > [!TIP] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 focuses specifically on **fixed-priority scheduling theory (RMS & DMS)** and the **architectural foundations of a real-time kernel**. Dynamic scheduling algorithms (EDF, LLF) are covered in earlier modules. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Logic of RMS — Step by Step The RMS algorithm operates on the following deterministic principles: - **Priority Assignment:** Sort tasks so that $T_1 \le T_2 \le T_3 \le \dots \le T_n$. Task 1 (shortest period) gets the highest priority, task $n$ (longest period) gets the lowest priority. - **Scheduling Rule:** At any instant, the scheduler dispatches the **highest-priority ready task**. If a higher-priority task arrives while a lower-priority task runs, immediate preemption occurs. - **Optimality:** RMS is **optimal** among all *fixed-priority* algorithms — if any fixed-priority schedule exists, RMS will find one. - **Critical Instant:** The worst-case response time for any task occurs when **all higher-priority tasks are released simultaneously with the task itself** — this is Liu & Layland's *critical instant theorem*. - **Critical Zone:** The longest response time for task $i$ is bounded by the time interval $[a_i, a_i + T_i]$ where $a_i$ is the critical instant. ### 2.2 Operational Logic of DMS — Step by Step - **Priority Assignment:** Sort tasks so that $D_1 \le D_2 \le D_3 \le \dots \le D_n$. Task 1 (shortest deadline) gets highest priority. - **Applicability:** Suitable for tasks with **constrained deadlines** ($D_i \le T_i$) and arbitrary fixed deadlines. - **Relation to RMS:** When $D_i = T_i$ for all tasks, DMS reduces to RMS. - **Schedulability:** DMS is *optimal* among fixed-priority algorithms when deadlines are less than or equal to periods. ### 2.3 The Liu & Layland Utilization Bound The most celebrated result in fixed-priority real-time scheduling theory: > **Theorem (Liu & Layland, 1973):** A set of $n$ periodic independent tasks scheduled by RMS on a uniprocessor will **always meet all deadlines** if the total processor utilization satisfies: $$U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \le n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)$$ - For $n = 1$, bound = **100%** (one task can fully use the CPU). - For $n = 2$, bound ≈ **82.84%**. - For $n = 3$, bound ≈ **78.00%**. - As $n \to \infty$, bound $\to \ln(2) \approx$ **69.31%**. > [!WARNING] > The Liu & Layland bound is **sufficient but not necessary**. Utilization *above* the bound does **not** automatically mean tasks will miss deadlines — it merely removes the guarantee. Use **Response Time Analysis (RTA)** or **exact schedulability tests** for precise verification. ### 2.4 Response Time Analysis (RTA) — Exact Test Response Time Analysis, developed by **Audsley et al. (1993)**, provides an exact schedulability test for fixed-priority systems: $$R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j$$ A task $i$ is schedulable **if and only if** $R_i \le D_i$. The recurrence is solved iteratively starting from $R_i^{(0)} = C_i$ until convergence (i.e., $R_i^{(k+1)} = R_i^{(k)}$) or until $R_i^{(k)} > D_i$ (fail). ### 2.5 Real-Time Kernel Architecture A typical real-time kernel (such as µC/OS-II, VxWorks, FreeRTOS) follows a layered architecture: - **Hardware Layer:** CPU, timers, interrupt controller, memory. - **Kernel Core:** Scheduler, dispatcher, tick handler, context switcher. - **Service Layer:** Task management, time management, semaphore/mutex/queue services. - **Application Layer:** User tasks and ISRs. Key design philosophies: - **Minimize non-determinism:** Avoid unbounded loops, dynamic memory allocation in critical paths. - **Interrupt Latency Bound:** Time from interrupt assertion to ISR start. - **Context Switch Time:** Time to save/restore CPU registers. - **Preemption Latency:** Time from event to highest-priority task execution. ### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **#** | **Concept** | **Formula / Rule** | **Symbol Meaning** | **Applicability** | |-------|-------------|--------------------|--------------------|-------------------| | 1 | RMS Priority Rule | Shorter $T_i \Rightarrow$ Higher Priority | $T_i$ = Period of task $i$ | Static priority | | 2 | DMS Priority Rule | Shorter $D_i \Rightarrow$ Higher Priority | $D_i$ = Relative deadline | Static priority | | 3 | Processor Utilization | $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i}$ | $C_i$ = WCET, $T_i$ = Period | Any scheduler | | 4 | Liu & Layland Bound | $U \le n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)$ | $n$ = Number of tasks | RMS only | | 5 | Utilization Limit (∞) | $U \le \ln(2) \approx 0.6931$ | $\ln$ = Natural log | Large $n$ | | 6 | Hyperbolic Bound (Hyperbolic Test) | $\prod_{i=1}^{n} \left( 1 + \frac{C_i}{T_i} \right) \le 2$ | Product form bound | RMS, less pessimistic | | 7 | RTA Recurrence | $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j$ | $hp(i)$ = higher-priority tasks | Exact test | | 8 | Schedulability Condition | $R_i \le D_i$ | Response $\le$ Deadline | Exact test | | 9 | Deadline Relation | $D_i \le T_i$ (constrained) | Feasible for DMS/RMS | Constrained deadline | | 10 | Priority Index (RMS) | $i < j \iff T_i \le T_j$ | Lower index = higher priority | Task ordering | | 11 | Interrupt Latency | $L_{int} = L_{detect} + L_{ISR\_entry}$ | Time before ISR runs | Kernel design | | 12 | Context Switch Time | $T_{cs} = T_{save} + T_{restore}$ | Register save/restore | Kernel design | | 13 | Worst-Case Response | $R_i^{wc} = \max_{t \ge 0} (f_i(t) - t)$ | Max completion delay | Verification | | 14 | Processor Demand Test | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{D_i} \le 1$ | Demand-bound | EDF only | | 15 | Slack Time | $S_i(t) = D_i - (t - a_i) - C_i^{rem}$ | Remaining free time | Dynamic scheduling | > [!TIP] > **KTU Engineering Utility:** These formulas power schedulability analysis in **automotive ECUs (AUTOSAR)**, **avionics (DO-178C)**, **medical devices (FDA IEC 62304)**, and **industrial robotics**. Every safety-critical embedded system you interact with daily has been validated by these very equations. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Liu & Layland Utilization Bound We prove the bound $U \le n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ for RMS. Consider the **critical instant** scenario where all $n$ tasks arrive simultaneously at $t = 0$. **Step 1:** Define the time interval $L$ as the largest time such that the total demand in $[0, L]$ does not exceed $L$: $$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \left\lceil \frac{L}{T_i} \right\rceil C_i \le L$$ This is the **schedulability condition** at the critical instant. **Step 2:** Without loss of generality, normalize so that $T_n = 1$ (the longest period is the unit of measurement). Then $T_i \in [0, 1]$ for $1 \le i \le n$. **Step 3:** Substituting $T_n = 1$, we have $T_i = 1/k_i$ for some integer $k_i \ge 1$. The bound becomes: $$L = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left\lceil \frac{L}{T_i} \right\rceil C_i$$ **Step 4:** Using the harmonic worst-case $T_i = T_n / i$ — this represents the *most demanding* configuration for fixed-priority scheduling — the schedulable utilization peaks at: $$U_{bound} = n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)$$ **Step 5:** Evaluate for $n = 1, 2, 3, \infty$: $$ \begin{aligned} U_1 &= 1 \cdot (2^{1/1} - 1) = 1 \cdot (2 - 1) = 1.0000 \\ U_2 &= 2 \cdot (2^{1/2} - 1) = 2 \cdot (1.4142 - 1) = 0.8284 \\ U_3 &= 3 \cdot (2^{1/3} - 1) = 3 \cdot (1.2599 - 1) = 0.7797 \\ U_\infty &= \lim_{n \to \infty} n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6:** Evaluate the limit using the standard identity $\lim_{x \to 0} \frac{2^x - 1}{x} = \ln 2$. Substituting $x = 1/n$: $$ \begin{aligned} \lim_{n \to \infty} n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right) &= \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{2^{1/n} - 1}{1/n} \\ &= \left. \frac{d}{dx}(2^x - 1) \right|_{x=0} \\ &= 2^x \ln 2 \,\Big|_{x=0} \\ &= \ln 2 \approx 0.6931 \end{aligned} $$ Therefore, the asymptotic bound for RMS is **$\ln 2 \approx 69.31\%$**. ### 3.2 Worked Example — RMS Schedulability Test **Task Set $\tau$:** | Task $i$ | Execution Time $C_i$ | Period $T_i$ | Utilization $C_i/T_i$ | |----------|----------------------|--------------|------------------------| | $\tau_1$ | 1 ms | 4 ms | 0.25 | | $\tau_2$ | 2 ms | 6 ms | 0.3333 | | $\tau_3$ | 3 ms | 10 ms | 0.30 | **Step 1:** Verify RMS priority order — $T_1 = 4 < T_2 = 6 < T_3 = 10$ ✓ (already in RMS order). **Step 2:** Compute total utilization: $$U = \frac{1}{4} + \frac{2}{6} + \frac{3}{10} = 0.25 + 0.3333 + 0.30 = 0.8833$$ **Step 3:** Compare against Liu & Layland bound for $n = 3$: $$U_{bound} = 3(2^{1/3} - 1) \approx 0.7797$$ Since $0.8833 > 0.7797$, the Liu & Layland test **fails** — but this does not mean unschedulable; we need an exact test. **Step 4:** Apply Response Time Analysis (RTA) to verify exact schedulability. For $\tau_3$ (lowest priority), iteratively solve: $$ \begin{aligned} R_3^{(0)} &= C_3 = 3 \\ R_3^{(1)} &= 3 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 \\ &= 3 + (1)(1) + (1)(2) = 6 \\ R_3^{(2)} &= 3 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 \\ &= 3 + (2)(1) + (1)(2) = 7 \\ R_3^{(3)} &= 3 + \left\lceil \frac{7}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{7}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 \\ &= 3 + (2)(1) + (2)(2) = 9 \\ R_3^{(4)} &= 3 + \left\lceil \frac{9}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{9}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 \\ &= 3 + (3)(1) + (2)(2) = 10 \\ R_3^{(5)} &= 3 + \left\lceil \frac{10}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{10}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 \\ &= 3 + (3)(1) + (2)(2) = 10 \end{aligned} $$ **Convergence reached:** $R_3 = 10$ ms. **Step 5:** Compare with deadline $D_3 = T_3 = 10$ ms: $$R_3 = 10 \le D_3 = 10 \quad \checkmark \text{ Schedulable!}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Conclusion:** Although the Liu & Layland bound test fails, the **exact RTA test confirms schedulability**. This illustrates the **pessimism** of the Liu & Layland sufficient condition. ### 3.3 Worked Example — DMS with $D_i \ne T_i$ **Task Set:** | Task $i$ | $C_i$ (ms) | $T_i$ (ms) | $D_i$ (ms) | |----------|-----------|-----------|-----------| | $\tau_1$ | 1 | 6 | 4 | | $\tau_2$ | 2 | 8 | 6 | | $\tau_3$ | 3 | 12 | 8 | **DMS Priority Order:** Sort by $D_i$: $4 < 6 < 8$ → $\tau_1, \tau_2, \tau_3$. So the priority is **already in DMS order**. **Apply RTA for $\tau_3$:** $$ \begin{aligned} R_3^{(0)} &= 3 \\ R_3^{(1)} &= 3 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{8} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 1 + 2 = 6 \\ R_3^{(2)} &= 3 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{8} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 1 + 2 = 6 \end{aligned} $$ Convergence: $R_3 = 6$ ms. Compare with $D_3 = 8$ ms. Since $6 \le 8$, **schedulable** ✓. > [!TIP] > **KTU Board Note:** In KTU examinations, whenever you see $D_i \ne T_i$, default to DMS. Whenever $D_i = T_i$ for all tasks, RMS and DMS give identical results. ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix | **Parameter** | **RMS** | **DMS** | **EDF (Reference)** | |---------------|---------|---------|---------------------| | Priority Type | Static (fixed) | Static (fixed) | Dynamic | | Priority Basis | Period $T_i$ | Deadline $D_i$ | Absolute deadline | | Optimal among its class | Yes (fixed-priority) | Yes (when $D_i \le T_i$) | Yes (dynamic, uniprocessor) | | Utilization Bound | $n(2^{1/n}-1)$, $\le$ 0.6931 | Not closed-form; use RTA | 1.0 (full CPU) | | Runtime Overhead | Very low | Very low | Higher (sorting) | | Implementation Complexity | Simple | Simple | Moderate | | Applicable when $D_i > T_i$? | No | No | Yes | | Applicable when $D_i < T_i$? | Suboptimal | Optimal | Optimal | ## 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]** *Define Rate Monotonic Scheduling. State its key properties and the priority assignment rule.* **Model Answer:** Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) is a fixed-priority, preemptive scheduling algorithm for periodic real-time tasks. **(1 mark)** Its key properties are: (i) priorities are static, assigned at design time; (ii) it is preemptive; (iii) it assumes independent tasks with known WCET and periods. **(1 mark)** The priority rule is: *the task with the shortest period receives the highest priority*. Equivalently, if $T_i < T_j$, then priority$(i) >$ priority$(j)$. **(1 mark)** **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]** *Compare Rate Monotonic Scheduling and Deadline Monotonic Scheduling. When is DMS preferred over RMS?* **Model Answer:** RMS assigns priority based on period $T_i$; DMS assigns priority based on relative deadline $D_i$. **(1 mark)** When $D_i = T_i$ for all tasks, RMS and DMS are equivalent. **(1 mark)** DMS is preferred over RMS when deadlines are *shorter than periods* ($D_i < T_i$), because RMS would yield a suboptimal schedule in such cases, while DMS is provably optimal among fixed-priority algorithms. **(1 mark)** --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]` **Consider a real-time task set with three periodic tasks:** | Task | Execution Time $C_i$ | Period $T_i$ | Deadline $D_i$ | |------|----------------------|--------------|----------------| | $\tau_1$ | 2 ms | 6 ms | 5 ms | | $\tau_2$ | 3 ms | 10 ms | 8 ms | | $\tau_3$ | 4 ms | 15 ms | 12 ms | **(a)** Apply Rate Monotonic Scheduling. Determine the priority order and check if the task set passes the Liu & Layland utilization test. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Perform Response Time Analysis (RTA) for the lowest-priority task and verify whether all deadlines are met under RMS. Compare your observation with the Liu & Layland result. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution (a):** **Step 1 — RMS Priority Order:** Sort by period ascending: $T_1 = 6 < T_2 = 10 < T_3 = 15$ Therefore, priority: $\tau_1$ (highest) > $\tau_2$ > $\tau_3$ (lowest). **(2 marks)** **Step 2 — Compute Utilization:** $$ \begin{aligned} U &= \frac{C_1}{T_1} + \frac{C_2}{T_2} + \frac{C_3}{T_3} \\ &= \frac{2}{6} + \frac{3}{10} + \frac{4}{15} \\ &= 0.3333 + 0.3000 + 0.2667 \\ &= 0.9000 \end{aligned} $$ **(2 marks)** **Step 3 — Liu & Layland Bound for $n = 3$:** $$U_{bound} = 3 \cdot (2^{1/3} - 1) \approx 3 \cdot 0.2599 = 0.7797$$ Since $0.9000 > 0.7797$, the Liu & Layland sufficient test **FAILS**. The test is inconclusive. **(3 marks)** --- **Model Solution (b):** Apply RTA iteratively for $\tau_3$ (lowest priority). Higher-priority tasks: $\tau_1, \tau_2$. **Iteration 1:** $$R_3^{(1)} = C_3 + \left\lceil \frac{4}{6} \right\rceil C_1 + \left\lceil \frac{4}{10} \right\rceil C_2 = 4 + 1 \cdot 2 + 1 \cdot 3 = 9$$ **Iteration 2:** $$R_3^{(2)} = 4 + \left\lceil \frac{9}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 + \left\lceil \frac{9}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 3 = 4 + 2 \cdot 2 + 1 \cdot 3 = 11$$ **Iteration 3:** $$R_3^{(3)} = 4 + \left\lceil \frac{11}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 + \left\lceil \frac{11}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 3 = 4 + 2 \cdot 2 + 2 \cdot 3 = 14$$ **Iteration 4:** $$R_3^{(4)} = 4 + \left\lceil \frac{14}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 + \left\lceil \frac{14}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 3 = 4 + 3 \cdot 2 + 2 \cdot 3 = 16$$ **Iteration 5:** $$R_3^{(5)} = 4 + \left\lceil \frac{16}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 + \left\lceil \frac{16}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 3 = 4 + 3 \cdot 2 + 2 \cdot 3 = 16$$ Convergence: $R_3 = 16$ ms. Compare with deadline $D_3 = 12$ ms. Since $R_3 = 16 > D_3 = 12$, **$\tau_3$ MISSES its deadline — task set is NOT schedulable under RMS**. **(5 marks)** **Comparison:** RTA confirms unschedulability, while Liu & Layland only signaled a *suspicion*. RTA is the *exact* and more powerful test. **(2 marks)** --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2** `[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model]` **(a)** Explain the architecture and essential services of a Real-Time Kernel. Discuss interrupt latency, context switch time, and preemption latency with their impact on schedulability. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Apply Deadline Monotonic Scheduling (DMS) on the task set given below and verify schedulability using Response Time Analysis. Comment on why DMS differs from RMS in this case. | Task | $C_i$ | $T_i$ | $D_i$ | |------|-------|-------|-------| | $\tau_1$ | 1 ms | 8 ms | 3 ms | | $\tau_2$ | 2 ms | 12 ms | 5 ms | | $\tau_3$ | 2 ms | 20 ms | 10 ms | --- **Model Solution (a):** **Architecture of a Real-Time Kernel:** A real-time kernel is the minimal core of an RTOS providing deterministic services. Its layered architecture is: 1. **Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL):** CPU, timers, interrupt controllers, memory. 2. **Kernel Core:** Scheduler, dispatcher, tick handler, ISR manager, context switch logic. 3. **Service Layer:** Task management, timing, synchronization primitives (semaphores, mutexes, event flags, queues). 4. **Application Layer:** User tasks and ISRs. **(2 marks)** **Essential Services:** - Task creation, deletion, suspension, resumption. - Preemptive fixed-priority (or dynamic) scheduling. - Timing services (delays, timeouts, tick-based wake-ups). - ISR management with bounded entry/exit. - Inter-task communication (queues, mailboxes, semaphores). **(2 marks)** **Timing Metrics:** - **Interrupt Latency ($L_{int}$):** Time from interrupt assertion to first instruction of the ISR. Bounded by kernel's interrupt-disable windows. - **Context Switch Time ($T_{cs}$):** Time to save the running task's context (registers, PC, SP) and load the next task's context. Must be deterministic. - **Preemption Latency ($L_{pre}$):** Total time from event occurrence to highest-priority task executing: $L_{pre} = L_{int} + L_{ISR} + T_{cs}$ approximately. **(2 marks)** **Impact on Schedulability:** - All three metrics must be **bounded and known a priori** to construct the WCET $C_i$ used in schedulability tests. - Unbounded interrupt latency invalidates the Liu & Layland bound because the assumption of predictable execution fails. - Long context switch times reduce effective CPU utilization available for tasks. **(1 mark)** --- **Model Solution (b):** **Step 1 — DMS Priority Order:** Sort by deadline ascending: $D_1 = 3 < D_2 = 5 < D_3 = 10$ Therefore priority: $\tau_1 > \tau_2 > \tau_3$ (already in DMS order). **(2 marks)** **Step 2 — Compare with RMS:** RMS would order by period: $T_1 = 8 < T_2 = 12 < T_3 = 20$, so RMS would give the *same* priority. However, here $D_i < T_i$ for all tasks, so DMS is the **correct formal choice**. RMS becomes a heuristic approximation in this regime. **(2 marks)** **Step 3 — RTA for $\tau_3$:** $$ \begin{aligned} R_3^{(0)} &= 2 \\ R_3^{(1)} &= 2 + \left\lceil \frac{2}{8} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{2}{12} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 2 + 1 + 2 = 5 \\ R_3^{(2)} &= 2 + \left\lceil \frac{5}{8} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{5}{12} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 2 + 1 + 2 = 5 \end{aligned} $$ Convergence: $R_3 = 5$ ms. Since $R_3 = 5 \le D_3 = 10$, $\tau_3$ is schedulable. **(2 marks)** **Step 4 — Verify all tasks:** - $\tau_1$: $R_1 = C_1 = 1 \le 3$ ✓ - $\tau_2$: $R_2 = C_2 + \lceil 2/8 \rceil \cdot 1 = 2 + 1 = 3 \le 5$ ✓ - $\tau_3$: $R_3 = 5 \le 10$ ✓ **All tasks meet their deadlines — the task set is schedulable under DMS.** **(1 mark)** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Misapplying RMS to $D_i \ne T_i$ tasks:** Examiners deduct 2–3 marks if you blindly use RMS when deadlines differ from periods. Always check $D_i$ vs $T_i$ first. > 2. **Forgetting the ceiling brackets:** The RTA recurrence uses $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ (ceiling), not floor. Writing $\lfloor \cdot \rfloor$ loses 1 mark. > 3. **Halting iteration too early:** You must iterate until $R_i^{(k+1)} = R_i^{(k)}$. Stopping at iteration 1 loses 2 marks. > 4. **Confusing Liu & Layland bound as necessary:** Writing "tasks will definitely miss deadlines" because $U > U_{bound}$ is incorrect — the bound is *sufficient*, not *necessary*. > 5. **Ignoring critical instant assumption:** RTA analysis assumes critical instant (all higher-priority tasks released simultaneously). State this explicitly. > 6. **Wrong symbol notation:** Writing $P_i$ instead of $T_i$, or $E_i$ instead of $C_i$ — KTU expects consistent IEEE standard notation. > 7. **Missing the DMS vs RMS distinction:** A 14-mark question often gives $D_i < T_i$ to test whether you recognize DMS applicability. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3 (RMS / DMS / Real-Time Kernel)** - **RMS** assigns fixed priorities inversely proportional to **period** $T_i$. *(Shorter period = Higher priority)* - **DMS** assigns fixed priorities inversely proportional to **relative deadline** $D_i$. *(Shorter deadline = Higher priority)* - RMS ⊂ DMS (when $D_i = T_i$, both are identical). - DMS is **optimal** among fixed-priority algorithms when $D_i \le T_i$. - **Liu & Layland Bound:** $U \le n(2^{1/n} - 1)$; for large $n$, $U \le \ln 2 \approx 0.6931$. - The bound is **sufficient but NOT necessary** — failing the bound does not prove unschedulability. - **Exact schedulability** test = **Response Time Analysis (RTA)**. - RTA Recurrence: $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \lceil R_i / T_j \rceil \cdot C_j$. - Schedulable iff $R_i \le D_i$ for every task $i$. - RTA is solved **iteratively** from $R_i^{(0)} = C_i$ until convergence. - The **critical instant** for a fixed-priority task is when all higher-priority tasks are released simultaneously with it. - A **real-time kernel** is characterized by **bounded worst-case latency**, not by raw speed. - Essential kernel metrics: **interrupt latency**, **context switch time**, **preemption latency** — all must be deterministic. - Real-time kernels (µC/OS, FreeRTOS, VxWorks) provide: task mgmt, scheduler, dispatcher, ISR mgmt, timing services, sync primitives. - Avoid dynamic memory allocation, unbounded loops, and non-deterministic I/O inside kernel-critical paths. - **EDF** (dynamic priority) can achieve 100% utilization but has higher runtime overhead than RMS/DMS. - KTU frequently tests **numerical RTA problems** — practice the iterative computation until mastery. - Always state **assumptions** in your answer: independent tasks, periodic, known WCET, preemptive kernel. > [!NOTE] > **Mnemonic for Priority Rules — *"Short Wins"*:** > - RMS: **S**hortest period **W**ins **I**n **N**umerical **S**orting *(period)* > - DMS: **S**hortest deadline **W**ins *(deadline)* > - Both: **S**tatic = **S**ame throughout **S**ystem **S**tate *(fixed-priority)*

topics.title: Structure, State Transition Diagram and Kernel Primitives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT785 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Structure, State Transition Diagram and Kernel Primitives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Structure of an RTOS Kernel

### Formal Academic Definition
The **RTOS kernel** is the core software component that manages the system's resources, schedules real-time tasks, handles interrupts, and provides inter-task communication primitives. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, the kernel structure typically refers to the organization of critical sections, foreground/background systems, and the layered architecture comprising the **Scheduler**, **Dispatcher**, **Interrupt Handler**, and **Service Routines**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Tone):**
> A real-time kernel is the smallest piece of software that provides task scheduling, task creation, inter-task communication, and timing services. It is preemptive, deterministic, and forms the heart of any RTOS.

### Conceptual Analogy 🏢 — The Air Traffic Control Tower

Imagine a busy international airport:
- The **Control Tower (Kernel)** is the centralized authority.
- The **Airplanes (Tasks)** come and go, each with different priorities (emergency landing, commercial flight, cargo).
- The **Runway Scheduler (Scheduler)** decides which plane lands/takes off next.
- The **Ground Controller (Dispatcher)** physically instructs the chosen plane to act.
- The **Radio Operator (Interrupt Handler)** handles sudden priority signals (e.g., a Mayday call).

Just as a control tower must respond **deterministically and instantly** to incoming planes, an RTOS kernel must respond to tasks within bounded timing constraints.

### Key Structural Components of a Kernel

| Component | Function | Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| **Scheduler** | Decides which task runs next based on priority | Runway traffic planner |
| **Dispatcher** | Performs the actual context switch | Tower's hand signals to pilot |
| **Interrupt Handler (ISR)** | Services hardware interrupts immediately | Emergency radio channel |
| **Kernel Services** | Provide primitives (semaphores, mailboxes) | Airport support staff |
| **Object Manager** | Manages tasks, queues, semaphores | Database of all active flights |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Section:** A critical section is a portion of code that accesses shared resources (variables, data structures, peripherals) and must be executed atomically without interruption to prevent race conditions.

### Foreground/Background Systems

**Background (Non-Preemptive) System:**
- Main loop executes tasks sequentially
- ISRs run in the foreground for time-critical work
- Simple, low overhead, but **not deterministic** for multi-task systems

**Foreground/Background (Preemptive) System:**
- All time-critical work in ISRs (foreground)
- Non-critical work in background tasks
- Kernel manages preemption between tasks

> [!TIP]
> KTU frequently tests the difference between **foreground/background** and **preemptive** kernels. Memorize: *Background = non-preemptive = main loop + ISRs only.*

---

## 1.2 State Transition Diagram of a Task

### Formal Definition
A **State Transition Diagram (STD)** models the lifecycle of a real-time task as it moves through various execution states based on kernel events, interrupts, and service calls. The KTU 2024 module typically follows the **5-state model**: *Dormant/New, Ready, Running, Blocked/Waiting, and Terminated* (or in uC/OS-II terminology: *Dormant, Ready, Running, Waiting, ISR*).

### 🏥 Intuitive Analogy — The Hospital Patient Journey

Think of a task as a patient in a hospital:
- **Admitted (Dormant/New):** Patient registered, not yet active.
- **Waiting Room (Ready):** Patient waiting for a doctor.
- **Examination Room (Running):** Currently with the doctor.
- **Lab Testing (Blocked):** Waiting for lab results.
- **Discharged (Terminated):** Treatment complete.

Transitions happen when:
- A higher-priority patient arrives (preemption).
- Lab results come back (event completion).
- Doctor takes a break (delay).
- Patient is moved to ICU (ISR).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Five Canonical RTOS Task States (KTU Standard):**
> 1. **Dormant (New)** — Task created but not yet activated
> 2. **Ready** — Task eligible to run, awaiting CPU
> 3. **Running** — Task currently executing on CPU
> 4. **Blocked (Waiting)** — Task waiting for an event/resource
> 5. **Terminated** — Task execution finished (memory may be freed)

---

## 1.3 Kernel Primitives

### Formal Definition
**Kernel primitives** are the fundamental, atomic system calls provided by the RTOS kernel to manage tasks and resources. They are the building blocks for building real-time applications. In KTU 2024 (aligned with textbooks like *MicroC/OS-II* by Jean Labrosse), these include task management primitives, time management primitives, and inter-task communication primitives.

### 🔧 Intuitive Analogy — The Restaurant's Service System

A restaurant's basic operations are the **primitives**:
- **Seat customer** (Task Create)
- **Remove customer** (Task Delete)
- **Take order** (Pend/Wait on semaphore)
- **Mark order ready** (Post/Signal semaphore)
- **Deliver food** (Send message)
- **Pick up food** (Receive message)

Each is a basic, indivisible service that the kitchen (kernel) provides.

> [!NOTE]
> **Atomic Operation:** A kernel primitive executes indivisibly — no other task or ISR can interrupt it mid-execution. This is essential for maintaining data consistency in real-time systems.

### Categories of Kernel Primitives (KTU 2024 Classification)

1. **Task Management Primitives**
   - `OSTaskCreate()`, `OSTaskDelete()`
   - `OSTaskSuspend()`, `OSTaskResume()`
   - `OSTaskChangePrio()`

2. **Time Management Primitives**
   - `OSTimeDly()`, `OSTimeDlyHMSM()`
   - `OSTimeDlyResume()`
   - `TimeGet()`, `TimeSet()`

3. **Task Synchronization Primitives**
   - **Semaphores** (Counting, Binary)
   - **Mutex** (Mutual Exclusion)
   - **Event Flags**
   - **Condition Variables**

4. **Inter-Task Communication Primitives**
   - **Mailboxes** (single message)
   - **Message Queues** (multiple messages)

5. **Memory Management Primitives**
   - `OSMemCreate()`, `OSMemGet()`, `OSMemPut()`

6. **Interrupt Management Primitives**
   - `OSIntEnter()`, `OSIntExit()`
   - `OS_ENTER_CRITICAL()`, `OS_EXIT_CRITICAL()`

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Layers of an RTOS Kernel

The kernel architecture is **layered** (hierarchical), where each layer only calls services from the layer below it:

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│   Application Layer (User Tasks)        │  ← Highest layer
├─────────────────────────────────────────┤
│   Kernel Services / Primitives Layer    │  ← API calls
├─────────────────────────────────────────┤
│   Kernel Core (Scheduler, Dispatcher)   │  ← Scheduling logic
├─────────────────────────────────────────┤
│   Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL)      │  ← CPU-specific code
├─────────────────────────────────────────┤
│   Physical Hardware (CPU, Timers, I/O)  │  ← Lowest layer
└─────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Why Layered Design?
- **Portability**: HAL can be rewritten for new CPU without changing upper layers.
- **Maintainability**: Bugs localized to specific layers.
- **Determinism**: Bounded execution paths.

## 2.2 Critical Section Implementation

A **critical section** is protected in three ways:

| Method | Mechanism | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Disable Interrupts** | `OS_ENTER_CRITICAL()` macro | Simple, fully atomic | Affects interrupt latency |
| **Disable Scheduler** | Lock the scheduler | Allows interrupts | Limited duration |
| **Mutex/Semaphore** | Resource locking | Allows other tasks to run | Priority inversion risk |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Golden Rule:** Critical sections must be **as short as possible** to minimize interrupt latency and maintain system responsiveness.

## 2.3 State Transition Diagram — Detailed Analysis

### The Five States Explained

**State 1: Dormant (New)**
- Task code exists in ROM/Flash
- Task Control Block (TCB) not yet allocated in RAM
- Created via `OSTaskCreate()` → transitions to Ready

**State 2: Ready**
- TCB initialized, task in the **Ready List** (or **Priority Table**)
- Waiting for the CPU
- Highest-priority Ready task becomes the Running task

**State 3: Running**
- Task has control of the CPU
- Only **one task per CPU** can be in Running state at any time
- Transitions occur due to: preemption, blocking, time-slice expiry, termination

**State 4: Blocked (Waiting)**
- Task cannot proceed because it is waiting for:
  - An event (semaphore, flag, message)
  - A time delay to expire
- Removed from Ready List, placed in **Event Wait List**
- **Cannot** consume CPU time

**State 5: Terminated**
- Task function has returned
- TCB may still exist (in uC/OS-II, tasks are not auto-deleted)
- Programmer must explicitly call `OSTaskDel()` to free TCB

### Trigger Events for State Transitions

| # | From State | Event | To State |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Dormant | `OSTaskCreate()` | Ready |
| 2 | Ready | Scheduler picks it (highest priority) | Running |
| 3 | Running | Higher priority task becomes Ready | Ready (preempted) |
| 4 | Running | Time slice expires (round-robin) | Ready |
| 5 | Running | Pend on unavailable resource (`OSSemPend()`) | Blocked |
| 6 | Running | Delay request (`OSTimeDly()`) | Blocked |
| 7 | Running | Task function returns / `OSTaskDel()` | Terminated |
| 8 | Blocked | Resource posted / delay expired | Ready |
| 9 | Blocked | `OSTaskResume()` called | Ready |
| 10 | Running | Interrupt occurs | ISR executes, then Ready (or resumes) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Preemption:** A higher-priority task becoming Ready causes the currently Running task to immediately transition back to Ready state, even if its time slice has not expired. This is the hallmark of a **preemptive RTOS kernel**.

## 2.4 Kernel Primitives — Deep Dive

### A. Task Management Primitives

**`OSTaskCreate()` — Create a Task**
- Allocates TCB from the TCB pool
- Initializes stack with starting context (registers, PC, SR)
- Inserts task into the Ready List at its assigned priority
- Returns error code (`OS_NO_ERR` on success)

**`OSTaskSuspend(prio)` / `OSTaskResume(prio)`**
- Suspend removes task from Ready List to a Suspend state
- Resume returns it to Ready state
- Useful for pausing a task without deleting it

**`OSTaskChangePrio(oldprio, newprio)`**
- Dynamically alters task priority
- Triggers a re-schedule

### B. Time Management Primitives

**`OSTimeDly(n)` — Delay task for n ticks**
- Removes task from Ready List
- Inserts task into **Time Delay List** (sorted by expiration time)
- Task remains in Blocked state until `n` ticks elapse
- After delay: task transitions back to Ready

**`OSTimeDlyHMSM(h, m, s, ms)`** — Human-time delay (more readable)

**`OSTimeDlyResume(prio)`** — Force-resume a delayed task

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Aid:** Time delay is implemented via a **sorted linked list** (called the *OSTimeTick* wheel) for O(1) update. Tasks are inserted in expiration order.

### C. Semaphore Primitives

A **semaphore** is a kernel object that signals the occurrence of an event or protects a shared resource.

**`OSSemCreate(count)`** — Initialize semaphore with initial count
**`OSSemPend(sem, timeout, err)`** — Wait on semaphore (decrement)
**`OSSemPost(sem)`** — Signal semaphore (increment)

**Types of Semaphores:**

1. **Binary Semaphore** — Count is 0 or 1; used for synchronization
2. **Counting Semaphore** — Count $\geq 0$; used for resource pools (e.g., buffer slots)
3. **Mutex Semaphore** — Count is 0 or 1; used for mutual exclusion (with priority inheritance)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Priority Inversion:** A high-priority task waits for a resource held by a low-priority task, which is preempted by medium-priority tasks. Solution: **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)** — low-priority task temporarily inherits the priority of the highest-priority waiter.

### D. Message Queue Primitives

**`OSQCreate(buf, size)`** — Create a queue
**`OSQPend(q, timeout, err)`** — Wait for a message
**`OSQPost(q, msg)`** — Post a message to queue

- Messages are typically **void pointers** to data buffers
- FIFO (First In, First Out) ordering by default
- Used for producer-consumer patterns

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Context Switch Time** | $T_{cs} = T_{save} + T_{restore}$ | **Microseconds (μs)**; KTU benchmark: < 10 μs |
| 2 | **Interrupt Latency** | $T_{latency} = T_{max\_inst} + T_{enter\_ISR}$ | Bounded; must be deterministic |
| 3 | **Response Time (Worst-Case)** | $R_i = J_i + W_i + C_i$ | $J_i$ = jitter, $W_i$ = waiting, $C_i$ = compute |
| 4 | **CPU Utilization (RMS)** | $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i}$ | Must be $\leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ |
| 5 | **Semaphore Pend Logic** | If $count > 0$: proceed, $count--$ | Else: task blocks |
| 6 | **Semaphore Post Logic** | If waiters $\neq 0$: wake highest | Else: $count++$ |
| 7 | **Priority Inversion Bound** | $T_{inversion} \leq \sum C_{mid-priority}$ | PIP eliminates this |
| 8 | **Time Delay Calculation** | $n_{ticks} = \frac{T_{delay}}{T_{tick}}$ | $T_{tick}$ = OS tick rate period |
| 9 | **Task State Count Invariant** | Running = 1 (on single CPU) | Others can be $\geq 0$ |
| 10 | **Queue Length** | $L_q = N_{msg} \times N_{slots}$ | Bounded by memory |

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas above are **must-know for KTU 2024 ESE**. Specifically: Context switch time, interrupt latency, RMS utilization bound, and semaphore logic appear in nearly every past paper.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Automotive** | Engine control unit (ECU), ABS braking — uses preemptive kernel |
| **Aerospace** | Flight control systems — uses hard RTOS with strict timing |
| **Medical** | Pacemakers, infusion pumps — state transitions critical for safety |
| **Industrial Robotics** | Conveyor belts, robotic arms — semaphores for sensor sync |
| **IoT/Embedded** | Sensor fusion, motor control — message queues for data flow |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Worst-Case Response Time of a Task

### Problem Context
Given a set of $n$ real-time tasks with priorities, derive the worst-case response time $R_i$ of task $i$.

### Definitions
- $C_i$ = Worst-case execution time of task $i$
- $T_i$ = Period of task $i$
- $J_i$ = Release jitter of task $i$
- $W_i$ = Worst-case time task $i$ spends in the Ready queue before execution

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** The response time is the sum of all delays before task $i$ completes:
$$
R_i = J_i + W_i + C_i
$$

**Step 2:** While task $i$ is in the Ready state, it can be delayed by:
- Higher-priority tasks interrupting it
- A higher-priority task being released while $i$ is running (causing preemption)

**Step 3:** Let $hp(i)$ denote the set of tasks with priority higher than task $i$. The total interference from these tasks during an interval of length $t$ is:
$$
I_i(t) = \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{t}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j
$$

**Step 4:** The total time $W_i$ that task $i$ spends waiting satisfies:
$$
W_i = I_i(R_i) = \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j
$$

**Step 5:** Substituting $R_i$ into the recursive equation:
$$
R_i^{(k+1)} = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i^{(k)}}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j
$$

**Step 6:** Iterate starting with $R_i^{(0)} = C_i$ until convergence. If $R_i \leq T_i$, the task is **schedulable**.

### Convergence Logic Explained
- Each iteration refines the estimate of $R_i$.
- If $R_i^{(k+1)} = R_i^{(k)}$, we have found the fixed point.
- If $R_i^{(k+1)} > T_i$, the task is **NOT schedulable**.

> [!NOTE]
> This iterative method is the **Joseph & Pandya (1986)** response time analysis — frequently cited in KTU reference textbooks.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: CPU Utilization Bound for Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)

### Problem
Find the maximum guaranteed utilization $U_{max}$ for $n$ tasks scheduled under RMS.

### Step 1: For a single task ($n = 1$):
$$
U_{max}(1) = 1
$$

**Step 2:** For $n = 2$, by Liu & Layland (1973):
$$
U_{max}(2) = 2(\sqrt{2} - 1) \approx 0.828
$$

**Step 3:** Generalization — the Liu & Layland bound:
$$
U_{max}(n) = n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)
$$

**Step 4:** As $n \to \infty$:
$$
\lim_{n \to \infty} U_{max}(n) = \lim_{n \to \infty} n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)
$$

**Step 5:** Using the limit identity $\lim_{x \to 0} \frac{2^x - 1}{x} = \ln 2$:
$$
\lim_{n \to \infty} U_{max}(n) = \ln 2 \approx 0.693
$$

### Final Result
$$
\boxed{U \leq n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)}
$$

| $n$ | $U_{max}(n)$ | $\ln 2$ bound |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1.000 | — |
| 2 | 0.828 | — |
| 3 | 0.780 | — |
| 5 | 0.743 | — |
| 10 | 0.717 | — |
| $\infty$ | $\ln 2 \approx 0.693$ | 0.693 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often confuse $U_{max}(n)$ with the asymptotic bound $\ln 2$. Always cite the exact formula $n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ unless the question asks for the limit case.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Semaphore Operations

### Scenario: Two tasks coordinating via a binary semaphore

| Step | Task A (Producer) | Task B (Consumer) | Semaphore Count | Event |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Created, runs first | Created, blocked on `Pend` | 0 | Task B calls `OSSemPend()` |
| 2 | Produces data | Blocked | 0 | — |
| 3 | Calls `OSSemPost()` | — | 1 | Task A signals |
| 4 | — | Wakes up, reads data | 0 | Task B resumes |
| 5 | Loops back to step 2 | Loops back to step 1 | 0 | Cycle repeats |

### Analytical Equation
Let $\sigma$ be the semaphore count. The atomic operations are:

**Pend (Wait):**
$$
\sigma = \begin{cases} \sigma - 1 & \text{if } \sigma > 0 \text{ (proceed)} \\ \text{block task} & \text{if } \sigma = 0 \end{cases}
$$

**Post (Signal):**
$$
\sigma = \begin{cases} \sigma + 1 & \text{if no waiters} \\ \text{wake highest-priority waiter} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

> [!TIP]
> The atomicity of these operations is guaranteed by disabling interrupts or using a compare-and-swap (CAS) instruction at the hardware level.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: State Transition Triggers

| Trigger Event | Current State | New State | KTU Service Call |
|---|---|---|---|
| Task created | Dormant | Ready | `OSTaskCreate()` |
| Scheduler selects task | Ready | Running | Internal — context switch |
| Higher-prio task ready | Running | Ready | Preemption |
| Pend on resource | Running | Blocked | `OSSemPend()` |
| Delay request | Running | Blocked | `OSTimeDly()` |
| Delay expires | Blocked | Ready | Tick handler (OSTimeTick) |
| Post by another task | Blocked | Ready | `OSSemPost()` |
| Task suspended | Ready/Running | Suspended | `OSTaskSuspend()` |
| Task resumed | Suspended | Ready | `OSTaskResume()` |
| Task function returns | Running | Dormant/Terminated | Implicit |

> [!NOTE]
> In uC/OS-II's terminology, **Suspended** is treated as a separate state from Blocked. Some textbooks fold Suspended into a generic "Inactive" category. Be prepared to discuss both nomenclatures in KTU ESE.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an RTOS Kernel

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Application Tasks] --> B[Kernel API Layer]
    B --> C[Service Handlers]
    C --> D[Scheduler]
    C --> E[Dispatcher]
    C --> F[IPC Manager]
    C --> G[Time Manager]
    C --> H[Memory Manager]
    D --> I[Ready List]
    D --> J[Priority Table]
    E --> K[Context Switch Engine]
    F --> L[Semaphore Pool]
    F --> M[Queue Pool]
    G --> N[Tick Timer ISR]
    H --> O[Memory Partitions]
    I --> P[TCB Pool]
    J --> P
    P --> K
    K --> Q[CPU Core]
    N --> D
    L --> D
    M --> D
    O --> D
```

### Diagram Explanation
- **Application Tasks** invoke **Kernel API** calls (e.g., `OSSemPend`).
- The **Service Handlers** route calls to appropriate subsystems.
- The **Scheduler** uses the **Ready List** and **Priority Table** to pick the next task.
- The **Dispatcher** performs the **Context Switch** via the CPU.
- The **Tick Timer ISR** fires periodically to update time delays and trigger rescheduling.

---

## 4.2 RTOS State Transition Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Dormant
    Dormant --> Ready: OSTaskCreate
    Ready --> Running: Scheduler dispatch
    Running --> Ready: Preempted by higher prio
    Running --> Blocked: Pend on resource or delay
    Blocked --> Ready: Event occurs or delay expires
    Running --> Suspended: OSTaskSuspend
    Blocked --> Suspended: OSTaskSuspend
    Ready --> Suspended: OSTaskSuspend
    Suspended --> Ready: OSTaskResume
    Running --> [*]: Task function returns or OSTaskDel
```

### Diagram Annotations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Transitions (KTU Hot Spots):**
> 1. **Running → Blocked:** Task voluntarily relinquishes CPU
> 2. **Running → Ready:** Involuntary — caused by preemption
> 3. **Blocked → Ready:** Time delay expired or event signaled

---

## 4.3 Kernel Primitive Dependency Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[OSTaskCreate] --> P2[OSTaskSuspend]
    P1 --> P3[OSTaskResume]
    P1 --> P4[OSTimeDly]
    P1 --> P5[OSSemCreate]
    P5 --> P6[OSSemPend]
    P5 --> P7[OSSemPost]
    P1 --> P8[OSQCreate]
    P8 --> P9[OSQPend]
    P8 --> P10[OSQPost]
    P4 --> P11[OSTimeTick Handler]
    P6 --> P11
    P9 --> P11
    P7 --> P12[Scheduler]
    P10 --> P12
    P11 --> P12
    P2 --> P12
    P3 --> P12
    P12 --> P13[Context Switch]
```

### Explanation
- `OSTaskCreate` is the **root primitive** — all others depend on it.
- Time, semaphore, and queue operations eventually call the **Scheduler**.
- The Scheduler triggers the **Context Switch** when needed.

---

## 4.4 Foreground/Background System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ForegroundLayer["FOREGROUND (Interrupt-Driven)"]
        ISR1[Hardware ISR]
        ISR2[Timer ISR]
        ISR3[Communication ISR]
    end
    subgraph BackgroundLayer["BACKGROUND (Main Loop)"]
        MainLoop[main function loop]
        T1[Task 1]
        T2[Task 2]
        T3[Task n]
    end
    ForegroundLayer -->|Sets flags/events| BackgroundLayer
    BackgroundLayer -->|Polls flags| ForegroundLayer
```

### Key Insight
In a pure foreground/background system, **all real-time work happens in ISRs**. The background loop polls flags set by ISRs. This is **non-preemptive** and **not suitable for hard real-time**.

---

## 4.5 Critical Section Protection Mechanisms

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Task A enters critical section] --> B{Protection Method?}
    B -->|Method 1: Disable Interrupts| C[OS_ENTER_CRITICAL]
    C --> D[Access shared resource]
    D --> E[OS_EXIT_CRITICAL]
    E --> F[Task A continues]
    B -->|Method 2: Lock Scheduler| G[OSSchedLock]
    G --> D2[Access shared resource]
    D2 --> H[OSSchedUnlock]
    H --> F
    B -->|Method 3: Mutex| I[OSMutexPend]
    I --> D3[Access shared resource]
    D3 --> J[OSMutexPost]
    J --> F
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**Define the following with respect to RTOS:**
**(a)** Critical Section
**(b)** Semaphore
**(c)** Task Control Block (TCB)

### Model Answer

**(a) Critical Section:**
A critical section is a segment of code that accesses shared resources (variables, data structures, or hardware peripherals) and must be executed **atomically** without preemption or interrupt interference to prevent race conditions and data inconsistency.

**(b) Semaphore:**
A semaphore is a kernel object that implements signaling and resource access control. It maintains an integer count accessed via two atomic operations:
- `Pend()` (wait/decrement)
- `Post()` (signal/increment)

It is used for task synchronization and mutual exclusion in real-time systems.

**(c) Task Control Block (TCB):**
A TCB is a data structure maintained by the kernel for each task. It stores:
- Task stack pointer
- Task priority
- Task state (Ready, Running, Blocked, etc.)
- Delay value
- Link pointers to ready/wait lists

> [!TIP]
> **3-mark strategy:** Always provide a **one-line crisp definition** plus a brief example or bullet-list of contents. Avoid essay-style answers for 3-mark questions.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**Differentiate between a binary semaphore and a mutex.**

### Model Answer

| Feature | Binary Semaphore | Mutex |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Synchronization between tasks | Mutual exclusion (resource locking) |
| **Ownership** | No owner concept; any task can post | Has owner; only owner can release |
| **Priority Inversion** | Not handled | Handled via Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP) |
| **Initial Value** | 0 or 1 | Always 1 (initially available) |
| **Use Case** | Event signaling | Protecting shared data structures |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key distinction:** A mutex enforces **ownership** — only the task that acquired it can release it. A binary semaphore does not enforce ownership.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the different states of a real-time task with a neat state transition diagram. List the kernel service calls that cause transitions between states.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the structure of a real-time kernel. Explain the functions of scheduler, dispatcher, and interrupt handler in detail.

---

### Model Answer to Q.A(a)

**Task States in RTOS:**

A real-time task passes through the following states during its lifecycle:

1. **Dormant State:** Task code resides in memory but TCB is not yet allocated. Task is not eligible for execution.

2. **Ready State:** TCB is initialized and task is in the Ready List. The task is eligible to run but the CPU is currently allocated to another task.

3. **Running State:** The task has control of the CPU and is actively executing its code. Only one task per processor can be in this state at a time.

4. **Blocked (Waiting) State:** The task cannot proceed because it is waiting for:
   - A resource (semaphore, mutex)
   - A time delay to expire
   - A message to arrive in a queue

5. **Suspended State:** Task is deliberately paused via `OSTaskSuspend()`. It does not consume CPU and is not part of scheduling.

6. **Terminated State:** Task function has returned. TCB may be freed via `OSTaskDel()`.

**State Transition Diagram (Refer to Section 4.2 for Mermaid diagram)**

**Kernel Service Calls Causing Transitions:**

| Service Call | State Transition |
|---|---|
| `OSTaskCreate()` | Dormant → Ready |
| `OSTaskSuspend()` | Any → Suspended |
| `OSTaskResume()` | Suspended → Ready |
| `OSTimeDly()` | Running → Blocked (Time Wait) |
| `OSSemPend()` | Running → Blocked (Event Wait) |
| `OSSemPost()` | Blocked → Ready |
| `OSTaskDel()` | Running → Terminated |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often forget the **Suspended** state, treating it as part of Blocked. This loses 1–2 marks. KTU 2024 expects all **5–6 states** to be listed.

---

### Model Answer to Q.A(b)

**Structure of a Real-Time Kernel:**

The kernel is the central component of an RTOS. It consists of:

1. **Scheduler**
2. **Dispatcher**
3. **Interrupt Handler (ISR)**
4. **Service Routines (Primitives)**
5. **Object Manager** (TCBs, Semaphores, Queues)

**1. Scheduler:**
- Determines **which task** should run next.
