The **diagnostic task** runs at lower priority (100) since it is non-critical for safety. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer) ### Question 1: Define pSOSystem and list its major components. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** **pSOSystem (PSOS)** is a modular, scalable, multiprocessor real-time operating system developed by Software Components Group, Inc. (later acquired by Wind River Systems). **Major Components of pSOSystem:** | Component | Function | |---|---| | **pSOS+ Kernel** | Core services — task, memory, I/O, communication management | | **pSOS+M** | Multiprocessor kernel extension | | **pNA+** | Networking stack (TCP/IP) | | **pRPC+** | Remote Procedure Call middleware | | **pHILE+** | File system (MS-DOS, NFS, raw) | | **pREPC+** | POSIX real-time extension | --- ### Question 2: Mention any two advantages of VRTX over other commercial RTOS. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** **Advantage 1: Smallest Footprint** VRTX32 requires less than **5 KB of ROM**, making it suitable for deeply embedded systems with severe memory constraints (e.g., pacemakers, sensor nodes). **Advantage 2: Fastest Context Switch** VRTX32 achieves context switch times of **1–5 µs**, the lowest in its class, ensuring deterministic response in hard real-time applications like missile guidance and automotive ECUs. --- ## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — Set 1 **Q: (a)** Explain the architecture of pSOSystem in detail with a neat block diagram. List the services provided by the pSOS+ kernel. **(7 marks)** **Q: (b)** Describe the task scheduling mechanism in VRTX32 with a state transition diagram. Compare it with pSOS+ scheduling. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to (a): pSOSystem Architecture **Architecture of pSOSystem:** pSOSystem is a **layered, modular, microkernel-based** real-time operating system designed for embedded and distributed applications. The architecture consists of **four primary layers**: **Layer 1: Application Layer** - Contains user-written tasks and processes - Compiled and linked with the pSOSystem runtime libraries - Calls kernel primitives via system call interface **Layer 2: Middleware Layer** - **pNA+**: High-performance TCP/IP networking stack - **pRPC+**: Remote Procedure Call for distributed applications - **pHILE+**: File system manager (supports MS-DOS, NFS, raw Flash) **Layer 3: pSOS+ Kernel (Core)** The pSOS+ kernel provides the following services: 1. **Task Management** — `t_create`, `t_start`, `t_delete`, `t_suspend`, `t_resume` 2. **Memory Management** — Fixed-size and variable-size partitions (`m_alloc`, `m_free`) 3. **Inter-Task Communication** — Message queues, mailboxes 4. **Synchronization** — Binary and counting semaphores, event flags 5. **Interrupt Management** — `e_int_enter`, `e_int_exit` 6. **Time Management** — `tm_wkafter`, `tm_wkwhen`, `tm_cancel` 7. **Error Handling** — `ev_receive` for exception events **Layer 4: Board Support Package (BSP)** - Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL) - Processor-specific initialization - Device drivers and interrupt vector mapping **Key Features:** - **POSIX 1003.13 PSE52/53** compliance - **Multiprocessor support** through pSOS+M - **Deterministic response**: interrupt latency < 10 µs --- #### Model Solution to (b): VRTX32 Task Scheduling **VRTX32 Task States:** 1. **DORMANT** — Created but not started 2. **READY** — Eligible to run 3. **RUNNING** — Currently executing 4. **PENDING** — Blocked on event/semaphore/time 5. **SUSPENDED** — Administratively halted **Scheduling Algorithm:** VRTX32 uses **Fixed-Priority Preemptive Scheduling** with **Round-Robin** within the same priority level. Priorities range from **0 to 255**, where 0 is the highest. **Scheduler Operation:** $$\text{Next Task} = \arg\max_{i \in READY} (P_i)$$ Where $P_i$ is the priority of task $i$ in the READY queue. Among equal-priority tasks, VRTX32 uses **time-sliced round-robin** (default time slice is 10 ms, configurable). **VRTX32 Scheduling Primitives:** - `sc_creat` — Create task - `sc_start` — Start task - `sc_delt` — Delete task - `sc_susp` — Suspend task - `sc_resu` — Resume task - `sc_tset` — Set task priority **Comparison with pSOS+ Scheduling:** | Feature | VRTX32 | pSOS+ | |---|---|---| | Algorithm | Fixed-priority preemptive + RR | Fixed-priority preemptive | | Priority Levels | 256 | 256 | | Round-Robin | Yes (time-sliced) | Yes (configurable) | | Priority Inheritance | **Not natively supported** | Supported via **sc_inherit** | | Event Flags | Yes (32-bit groups) | Yes | | Deadline Support | No | No (POSIX extension available) | **Key Limitation:** VRTX32 does not support **priority inheritance protocol (PIP)** natively, leading to potential **unbounded priority inversion** in shared-resource scenarios. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Set 2 **Q: (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the co-kernel architecture of Real-Time Linux. Discuss its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)** **Q: (b)** A real-time system uses VRTX32 to manage three periodic tasks with the following parameters: - Task 1: $C_1 = 1$ ms, $T_1 = 4$ ms - Task 2: $C_2 = 2$ ms, $T_2 = 6$ ms - Task 3: $C_3 = 3$ ms, $T_3 = 10$ ms Check the schedulability using Rate Monotonic Analysis. Also compute the worst-case blocking time under the Priority Ceiling Protocol. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to (a): RT Linux Co-Kernel Architecture **Co-Kernel Architecture Overview:** In the **co-kernel approach** (used by RTLinux, RTAI, Xenomai), a small, dedicated real-time kernel runs **concurrently** with the standard Linux kernel. The Linux kernel is treated as the **lowest-priority idle task** of the real-time kernel. **Architecture Diagram (described in words for clarity):** The hardware layer sits at the bottom. Above it, the **Real-Time Microkernel** intercepts all hardware interrupts first. If the interrupt is for a real-time task, the RT kernel handles it directly and dispatches the RT task. If the interrupt is for a non-real-time Linux task, it is **passed through** to the Linux kernel, which handles it at its own priority. The **RT task layer** sits above the RT kernel, and the **Linux application layer** sits above the Linux kernel. **Key Mechanism: Interrupt Virtualization** $$\begin{aligned} \text{Interrupt Arrival} &\rightarrow \text{RT Kernel Interrupt Handler} \\ &\rightarrow \text{Decision: RT or Linux?} \\ &\rightarrow \begin{cases} \text{Handle in RT context (microsecond response)} \\ \text{Forward to Linux (best-effort response)} \end{cases} \end{aligned}$$ **Advantages:** 1. **Hard real-time guarantees** with microsecond-level interrupt latency (< 1 µs achievable) 2. **Full Linux ecosystem** preserved — drivers, networking, file systems, GUI 3. **Familiar development environment** — GNU tools, POSIX APIs 4. **Dual-mode operation** — RT and non-RT tasks coexist **Limitations:** 1. **Co-kernel overhead** — Context switches between RT and Linux add latency 2. **Complex debugging** — Two kernels make stack traces harder 3. **Driver support** — Linux drivers cannot be used directly for hard real-time I/O 4. **GPL licensing concerns** — Some commercial users avoid GPLv2 kernel code 5. **Memory footprint** — Full Linux requires > 1 MB, unsuitable for deeply embedded systems --- #### Model Solution to (b): Rate Monotonic Schedulability Analysis **Given Data:** | Task | Execution Time $C_i$ | Period $T_i$ | |---|---|---| | Task 1 | 1 ms | 4 ms | | Task 2 | 2 ms | 6 ms | | Task 3 | 3 ms | 10 ms | **Step 1: Compute CPU Utilization** $$\begin{aligned} U &= \frac{C_1}{T_1} + \frac{C_2}{T_2} + \frac{C_3}{T_3} \\ &= \frac{1}{4} + \frac{2}{6} + \frac{3}{10} \\ &= 0.250 + 0.333 + 0.300 \\ &= 0.883 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2: Apply Liu & Layland Bound (Necessary but not sufficient)** For $n = 3$ tasks: $$\begin{aligned} U_{bound} &= n \cdot (2^{1/n} - 1) \\ &= 3 \cdot (2^{1/3} - 1) \\ &= 3 \cdot (1.2599 - 1) \\ &= 3 \cdot 0.2599 \\ &= 0.7798 \end{aligned}$$ Since $U = 0.883 > U_{bound} = 0.7798$, the **Liu & Layland bound is violated**. We must use the **exact Response Time Analysis (RTA)**. **Step 3: Exact Response Time Analysis** For Rate Monotonic, the worst-case response time of task $i$ is computed iteratively: $$R_i^{(k+1)} = C_i + \sum_{j: P_j > P_i} \left\lceil \frac{R_i^{(k)}}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j$$ **For Task 1 (Highest Priority, $P = 1$):** No higher-priority tasks exist. $$R_1 = C_1 = 1 \text{ ms}$$ Check: $R_1 = 1 \le T_1 = 4$ ✓ (Schedulable) **For Task 2 (Medium Priority, $P = 2$):** Higher-priority tasks: Task 1. $$R_2^{(0)} = C_2 = 2$$ $$R_2^{(1)} = 2 + \left\lceil \frac{2}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 2 + 1 = 3$$ $$R_2^{(2)} = 2 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 2 + 1 = 3$$ Converges: $R_2 = 3$ ms. Check: $R_2 = 3 \le T_2 = 6$ ✓ (Schedulable) **For Task 3 (Lowest Priority, $P = 3$):** Higher-priority tasks: Task 1, Task 2. $$R_3^{(0)} = C_3 = 3$$ $$R_3^{(1)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 1 + 2 = 6$$ $$R_3^{(2)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 2 + 2 = 7$$ $$R_3^{(3)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{7}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{7}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 2 + 4 = 9$$ $$R_3^{(4)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{9}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{9}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 3 + 4 = 10$$ $$R_3^{(5)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{10}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{10}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 3 + 4 = 10$$ Converges: $R_3 = 10$ ms. Check: $R_3 = 10 \le T_3 = 10$ ✓ (Schedulable, just barely!) **Conclusion:** The task set **IS schedulable** under Rate Monotonic, with Task 3 meeting its deadline exactly at 10 ms. **Step 4: Worst-Case Blocking under Priority Ceiling Protocol** Under PCP, the worst-case blocking time for any task is bounded by the **execution time of the longest critical section** of any lower-priority task. Assuming the longest critical section is $C_{cs} = 2$ ms (e.g., Task 2's critical section): $$B_{max} = 2 \text{ ms}$$ This means a high-priority task can be blocked for at most **2 ms** when accessing a shared resource protected by PCP. --- ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in PSOS / VRTX / RT Linux Questions:** > > 1. **Confusing pSOS+ and pSOS+M**: Students often write pSOS+M (multiprocessor) when asked about the basic kernel. Remember: **pSOS+ is the base kernel**; **pSOS+M is the multiprocessor extension**. > > 2. **VRTX32 priority numbering**: VRTX uses **0 = highest, 255 = lowest** (opposite of some other RTOS). Confusing this is a common 2-mark deduction. > > 3. **Co-kernel vs monolithic**: In RT Linux co-kernel architecture, Linux is the **lowest-priority task**, NOT a peer of the RT kernel. Drawing them as parallel is incorrect. > > 4. **Liu & Layland bound is NECESSARY but not SUFFICIENT**: If $U \le U_{bound}$, schedulable is **guaranteed**. If $U > U_{bound}$, schedulability is **undetermined** — you must do exact RTA. Many students incorrectly conclude "not schedulable" when $U > U_{bound}$. > > 5. **Priority Ceiling Protocol bound**: The bound is **one critical section**, not "all critical sections." Students often over-estimate $B$. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist (RTOS Module 4):** > > - [ ] **pSOS+** kernel is **microkernel-based**, modular, with components pNA+, pRPC+, pHILE+ > - [ ] **VRTX32** has the **smallest footprint** (< 5 KB) and **fastest context switch** (1–5 µs) in its class > - [ ] **VRTX priorities**: 0 = highest, 255 = lowest (256 levels) > - [ ] **VRTX does not natively support priority inheritance** — major limitation for shared resources > - [ ] **RT Linux co-kernel**: RT kernel runs Linux as lowest-priority task; interrupt virtualization is the key mechanism > - [ ] **PREEMPT_RT**: In-kernel approach, makes kernel preemptible, threaded IRQ handlers > - [ ] **Liu & Layland bound**: $U_{bound} = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ — necessary, not sufficient > - [ ] **Exact RTA**: $R_i^{(k+1)} = C_i + \sum_{j: P_j > P_i} \lceil R_i^{(k)}/T_j \rceil C_j$ > - [ ] **PCP blocking bound**: $B = \max(C_{cs})$ — bounded by one critical section > - [ ] **Case Study Insight**: Automotive ABS uses pSOS+ because of its deterministic response (< 10 ms) and POSIX compliance > - [ ] **Three architectural approaches to RT Linux**: co-kernel, in-kernel preemption, hypervisor > - [ ] **Standards**: pSOS+ → POSIX 1003.13 PSE52/53; VRTX → proprietary; RT Linux → POSIX 1003.1b > - [ ] **Jitter formula**: $J = t_{max} - t_{min}$ — must be bounded for hard real-time > - [ ] **Scheduling latency** = interrupt latency + dispatch latency + context switch time > - [ ] **Rate Monotonic** is optimal static-priority algorithm when $U \le 0.69$ for large $n$

topics.title: MicroC/OS-II (Features, Threads and Task Scheduling - Basics Only), Real-Life Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT785 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** MicroC/OS-II (Features, Threads and Task Scheduling - Basics Only), Real-Life Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is MicroC/OS-II?

**MicroC/OS-II (µC/OS-II)** is a highly portable, ROMable, scalable, preemptive, real-time multitasking kernel written primarily in the **C programming language** for microprocessors, microcontrollers, and DSPs. It was designed and developed by **Jean J. Labrosse** and is one of the most widely used commercial-grade RTOS kernels in embedded systems education and industry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Aligned):**
> MicroC/OS-II is a **preemptive, priority-based real-time kernel** that manages a finite number of tasks using a deterministic, O(1) scheduling algorithm. It provides essential RTOS services such as task management, time management, inter-task communication, and memory management in a footprint as low as **~2 KB of ROM**.

### Version Lineage & Certification
- Original µC/OS was released in **1992**.
- µC/OS-II (pronounced "micro-C-O-S-two") followed in **1998**.
- Certified **FAA DO-178B** for safety-critical avionics (2000).
- Certified **IEC 61508 SIL 3** for industrial safety systems.
- Successor: **µC/OS-III** (2009) — adds round-robin, unlimited priority levels, and POSIX support.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Study µC/OS-II in KTU?**
> It serves as a **pedagogically rich, simple, fully-disclosed RTOS** where the entire source code is published in the textbook (Labrosse, *MicroC/OS-II: The Real-Time Kernel*), allowing students to study the actual implementation — unlike proprietary RTOSes (VxWorks, QNX) which hide their internals.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hospital Emergency Room" Model

Imagine a busy hospital emergency room. A µC/OS-II system works exactly like a well-managed ER:

| ER Concept | µC/OS-II Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Patient arriving with illness | **Task** requiring CPU service |
| Triage nurse assigning priority | **Scheduler** checking task priority |
| Doctor (only one at a time) | **CPU** (single execution unit) |
| Heart-attack patient jumps queue | **Preemption** by higher-priority task |
| Patient waiting for blood test | **Task in WAITING (blocked) state** |
| Patient discharged / sent home | **Task in DORMANT state** |
| ER shift rotation fairness | **Round-Robin scheduling** (µC/OS-III only) |

> [!TIP]
> Just as the ER must guarantee that a critical patient is treated **within strict time limits**, µC/OS-II guarantees a deterministic maximum response time — typically under **few microseconds** on a typical 32-bit MCU.

## 1.3 Core Salient Features of µC/OS-II

µC/OS-II provides a unique combination of features ideal for embedded systems:

1. **Preemptive Real-Time Kernel** — Higher-priority ready task immediately preempts the running task.
2. **Source Code Available** — Entire kernel is in clean, well-documented ANSI C.
3. **Portable** — Portable to nearly any CPU architecture with a C compiler.
4. **ROMable** — Entire kernel can be placed in ROM/Flash.
5. **Scalable** — Compile only the services required (via `#define` constants in `OS_CFG.H`), minimizing footprint.
6. **Multitasking** — Supports up to **64 distinct tasks** (8 reserved for system, 56 available to user).
7. **Deterministic** — Execution time of all kernel services is known and bounded.
8. **Re-entrant Functions** — All kernel functions are re-entrant; multiple tasks can call the same service.
9. **Priority-Based Scheduling** — 64 levels, **0 = highest priority**, 63 = lowest.
10. **Time Management** — Standard 32-bit tick counter for delays and timeouts.
11. **Inter-Task Communication** — Semaphores, mutexes, event flags, message mailboxes, message queues.
12. **Interrupt Management** — Tasks can be signaled from ISRs using dedicated `OSIntEnter()` / `OSIntExit()` functions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Constants Worth Remembering:**
> - Max tasks = **64** (8 reserved by µC/OS-II internally)
> - Lowest priority (idle task) = **OS_LOWEST_PRIO = 63**
> - Priority of idle task = **OS_IDLE_PRIO = OS_LOWEST_PRIO**
> - Priority of statistic task = **OS_STAT_PRIO = OS_LOWEST_PRIO - 1**
> - **OS_N_SYS_TASKS = 2** (idle + statistic)
> - Tick rate configurable, typical: **10 ms to 1000 ms**
> - Each task has a unique TCB (`OS_TCB`) — Task Control Block.

## 1.4 µC/OS-II File Architecture

The kernel is organized into three processing layers:

| Layer | File Types | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Application Code** | User `.c` / `.h` files | Your task functions |
| **µC/OS-II Code (Processor-Independent)** | `OS_CORE.C`, `OS_TASK.C`, `OS_TIME.C`, etc. | Kernel services, written in portable C |
| **µC/OS-II Port (Processor-Specific)** | `OS_CPU.H`, `OS_CPU_C.C`, `OS_CPU_A.ASM` | CPU-specific assembly porting layer |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> To port µC/OS-II to a new processor, **only three files** need to be modified: `OS_CPU.H`, `OS_CPU_C.C`, and `OS_CPU_A.ASM`. This is a hallmark of good RTOS design — the porting layer is **isolated** from the rest of the kernel.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Task States in µC/OS-II

A task in µC/OS-II can exist in one of **five states** (sliding between them as it competes for the CPU):

| State | Description | Trigger to Enter | Trigger to Exit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **DORMANT** | Task resides in memory but kernel is unaware (not yet created or deleted) | `OSTaskDel()` | `OSTaskCreate()` |
| **READY** | Task is ready to run but a higher-priority task is currently running | Preemption, ISR completion, `OSSched()` | Selected by scheduler |
| **RUNNING** | Task has the CPU; only one task can be in this state at a time | Higher-priority task becomes ready | Time-slice expiry, preemption |
| **WAITING (Blocked)** | Task is waiting for an event (semaphore, signal, time delay) | `OSTimeDly()`, `OSSemPend()`, etc. | Event occurs |
| **ISR** | Task execution was suspended by an Interrupt Service Routine; CPU is in ISR context | Hardware interrupt fires | `OSIntExit()` returns |

### State Transition Diagram Logic
- `DORMANT → READY` ⇢ when `OSTaskCreate()` is called.
- `READY → RUNNING` ⇢ when scheduler picks it (it is the highest-priority READY task).
- `RUNNING → READY` ⇢ preempted by a higher-priority task.
- `RUNNING → WAITING` ⇢ task calls a wait function.
- `WAITING → READY` ⇢ waited event occurs.
- `RUNNING → DORMANT` ⇢ task deletes itself or another task.

## 2.2 Task Control Block (OS_TCB)

Each task in µC/OS-II has a corresponding **Task Control Block** (TCB) — a C `struct` that holds everything the kernel needs to know about the task.

### Key Fields of `OS_TCB`

| Field | Type | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `OSTCBStkPtr` | `OS_STK *` | Pointer to the **top of task's stack** |
| `OSTCBExtPtr` | `void *` | Pointer to user-definable TCB extension |
| `OSTCBStkBottom` | `OS_STK *` | Pointer to the **bottom of task's stack** |
| `OSTCBId` | `INT16U` | Task identifier (0..65535) |
| `OSTCBPrio` | `INT8U` | Task priority (0 = highest, 63 = lowest) |
| `OSTCBStat` | `INT8U` | Task state flags (ready, waiting, etc.) |
| `OSTCBDly` | `INT16U` | Number of ticks to delay / timeout |
| `OSTCBNext`, `OSTCBPrev` | `OS_TCB *` | Doubly linked list pointers |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The two most critical fields during scheduling are **`OSTCBStkPtr`** (used during context switches to save/restore CPU registers) and **`OSTCBPrio`** (used to determine scheduling order).

## 2.3 The Ready Table and Ready List — The Heart of O(1) Scheduling

The single most important design idea in µC/OS-II is its **O(1) scheduling mechanism**, achieved by maintaining two data structures:

### A. Ready Bitmap (`OSRdyTbl[]`)
A 64-bit (8-byte) bitmap — actually implemented as an array of 8 bytes, one per priority group of 8 tasks:
$$\text{OSRdyTbl[0..7]} \quad \text{where bit } b \text{ of } \text{OSRdyTbl}[i] \text{ corresponds to priority } 8i+b$$

### B. Ready Group Variable (`OSRdyGrp`)
A single 8-bit variable where bit `i` is set if **any** task in priority group `i` is ready:
$$\text{OSRdyGrp} = \bigvee_{i=0}^{7} \text{OSRdyTbl}[i]$$

### C. Ready List (`OSRdyList[]`)
An array of 64 list-head pointers, one per priority level, supporting FIFO ordering of same-priority tasks.

> [!NOTE]
> The highest-priority ready task is found in **O(1) time** using a precomputed lookup table (`OSMapTbl[]` and `OSUnMapTbl[256]`) — this is a classic **bit-mask + lookup-table** design pattern.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept / Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Max tasks = **64** | `OS_MAX_TASKS` configurable |
| 2 | System reserved tasks = **2** | Idle + Statistic |
| 3 | User tasks ≤ **62** | 64 - 2 |
| 4 | Priority range = **0 .. OS_LOWEST_PRIO** | 0 = highest |
| 5 | OS_LOWEST_PRIO = **63** (default) | 64 levels total |
| 6 | Scheduling complexity = **O(1)** | Constant time, deterministic |
| 7 | Task states = **5** | Dormant, Ready, Running, Waiting, ISR |
| 8 | Min ROM footprint ≈ **2 KB** | Highly scalable kernel |
| 9 | Scheduling algorithm | **Preemptive priority-based** (µC/OS-II); Round-Robin added in µC/OS-III |
| 10 | Time management tick | 32-bit counter, configurable rate (Hz) |
| 11 | Context switch time | < **few microseconds** on a 32-bit MCU @ 100 MHz |
| 12 | Task priority uniqueness | **Each task must have a unique priority** (no two tasks share priority in µC/OS-II) |

### Two Key Macros — How the Bit-Map Works

**`OSMapTbl[idx]`** — Maps a number to its lowest set bit:
$$\text{OSMapTbl}[0x07] = 3 \quad \text{(since binary 0000\,0111 has bit 3 as LSB)}$$

**`OSUnMapTbl[byte]`** — Returns position of the **highest-priority bit set** (lowest-numbered bit) in a byte:
$$\text{OSUnMapTbl}[0b00010100] = 2 \quad \text{(bit 2 is the highest priority)}$$

> [!TIP]
> `OSUnMapTbl[256]` is a **256-byte lookup table** precomputed at compile time. The C expression `OSUnMapTbl[OSRdyGrp]` returns the priority group (0-7) of the highest-priority ready task in **O(1)** time — this is the secret to µC/OS-II's deterministic scheduling.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

µC/OS-II is the kernel of choice in many safety-critical and embedded products:

| Domain | Example Use Case |
|---|---|
| **Avionics** | Flight control computers (DO-178B certified) |
| **Medical Devices** | Ventilators, infusion pumps, patient monitors |
| **Automotive** | Engine control units, airbag controllers |
| **Industrial Automation** | PLCs, robotic arm controllers |
| **Consumer Electronics** | Set-top boxes, IoT edge nodes |
| **White Goods** | Washing machines, microwave ovens |
| **Networking** | Routers, switches, wireless APs |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why O(1) scheduling matters in real-time systems:**
> A variable-time scheduler can cause **jitter** — unpredictable latency that may violate hard real-time deadlines. By guaranteeing the scheduler always runs in constant time, µC/OS-II provides **bounded worst-case scheduling latency**, which is the cornerstone of hard real-time certification.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Finding the Highest-Priority Ready Task

This is the **central algorithm** of µC/OS-II. We derive it step-by-step so the KTU student can both understand the code and reproduce it in an exam.

### Step 1 — Mark a Task as Ready
When a task of priority `prio` is made ready (e.g., created, unblocked, or resumed from ISR), the kernel executes:

$$
\text{OSRdyTbl}[\text{prio} \gg 3] \;\vert=\; \text{OSMapTbl}[\text{prio} \,\&\, 0x07]
$$

**Logic:**
- `prio >> 3` gives the priority group (0-7). This is integer division by 8.
- `prio & 0x07` gives the position within the group (0-7). This is the modulo-8 remainder.
- `OSMapTbl[rem]` returns a single-bit mask with bit `rem` set (e.g., `OSMapTbl[2] = 0x04`).
- `|=` sets the corresponding bit in the group byte.
- After setting the group byte, also OR the group index into `OSRdyGrp`:

$$
\text{OSRdyGrp} \;\vert=\; \text{OSMapTbl}[\text{prio} \gg 3]
$$

### Step 2 — Find the Highest-Priority Ready Task
To find the highest-priority (lowest numeric value) task currently ready, the kernel executes:

$$
\text{high3} = \text{OSUnMapTbl}[\text{OSRdyGrp}]
$$

$$
\text{low3} = \text{OSUnMapTbl}[\text{OSRdyTbl}[\text{high3}]]
$$

$$
\text{prio} = (\text{high3} \ll 3) \;\vert\; \text{low3}
$$

**Logic:**
- `OSUnMapTbl[OSRdyGrp]` returns the index of the **lowest-numbered set bit** in `OSRdyGrp`. This is the highest-priority group (0 = highest).
- `OSUnMapTbl[OSRdyTbl[high3]]` returns the lowest-numbered set bit within that group — the highest-priority task within the group.
- Combine the two 3-bit indices to reconstruct the 6-bit priority.

### Step 3 — Worked Numerical Example

Suppose three tasks are ready with priorities **5, 17, and 42**. Find the highest-priority one.

**Step 3.1: Convert each priority to (group, bit):**

| Priority | Group = `prio >> 3` | Bit = `prio & 7` | Binary |
|---|---|---|---|
| 5 | 0 | 5 | `0000 0101` |
| 17 | 2 | 1 | `0001 0001` |
| 42 | 5 | 2 | `0010 1010` |

**Step 3.2: Build `OSRdyTbl[]`:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{OSRdyTbl[0]} &= 0\text{b}0010\,0000 \quad (\text{bit 5 set, from prio 5}) \\
\text{OSRdyTbl[2]} &= 0\text{b}0000\,0010 \quad (\text{bit 1 set, from prio 17}) \\
\text{OSRdyTbl[5]} &= 0\text{b}0000\,0100 \quad (\text{bit 2 set, from prio 42}) \\
\text{Other bytes} &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3.3: Build `OSRdyGrp`:** Set bit `i` for each non-zero `OSRdyTbl[i]`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{OSRdyGrp} &= 0\text{b}0010\,0100 \quad (\text{bits 2 and 5 set}) \\
&\; \text{(i.e., decimal 36)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3.4: Find highest-priority group:** `OSUnMapTbl[0b0010 0100]`. The lowest-numbered set bit is bit **2**. So:

$$\text{high3} = 2$$

**Step 3.5: Look inside that group:** `OSUnMapTbl[OSRdyTbl[2]] = OSUnMapTbl[0b0000 0010]`. The lowest-numbered set bit is bit **1**. So:

$$\text{low3} = 1$$

**Step 3.6: Combine:**

$$
\text{prio} = (2 \ll 3) \;\vert\; 1 = 16 \;\vert\; 1 = 17
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The algorithm correctly identifies priority **17** as the highest-priority ready task — even though we also had tasks at priorities 5 and 42, the **lowest numeric value** represents the **highest priority** in µC/OS-II's convention.

### Step 4 — Why This is O(1)

The `OSUnMapTbl` lookup table is built at **compile time** (all 256 entries precomputed). At runtime:

- 1 array access to read `OSRdyGrp`.
- 1 table lookup → `high3`.
- 1 array access to read `OSRdyTbl[high3]`.
- 1 table lookup → `low3`.
- 2 shift/OR operations to combine.

**Total: a constant number of operations, regardless of how many tasks exist.** This is the definition of O(1) and is the formal proof of µC/OS-II's deterministic scheduling.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: µC/OS-II vs. µC/OS-III vs. FreeRTOS

| Feature | µC/OS-II | µC/OS-III | FreeRTOS |
|---|---|---|---|
| Max priorities | 64 | Unlimited (configurable) | Unlimited (configurable) |
| Same-priority tasks | ❌ No (each task unique priority) | ✅ Yes (round-robin within priority) | ✅ Yes (time-slicing) |
| Round-Robin | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| POSIX support | ❌ No | ✅ Partial | ✅ Partial |
| Source availability | Full | Full | Full |
| Certification | DO-178B, IEC 61508 SIL 3 | DO-178C, IEC 61508 SIL 3, FDA | None (open source) |
| Commercial license | Required | Required | MIT (free) |
| Min ROM | ~2 KB | ~6 KB | ~4 KB |
| Scheduling | Preemptive priority | Preemptive + Round-Robin | Preemptive + optional RR |
| Year released | 1998 | 2009 | 2003 |

## 3.3 Numerical Exercise: Tick Counter Overflow

The µC/OS-II tick counter is a **32-bit unsigned integer**. If the tick rate is 1000 Hz (1 ms ticks), when does it overflow?

**Step 1:** Maximum value of 32-bit unsigned integer:
$$N_{\max} = 2^{32} - 1 = 4\,294\,967\,295$$

**Step 2:** Convert to seconds (at 1000 Hz):
$$T = \frac{N_{\max}}{1000} = 4\,294\,967.295 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 3:** Convert to days:
$$T_{\text{days}} = \frac{4\,294\,967.295}{86\,400} \approx 49.71 \text{ days}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Implication for KTU exam:** The tick counter overflows roughly every **49.7 days** at 1 kHz tick rate. For long-running embedded systems, this must be considered in delay calculations. µC/OS-II v2.86+ provides a 16-bit "OSTickCtr" overflow-tolerant API to handle this.

## 3.4 Code Snippet — Typical Task Structure (For Reference Only)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **C function template** every µC/OS-II task must follow. A task is essentially an infinite loop that calls kernel services to wait for events. A task may delete itself, but it must never simply `return` (unlike a normal C function).

```c
void MyTask(void *p_arg) {
    (void)p_arg;                       /* Avoid compiler warning   */
    
    while (1) {                         /* Infinite task loop        */
        /* ---- Critical work section ---- */
        /* Service the device, compute, etc. */
        
        OSTimeDlyHMSM(0, 0, 0, 500);   /* Delay 500 ms, yield CPU  */
        /* ---- End of one cycle ---- */
    }
}
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical rule from Labrosse's book:** *A task must NEVER simply return from its function — it must explicitly call `OSTaskDel(OS_PRIO_SELF)` if it wishes to terminate, or stay in an infinite loop.* Returning to an unknown stack state can cause a system crash.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 µC/OS-II Task State Transition Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram represents the **state machine** of a task in µC/OS-II. Every transition is labeled with the kernel API call that causes it.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    direction LR
    [*] --> Dormant
    Dormant --> Ready : OSTaskCreate() / OSTaskCreateExt()
    Ready --> Running : OSSched() picks it (highest priority)
    Running --> Ready : Preempted by higher-priority task
    Running --> Waiting : OSTimeDly() / OSSemPend() / OSMboxPend()
    Waiting --> Ready : Event signaled (timeout / IPC)
    Running --> Dormant : OSTaskDel(OS_PRIO_SELF) or another task deletes it
    Dormant --> [*]
    Running --> ISR : Hardware interrupt fires
    ISR --> Ready : OSIntExit() / higher-priority task made ready
```

## 4.2 µC/OS-II Kernel Architecture Block Diagram

The architecture is layered: **Application** → **Kernel Services** → **Porting Layer** → **Hardware**.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph APP["Application Layer"]
        T1[Task 1 - MainControl]
        T2[Task 2 - SensorRead]
        T3[Task 3 - UserInterface]
        T4[ISR - Tick Handler]
    end
    
    subgraph KERNEL["Kernel Services - OS_CORE.C / OS_TASK.C / OS_TIME.C / OS_SEM.C / OS_MBOX.C"]
        SCHED[Scheduler - OSSched]
        TMR[Time Management - OSTimeTick]
        IPC[IPC - Sem / Mbox / Queue / Mutex]
        TSKMGT[Task Management - OSTaskCreate / Del / Suspend / Resume]
    end
    
    subgraph PORT["Porting Layer - CPU Specific"]
        C[H - OS_CPU.H]
        CC[C - OS_CPU_C.C]
        ASM[ASM - OS_CPU_A.ASM]
    end
    
    subgraph HW["Hardware"]
        CPU[CPU / MCU]
        MEM[ROM / RAM]
        TICK[System Tick Timer]
    end
    
    T1 --> KERNEL
    T2 --> KERNEL
    T3 --> KERNEL
    T4 --> TMR
    SCHED --> PORT
    TMR --> PORT
    IPC --> PORT
    TSKMGT --> PORT
    PORT --> HW
```

## 4.3 O(1) Scheduler — Ready Table Lookup Flow

This shows how the bit-mapped ready table is queried to find the highest-priority ready task in constant time.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[OSRdyGrp - 8-bit variable] --> B[OSUnMapTbl lookup - returns high3]
    B --> C[high3 is the group with highest priority]
    C --> D[OSRdyTbl high3 - 8-bit variable]
    D --> E[OSUnMapTbl lookup - returns low3]
    E --> F[low3 is the position within the group]
    F --> G[prio = high3 shifted left 3 OR low3]
    G --> H[Highest-priority ready task identified]
    H --> I[OSRdyList prio - returns TCB pointer]
    I --> J[OS_TASK_SW - context switch to that task]
```

## 4.4 Context Switch Mechanism

Context switching is the act of saving the state of the currently running task and restoring the state of the next task. In µC/OS-II it is performed by `OSCtxSw()` (or `OSIntCtxSw()` from ISR).

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant SCHED as Scheduler (OSSched)
    participant CUR as Current Task TCB
    participant NEXT as Next Task TCB
    participant CPU as CPU Registers
    
    SCHED->>CUR: 1. Save CPU registers to TCB->OSTCBStkPtr
    SCHED->>CPU: 2. Save current SP into CUR->OSTCBStkPtr
    SCHED->>SCHED: 3. Update OSPrioCur = OSPrioHighRdy
    SCHED->>SCHED: 4. Update OSTCBCur = OSTCBHighRdy
    SCHED->>NEXT: 5. Get new SP from NEXT->OSTCBStkPtr
    SCHED->>CPU: 6. Restore CPU registers from new stack
    SCHED->>NEXT: 7. Resume execution of next task
    Note over SCHED,NEXT: Total time: < 10 microseconds on typical 32-bit MCU
```

## 4.5 Real-Life Application: Anti-Lock Braking System (ABS)

A classic hard real-time application where µC/OS-II is used to manage multiple concurrent sensing/control tasks.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SENSE["Sensor Tasks - Period 5 ms"]
        S1[Wheel Speed Sensor Task - Prio 5]
        S2[Brake Pressure Sensor Task - Prio 6]
        S3[Yaw Rate Sensor Task - Prio 7]
    end
    
    subgraph CTRL["Control Task - Period 10 ms"]
        C1[ABS Control Algorithm - Prio 3 - Highest]
    end
    
    subgraph ACT["Actuator Task - Event-driven"]
        A1[Brake Modulator Task - Prio 4]
    end
    
    subgraph DIAG["Diagnostics - Background"]
        D1[Self-Test Task - Prio 30 - Lowest]
    end
    
    S1 --> C1
    S2 --> C1
    S3 --> C1
    C1 --> A1
    C1 -.preempts.-> D1
    A1 -.preempts.-> D1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In this ABS example, µC/OS-II's **preemptive priority scheduler** ensures that the highest-priority control task always runs as soon as sensor data is ready, guaranteeing a deterministic response time of under **10 ms** — well within the hard real-time deadline for ABS systems.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]`
**List any six salient features of MicroC/OS-II.**

**Model Answer:**

The six salient features of MicroC/OS-II (µC/OS-II) are:

1. **Preemptive Real-Time Kernel** — A higher-priority ready task immediately preempts the running task, ensuring deterministic response.
2. **Portable** — The kernel is written in ANSI C and can be ported to almost any CPU; only `OS_CPU.H`, `OS_CPU_C.C`, and `OS_CPU_A.ASM` need to be rewritten for a new processor.
3. **ROMable** — The entire kernel can reside in ROM/Flash memory, making it suitable for embedded systems with no disk.
4. **Scalable** — Through conditional compilation in `OS_CFG.H`, only the services actually used are included, allowing ROM footprint as low as ~2 KB.
5. **Multitasking** — Supports up to **64 tasks** (8 reserved by the kernel for idle and statistic tasks; 56 available to the user).
6. **Deterministic O(1) Scheduling** — All kernel services, including the scheduler, execute in a constant amount of time that does not depend on the number of tasks created.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Note:** The word "deterministic" is worth 1 mark by itself — students often miss this. Mention **O(1)** explicitly to score full marks.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model QP]`
**Explain the five task states in MicroC/OS-II with the help of a state diagram.**

**Model Answer:**

A task in µC/OS-II can be in one of five states:

1. **Dormant State** — The task code resides in memory but the kernel is not aware of it (i.e., `OSTaskCreate()` has not been called, or it has been deleted). No TCB is allocated.

2. **Ready State** — The task has been created and is ready to run, but a higher-priority task is currently occupying the CPU. It is in the ready list waiting to be selected by the scheduler.

3. **Running State** — The task is currently executing on the CPU. Only one task can be in this state at a time in a single-core system.

4. **Waiting (Blocked) State** — The task has called a kernel service (e.g., `OSTimeDly()`, `OSSemPend()`) and is waiting for an event, signal, or timeout to occur. It is **not** in the ready list.

5. **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR) State** — The CPU is currently executing an interrupt handler. The task that was running before the interrupt is suspended; the kernel tracks this via `OSIntNesting` counter.

> [!NOTE]
> State transitions are triggered by kernel API calls: `OSTaskCreate()`, `OSTaskDel()`, `OSSched()`, `OSTimeDly()`, `OSSemPend()`, `OSIntEnter()`, `OSIntExit()`.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note on KTU ESE Pattern:** Part B questions in KTU 2024 scheme have **internal choice** — students answer EITHER (a) OR (b), each carrying **7 + 7 = 14 marks**. Two independent alternatives are provided below.

### OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks)

#### `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified ESE Pattern]`

**Q. (a)** Explain in detail the **task scheduling mechanism** of MicroC/OS-II. How does it achieve **O(1)** scheduling complexity? Discuss the role of **`OSRdyTbl[]`**, **`OSRdyGrp`**, and the **`OSUnMapTbl[]`** lookup table. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — The Two Data Structures (2 marks):**

µC/OS-II maintains two data structures to track the readiness of all 64 task priorities:

- **`OSRdyTbl[8]`** — An array of 8 bytes. Bit `b` of `OSRdyTbl[i]` is set if the task with priority `8i + b` is ready.
- **`OSRdyGrp`** — A single 8-bit variable. Bit `i` of `OSRdyGrp` is set if **any** task in group `i` is ready.

This decomposes the 64-bit priority space into 8 groups of 8 priorities each.

**Step 2 — Adding a Task to the Ready List (1 mark):**

When a task of priority `prio` becomes ready, two operations are performed:

```c
OSRdyTbl[prio >> 3] |= OSMapTbl[prio & 0x07];
OSRdyGrp         |= OSMapTbl[prio >> 3];
```

**Step 3 — Finding the Highest-Priority Ready Task in O(1) (3 marks):**

The kernel uses the **`OSUnMapTbl[256]`** lookup table, which is built at compile time and returns the position of the **lowest-numbered set bit** in a byte (0–7). The scheduler executes:

```c
high3 = OSUnMapTbl[OSRdyGrp];                      // 1 table lookup
low3  = OSUnMapTbl[OSRdyTbl[high3]];               // 1 table lookup
prio  = (high3 << 3) | low3;                       // shift + OR
```

- `OSUnMapTbl[OSRdyGrp]` → returns the highest-priority group (0–7).
- `OSUnMapTbl[OSRdyTbl[high3]]` → returns the highest-priority task within that group.
- The two are combined to yield the final 6-bit priority (0–63).

**Step 4 — Why O(1) (1 mark):**

The algorithm performs a **fixed number of operations** — one array access, two table lookups, and two bitwise operations — irrespective of how many tasks exist in the system. This is the formal definition of constant-time complexity, and it makes scheduling **deterministic**, which is a strict requirement of hard real-time systems.

---

**(b)** Describe the **Task Control Block (OS_TCB)** of MicroC/OS-II. List and explain any **six important fields** of the TCB. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **Task Control Block (TCB)** is a C structure (`OS_TCB`) that the kernel allocates for every task. It stores all information the kernel needs to manage the task. The TCBs of all created tasks are linked into a doubly linked list.

**Important Fields of OS_TCB:**

1. **`OSTCBStkPtr` (OS_STK \*)** — Pointer to the **current top of the task's stack**. This is the most critical field; the kernel saves and restores CPU registers through this pointer during context switches.

2. **`OSTCBPrio` (INT8U)** — The task's priority (0–63). 0 is the highest priority. Each task in µC/OS-II must have a unique priority.

3. **`OSTCBStat` (INT8U)** — State flags indicating the current condition of the task (e.g., ready, waiting for semaphore, waiting for time delay).

4. **`OSTCBDly` (INT16U)** — Stores the number of clock ticks remaining for a timeout when the task is in the waiting state. Decremented by `OSTimeTick()`.

5. **`OSTCBStkBottom` (OS_STK \*)** — Pointer to the **base (bottom)** of the task's stack, used for stack-overflow checking by the statistic task.

6. **`OSTCBExtPtr` (void \*)** — Pointer to a user-definable extension of the TCB. Allows the application to attach custom data (e.g., task name, statistics) without modifying the kernel.

7. **`OSTCBId` (INT16U)** — A unique identifier (0–65535) assigned at task creation, used for debugging.

8. **`OSTCBNext` / `OSTCBPrev`** — Doubly linked list pointers used by the kernel to traverse the TCB list.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic:** "**S**tack-**P**riority-**S**tate-**D**elay" → `StkPtr`, `Prio`, `Stat`, `Dly` — the four most commonly tested fields.

---

### OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks)

#### `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model QP]`

**Q. (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **architecture of MicroC/OS-II** including the application layer, kernel services, and porting layer. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

µC/OS-II is structured in **three well-defined layers** to maximize portability:

**1. Application Layer (Top)** — Contains the user's task functions and interrupt service routines. The application calls kernel API services (e.g., `OSTaskCreate`, `OSSemPend`) but does not directly access hardware registers.

**2. Kernel Services Layer (Middle)** — Processor-independent code providing:
   - Task management (`OS_TASK.C`) — `OSTaskCreate()`, `OSTaskDel()`, `OSTaskSuspend()`, `OSTaskResume()`.
   - Time management (`OS_TIME.C`) — `OSTimeDly()`, `OSTimeDlyHMSM()`, `OSTimeGet()`.
   - Inter-task communication (`OS_SEM.C`, `OS_MBOX.C`, `OS_Q.C`, `OS_MUTEX.C`, `OS_FLAG.C`).
   - Memory management (`OS_MEM.C`) — Fixed-size block memory partitions.
   - Scheduler (`OS_CORE.C`) — `OSSched()`, `OSIntExit()`.

**3. Porting Layer (Bottom)** — Processor-specific code consisting of exactly three files:
   - **`OS_CPU.H`** — Type definitions, stack growth direction, critical section macros.
   - **`OS_CPU_C.C`** — C functions like `OSTaskStkInit()`, `OSInitHookBegin()`, `OSTimeTickHook()`.
   - **`OS_CPU_A.ASM`** — Assembly functions: `OSStartHighRdy()`, `OSCtxSw()`, `OSIntCtxSw()`, `OSTickISR()`.

**Benefits of this architecture:**
- To port µC/OS-II to a new processor, **only 3 files** need to be modified.
- The bulk of the kernel (~5000+ lines of C) remains unchanged.
- The application's task code is also processor-independent.
- New CPU support can be added in days, not months.

---

**(b)** Discuss **five real-life applications** of MicroC/OS-II in embedded systems. For each, mention the specific real-time constraints and explain why a hard real-time kernel is essential. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Avionics — Flight Control Computers (FCCs)** (1.5 marks)
   - µC/OS-II is **FAA DO-178B certified**, making it legally usable in commercial aircraft systems.
   - Real-time constraint: Control surfaces must respond within **10–20 ms** of pilot input to maintain stability.
   - Why hard RTOS: Missing a deadline could cause loss of aircraft control.

2. **Medical Devices — Infusion Pumps and Ventilators** (1.5 marks)
   - µC/OS-II is **IEC 61508 SIL 3 certified** for medical device software.
   - Real-time constraint: Drug delivery rate must be accurate to within milliseconds.
   - Why hard RTOS: A late or missed alarm can mean loss of patient life.

3. **Automotive — Anti-lock Braking System (ABS)** (1.5 marks)
   - Real-time constraint: Wheel slip detection and brake modulation must occur every **5–10 ms**.
   - Multiple concurrent tasks: wheel-speed sensing, brake-pressure monitoring, control algorithm, actuator driver.
   - Why hard RTOS: Preemptive priority scheduling ensures the control algorithm always preempts lower-priority diagnostics.

4. **Industrial Automation — Programmable Logic Controllers (PLCs)** (1 mark)
   - Real-time constraint: Scan cycle must complete within **1–10 ms** to keep up with factory processes.
   - Why hard RTOS: Missing a cycle can cause product defects or safety incidents.

5. **Consumer Electronics — Set-Top Boxes and IoT Edge Devices** (1 mark)
   - Real-time constraint: Audio/video decoding jitter must remain below perceptible thresholds (~20 ms).
   - Why hard RTOS: Deterministic scheduling prevents glitches in real-time media streams.

6. **Networking — Routers and Switches** (Bonus, 0.5 marks)
   - Real-time constraint: Packet forwarding latency must be sub-millisecond.
   - Why hard RTOS: Bounded latency guarantees Quality of Service (QoS) for VoIP and video traffic.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. ✗ **Confusing µC/OS-II with µC/OS-III.** µC/OS-II does **NOT** support round-robin scheduling and does **NOT** allow multiple tasks at the same priority. If the question asks about round-robin, mention that this is a µC/OS-III feature.
> 2. ✗ **Wrong direction of priority.** A common mistake is to say "63 is the highest priority." In µC/OS-II, **0 is the highest** and 63 is the lowest.
> 3. ✗ **Forgetting the 8 reserved tasks.** Stating "µC/OS-II supports 64 user tasks" is wrong — only **56 tasks** are available to the user; 2 are reserved (idle, statistic) and 4 are reserved for future expansion.
> 4. ✗ **Ignoring the porting layer.** When asked about architecture, many students draw only the application and kernel, forgetting `OS_CPU.H`, `OS_CPU_C.C`, and `OS_CPU_A.ASM`.
> 5. ✗ **Writing "OSUnMapTbl returns the highest set bit."** It returns the **lowest-numbered set bit** (i.e., the highest-priority bit). The two are not the same — a subtle but frequently tested point.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: MicroC/OS-II**

- [x] **µC/OS-II** is a **preemptive, priority-based, real-time kernel** written in C by **Jean J. Labrosse** (1998).
- [x] **Predecessor:** µC/OS (1992) → **Successor:** µC/OS-III (2009, adds round-robin).
- [x] **Certifications:** FAA DO-178B, IEC 61508 SIL 3.
- [x] **Maximum tasks:** **64** (8 reserved internally, **56 user tasks**).
- [x] **Priority range:** **0 (highest) to 63 (lowest)**, where `OS_LOWEST_PRIO = 63` by default.
- [x] **Each task MUST have a unique priority** in µC/OS-II (no same-priority tasks).
- [x] **5 task states:** Dormant, Ready, Running, Waiting, ISR.
- [x] **2 key ready data structures:** `OSRdyTbl[8]` (8-byte bitmap) and `OSRdyGrp` (1-byte group summary).
- [x] **2 key lookup tables:** `OSMapTbl[8]` (set a bit) and `OSUnMapTbl[256]` (find lowest set bit).
- [x] **Scheduling complexity = O(1)** — the secret to deterministic, hard real-time behavior.
- [x] **Algorithm to find highest-priority ready task:**
  `high3 = OSUnMapTbl[OSRdyGrp];`
  `low3 = OSUnMapTbl[OSRdyTbl[high3]];`
  `prio = (high3 << 3) | low3;`
- [x] **Tick counter:** 32-bit, overflows in **~49.7 days** at 1 kHz tick rate.
- [x] **Critical rule:** A task must **never simply `return`** — it must call `OSTaskDel(OS_PRIO_SELF)` or loop forever.
- [x] **Porting requires only 3 files:** `OS_CPU.H`, `OS_CPU_C.C`, `OS_CPU_A.ASM`.
- [x] **Minimum ROM footprint:** ~**2 KB** (highly scalable via `OS_CFG.H`).
- [x] **Context switch time:** < **few microseconds** on a typical 32-bit MCU @ 100 MHz.
- [x] **OS_N_SYS_TASKS = 2** (idle + statistic); both use `OS_LOWEST_PRIO` and `OS_LOWEST_PRIO-1`.
- [x] **Time management API:** `OSTimeDly()`, `OSTimeDlyHMSM()`, `OSTimeGet()`, `OSTimeSet()`.
- [x] **Real-life application domains:** Avionics, medical, automotive ABS, industrial PLCs, consumer electronics, networking.
- [x] **Key difference from µC/OS-III:** µC/OS-II lacks round-robin; µC/OS-III adds it and supports unlimited priorities and same-priority tasks.
- [x] **Key difference from FreeRTOS:** µC/OS-II is commercial + certified; FreeRTOS is open-source + uncertified.

> [!TIP]
> **Final 30-Second Exam Strategy:**
> If you remember just **three numbers** — **64 (max tasks), 0 (highest priority), and O(1) (scheduling)** — and the **two-step OSUnMapTbl formula**, you can answer ~80% of the typical Part-A and Part-B questions on this topic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Adaptive Cruise Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT785 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Adaptive Cruise Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC): Foundational Definition & Intuition

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC)** is an advanced driver-assistance system (ADAS) that automatically adjusts a vehicle's longitudinal speed to maintain a safe following distance from a preceding vehicle. From an **RTOS perspective**, ACC is a canonical **hard real-time embedded control system** where multiple concurrent tasks — sensor acquisition, data fusion, control law computation, and actuator actuation — must execute under **deterministic deadlines** to guarantee passenger safety.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEEIT785 – RTOS)** syllabus, ACC is studied as a benchmark application to analyze:
- **Task modeling** (periodic, sporadic, aperiodic)
- **Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET)** estimation
- **Schedulability analysis** under Rate Monotonic (RMS) and Earliest Deadline First (EDF) algorithms
- **Priority inversion** and **deadlock** resolution
- **Inter-Task Communication (ITC)** via shared memory, message queues, and CAN buses

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: "The Orchestra Conductor"** 🎼
> Imagine an automobile as a symphony orchestra. The **conductor (RTOS scheduler)** ensures every musician (task) plays their note at precisely the right moment. The **violinist (radar task)** must play every **100 ms** (periodic). The **drummer (emergency braking task)** must strike *immediately* when a crash is imminent (sporadic with hard deadline). If the drummer is late even by **50 ms**, the music collapses — analogous to a collision. ACC is the **sheet music** that defines *who plays what, when, and how loud*, ensuring the orchestra (vehicle) performs flawlessly under all conditions.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics

The following **standardized metrics** define ACC system quality:

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value | RTOS Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Time-to-Collision** | $TTC$ | $\geq 2.5\,\text{s}$ | Sets control loop deadline |
| **Headway Time** | $t_h$ | $1.0 - 2.0\,\text{s}$ | Determines task period |
| **Sensor Sampling Period** | $T_s$ | $50 - 100\,\text{ms}$ | Periodic task activation |
| **Braking Reaction Deadline** | $D_b$ | $\leq 100\,\text{ms}$ | **Hard real-time constraint** |
| **Maximum Deceleration** | $a_{max}$ | **$-7\ \text{m/s}^2$** | Actuator saturation limit |
| **Controller Bandwidth** | $\omega_c$ | $0.5 - 2.0\,\text{Hz}$ | CPU utilization budget |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constant:** The standard gravitational acceleration used in vehicle dynamics is **$g = 9.81\,\text{m/s}^2$**. Braking distances and tire-road friction coefficients ($\mu \approx 0.7 - 0.9$ for dry asphalt) are derived from this constant.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 System Architecture Decomposition

An ACC system is decomposed into **five logical layers**, each representing a distinct RTOS task category:

### Layer 1 — **Sensor Acquisition Layer** (Periodic Tasks)
- **Radar/Lidar Task** ($T_1$): Period = **50 ms**, WCET = 5 ms → Range & relative velocity
- **Camera Task** ($T_2$): Period = **100 ms**, WCET = 8 ms → Lane & object classification
- **Wheel Speed Task** ($T_3$): Period = **20 ms**, WCET = 1 ms → Ego-vehicle odometry
- **Steering Angle Task** ($T_4$): Period = **20 ms**, WCET = 1 ms → Curvature estimation

### Layer 2 — **Sensor Fusion Layer** (Sporadic Task)
- **Track Fusion Task** ($T_5$): Activated on new radar return, WCET = 12 ms → Kalman filter update

### Layer 3 — **Control Law Computation Layer** (Periodic Hard Real-Time)
- **Longitudinal Controller** ($T_6$): Period = **50 ms**, WCET = 6 ms → Computes throttle/brake command
- **Safety Monitor** ($T_7$): Sporadic, hard deadline **100 ms**, WCET = 3 ms → Emergency brake intervention

### Layer 4 — **Actuator Layer** (I/O-Bound Tasks)
- **Throttle Actuator** ($T_8$): Period = 10 ms, WCET = 0.5 ms
- **Brake Actuator** ($T_9$): Period = 10 ms, WCET = 0.5 ms
- **HMI Display** ($T_{10}$): Period = 200 ms, WCET = 2 ms → **Soft real-time**

### Layer 5 — **Communication Layer**
- **CAN Bus Tx/Rx Tasks** ($T_{11}, T_{12}$): Handles messages at 500 kbps, WCET = 1 ms

## 2.2 The Core 'Why' and 'How' Behind ACC

**Why is RTOS necessary?** Because ACC involves concurrent, time-critical processes with mixed criticality. A general-purpose OS cannot guarantee deterministic response within the **100 ms braking deadline**.

**How does scheduling work?**
1. **Static priority assignment** via Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) — shorter period ⇒ higher priority.
2. **Dynamic priority** via EDF — closest deadline gets CPU.
3. **Priority ceiling protocol (PCP)** prevents unbounded priority inversion when tasks share the brake actuator resource.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cheat Sheet — All Critical Formulas**

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i}$ | **CPU Utilization** (Liu & Layland) | Dimensionless, $\leq 1$ |
| 2 | $U_{RMS}^{bound} = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ | **RMS Sufficient Schedulability** | $n$ = number of tasks |
| 3 | $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$ | **Response Time Analysis** | Iterative, $R_i \leq D_i$ |
| 4 | $TTC = \frac{\Delta d}{\Delta v}$ | **Time-to-Collision** | $\Delta v = v_{ego} - v_{target}$ |
| 5 | $t_h = \frac{d}{v_{ego}}$ | **Headway Time** | $d$ = inter-vehicle distance (m) |
| 6 | $a_{cmd} = K_p e + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \frac{de}{dt}$ | **PID Control Law** | $e$ = tracking error |
| 7 | $d_{safe} = v_{ego} \cdot t_h + \frac{v_{ego}^2}{2 \cdot a_{max}}$ | **Safe Following Distance** | Kinematic model |
| 8 | $f_{Nyq} = \frac{1}{2 T_s}$ | **Nyquist Sampling Frequency** | Must exceed signal bandwidth |
| 9 | $J = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \left( e(k)^2 + \lambda \cdot \Delta u(k)^2 \right)$ | **Cost Function** (MPC variant) | $\lambda$ = control weight |
| 10 | $P_{inv} = \frac{T_{max}}{T_{rev}}$ | **Priority Inversion Bound** (PCP) | Bounded by ceiling duration |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are not isolated — they appear in **automotive ECU design (Bosch, Continental), aerospace flight control (DO-178C), and ISO 26262 ASIL-D certification** workflows. Mastery enables careers in ADAS development at Mercedes-Benz, Tesla, Bosch, and Wipro Automotive.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

ACC principles extend to:
- **Autonomous Emergency Braking (AEB)** — adds pedestrian detection
- **Lane Keeping Assist (LKA)** — lateral control counterpart
- **Platooning** — truck convoys with $< 0.3\,\text{s}$ headway
- **Aerospace autopilot** — analogous longitudinal control laws
- **Industrial AGV (Automated Guided Vehicles)** — warehouse robotics
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Safe Following Distance Model

The **kinematic safe following distance** is derived from Newton's equations of motion. Consider the ego vehicle (E) approaching a slower target vehicle (T) ahead.

### Step 1 — Define relative velocity
The closing velocity between vehicles is:
$$\Delta v(t) = v_E(t) - v_T(t)$$

### Step 2 — Apply kinematic braking equation
For a vehicle decelerating at maximum rate $a_{max}$ (negative value), the stopping distance is:
$$d_{stop} = \frac{v_0^2}{2 \cdot \vert a_{max} \vert}$$

### Step 3 — Account for reaction delay
The driver (or controller) reacts after time $t_r$, during which the ego vehicle travels:
$$d_{react} = v_E \cdot t_r$$

### Step 4 — Compose total safe distance
The minimum safe inter-vehicle gap is the sum of the reaction distance and the braking distance differential:
$$d_{safe} = v_E \cdot t_r + \frac{v_E^2 - v_T^2}{2 \cdot a_{max}}$$

### Step 5 — Substitute headway time $t_h$
Rewriting in headway-time form for ACC controllers:
$$\boxed{d_{safe} = v_E \cdot t_h + \frac{v_E^2}{2 \cdot \vert a_{max} \vert}}$$

> **Explanation:** The first term captures the **steady-state gap** required for comfort; the second term represents the **emergency-stop margin** under maximum braking.

## 3.2 Derivation: Rate Monotonic Schedulability Bound (Liu & Layland, 1973)

For $n$ independent periodic tasks under preemptive RMS, a sufficient condition for schedulability is:

### Step 1 — State the utilization condition
$$U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i}$$

### Step 2 — Derive the worst-case critical instant
The critical instant for task $T_i$ occurs when **all higher-priority tasks release simultaneously** with $T_i$. The interference from each higher-priority task $T_j$ is:
$$I_j = \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$$

### Step 3 — Sum the interference
Total blocking + interference on $T_i$:
$$R_i^{(k+1)} = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i^{(k)}}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$$

### Step 4 — Iterative fixed-point solution
Iterate until convergence. Schedulability holds iff:
$$\forall i : R_i \leq D_i$$

### Step 5 — Simplification for utilization
Under $D_i = T_i$, Liu & Layland proved:
$$U \leq n \left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)$$

For large $n$, this bound approaches **$\ln(2) \approx 0.693$**.

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> **Given:** 3 tasks with parameters:

| Task | $C_i$ (ms) | $T_i$ (ms) | $D_i$ (ms) |
|------|------------|------------|------------|
| $T_1$ | 1          | 4          | 4          |
| $T_2$ | 2          | 6          | 6          |
| $T_3$ | 3          | 10         | 10         |

**Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
U &= \frac{1}{4} + \frac{2}{6} + \frac{3}{10} \\[4pt]
  &= 0.250 + 0.333 + 0.300 \\[4pt]
  &= 0.883
\end{aligned}$$

Compare against the RMS bound for $n=3$:
$$U_{bound} = 3\left(2^{1/3} - 1\right) = 3(1.2599 - 1) = 0.7797$$

Since $0.883 > 0.7797$, the **sufficient condition fails**. However, this is only sufficient — not necessary. We must perform **exact Response Time Analysis**.

**Response Time for $T_3$ (lowest priority):**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_3^{(0)} &= C_3 = 3 \\
R_3^{(1)} &= 3 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 \\
          &= 3 + 1(1) + 1(2) = 6 \\
R_3^{(2)} &= 3 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 \\
          &= 3 + 2(1) + 1(2) = 7 \\
R_3^{(3)} &= 3 + \left\lceil \frac{7}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{7}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 \\
          &= 3 + 2 + 2 = 7 \quad \text{(converged)}
\end{aligned}$$

Since $R_3 = 7 \leq D_3 = 10$, the system **is schedulable** under RMS.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: Choosing the Scheduling Policy

| Criterion | Rate Monotonic (RMS) | EDF | Priority Ceiling (PCP) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Priority Type** | Static | Dynamic | Static (resource-based) |
| **Optimality** | Optimal among static | Optimal (any policy) | Resolves inversion |
| **Implementation** | Simple | Requires deadline queue | Moderate |
| **Overhead** | Very low | Moderate | Low |
| **ACC Use Case** | Sensor sampling | Mixed criticality | Brake/throttle resource sharing |
| **Predictability** | High | Highest | High |

## 3.4 Derivation: PID Controller Tuning for ACC

The longitudinal error $e(t)$ is defined as:
$$e(t) = d_{actual}(t) - d_{safe}(v_{ego})$$

The PID control command for acceleration is:

$$a_{cmd}(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\,d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tuning Rule (Ziegler-Nichols for ACC):**
> - $K_p = 0.8 \cdot K_u$
> - $K_i = 2 \cdot K_p / T_u$
> - $K_d = K_p \cdot T_u / 8$
>
> Where $K_u$ is the ultimate gain and $T_u$ is the oscillation period observed when $K_i = K_d = 0$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
